sourceExcerpt:
When one thing is predicated of another, all that which is predicable
of the predicate will be predicable also of the subject. Thus, 'man' is
predicated of the individual man; but 'animal' is predicated of 'man';
it will, therefore, be predicable of the individual man also: for the
individual man is both 'man' and 'animal'.
intent: Capture Aristotle's worked syllogism for the transitivity of the said-of relation — the meta-rule that what is predicable of a predicate is predicable of the subject, instantiated as the canonical animal/man/individual-man case. Structurally Barbara (AAA-1).
extractorNote: The cleanest aristotelian extraction in the corpus so far, and the first to occupy the previously zero-coverage aristotelian formalism. Lossy in: (1) the meta-claim about transitivity-of-predication, encoded only in reviewer notes since the AST carries the instance not the meta-rule; (2) Aristotle's explicit gloss at line 93 ('for the individual man is both man and animal') which provides the term-sharing justification — not represented in the syllogism payload but is the natural reading of why the middle term is admissible across both premises.

assessments.reason: Could be rendered as ∀x. Man(x) → Animal(x); ∀x. IndividualMan(x) → Man(x); ⊢ ∀x. IndividualMan(x) → Animal(x). FOL captures the inference but discards the subject-predicate term-logic packaging (mood, figure, middle term) that the passage is itself illustrating.
assessments.distortionRisk: Flattening the syllogistic form would lose what Aristotle is here demonstrating, namely the validity-pattern of Barbara as a transitivity rule on the said-of relation.

assessments.reason: The instance reads as a one-step nd deduction over FOL formulas — two universal premises and one universal conclusion. Same loss as fol: the syllogistic packaging is the point of the passage.
assessments.distortionRisk: Nd would record the inferential step but in a propositional/FOL idiom that does not surface the (subject, predicate, middle, figure) structure Aristotle's prose is making explicit.

assessments.reason: Not dialogical. This is a treatise statement of a rule plus a worked example, with no exchange between speakers, no concessions, no objections.

reviewerNotes: The text uses 'predicated of' (κατηγορεῖσθαι κατά) where modern Barbara reads 'All A is B.' The mapping is standard: in Aristotelian term logic, 'P is predicated of S' functions as the universal-affirmative copula between terms, so 'animal is predicated of man' encodes as the A-proposition 'All man is animal.'

The minor premise — 'man is predicated of the individual man' — looks identifying in modern eyes ('Socrates is a man'), but in Aristotle's term logic singular subjects can stand as the subject term of A-propositions. 'All individual man is man' is the standard term-logic rendering; treating it as a universal preserves the figure-1 structure.

The instance traverses three ontological levels — genus (animal), species (man), individual (individual man). The same tri-level structure is the backbone of the said-of / present-in fourfold at Categories 59–83 and recurs in the primary/secondary substance discussion in Part 5.

Lines 89–90 state the principle in its general form ('all that which is predicable of the predicate will be predicable also of the subject'). This is a meta-claim — that the said-of relation is transitive, or that Barbara is valid — and is itself an extractable separate claim, FOL-shaped (∀R. Transitive(said-of)). The Categories 89–93 extraction encodes only the worked instance, which is what the aristotelian AST is shaped to carry.
Bekker reference 1b10–15 (Categories 1b10ff., Part 3 opening). Edghill (1928) translation; PG #2412.

The medieval Latin tradition calls this principle the dictum de omni ('what is said of all is said of any'). The same rule underwrites the validity of Barbara and Celarent in Aristotle's later syllogistic (Prior Analytics I.1, 24b28–30) — Categories Part 3 is the earliest statement.

When one thing is predicated of another, all that which is predicable
of the predicate will be predicable also of the subject. Thus, 'man' is

it will, therefore, be predicable of the individual man also: for the
individual man is both 'man' and 'animal'.

sourceExcerpt:
Substance, in the truest and primary and most definite sense of the
word, is that which is neither predicable of a subject nor present in a
subject; for instance, the individual man or horse.
intent: Capture the Categories' foundational definition of primary substance (Part 5, Bekker 2a11-14): the truest/primary sense of substance is that which is neither predicable of a subject nor present in a subject, i.e. PrimarySubstance(x) iff not-said-of-a-subject and not-present-in-a-subject.
extractorNote: Chose fol over aristotelian deliberately: although Categories is the corpus's paradigm aristotelian source and extraction 001 is aristotelian, THIS clause's reasoning shape is a one-place definitional biconditional with internal conjunction and negation, which the aristotelian categorical AST cannot carry without distortion (see secondary assessment). This is an instance of the CLAUDE.md rule 'choose formalism by local reasoning shape, not historical period / source work'. Lossy in: (1) the focal/pros-hen ambiguity of 'substance' (primary vs secondary senses) — flagged in reviewer notes, not in the AST; (2) the abstraction of said-of and present-in to one-place predicates rather than the binary relations of extraction 001 — a defensible reading of the passage's intransitive phrasing but a simplification.

assessments.reason: The clause superficially looks term-logical ('predicable of a subject'), but the load-bearing content is a biconditional definition of a one-place predicate (PrimarySubstance) by a conjunction of two negated relational conditions. Aristotelian categorical syntax (A/E/I/O propositions between two terms) cannot carry a defining biconditional, internal conjunction, or the double negation. The said-of/present-in relations are themselves binary and resist the unary subject-predicate copula.
assessments.distortionRisk: Forcing this into A/E/O propositions would lose the definitional (iff) force and the two-condition conjunction that jointly individuate primary substance; the result would not state what Aristotle defines here.

assessments.reason: No premises-to-conclusion inference is performed in this clause — it is a stipulative definition, not a derivation. The inferential use (e.g. deriving that the individual man is a primary substance from its not being said-of/present-in anything) comes later in Part 5; this clause is the defining biconditional itself.
assessments.distortionRisk: An nd rendering would have to invent a deduction the passage does not contain.

reviewerNotes: Bekker reference 2a11-14 (Categories Part 5 opening). Edghill (1928) translation; PG #2412.

'Predicable of a subject' renders the said-of (καθ' ὑποκειμένου λέγεται) relation and 'present in a subject' (ἐν ὑποκειμένῳ) the inherence relation — the two relations defined in Part 2 (lines 59-83). Primary substance is the bottom-left cell of that fourfold: neither said-of nor present-in anything. Encoding both as one-place predicates (PredicableOfASubject, PresentInASubject) follows the passage's own intransitive phrasing ('neither predicable of a subject nor present in a subject'), which abstracts over the second relatum; the fuller binary relations Said-Of(x, y) and Present-In(x, y) are exercised in extraction 001 and could anchor a relational refinement if a future extraction targets Part 2's fourfold directly.

The 'truest and primary and most definite sense' phrasing flags that 'substance' (οὐσία) is being used focally/pros-hen: there is also a secondary sense (species and genera, lines 134-140) which this clause explicitly contrasts with. The definition extracted is of PRIMARY substance specifically; SecondarySubstance is a distinct predicate not captured here.

'for instance, the individual man or horse' supplies paradigm instances (this is the same individual-man that figures in extraction 001's syllogism, now classified ontologically rather than used as a syllogistic term). The instances are illustrative, not part of the definition, and are not encoded in the AST.

The biconditional reading (iff rather than only-if) is licensed by the surrounding argument: Aristotle treats the two negative conditions as jointly sufficient as well as necessary — at lines 160-170 'Everything except primary substances is either predicable of a primary substance or present in a primary substance', i.e. anything satisfying both negations IS a primary substance. The definitional 'is that which' construction is standardly read as a definiens, hence iff.

sourceExcerpt: it would be possible for the soul to exist in separation from the body: if, on the other hand, there be no functions or affections so belonging to it, the soul would not admit of separate existence
intent: Capture Aristotle's conditional biconditional from De Anima I.1 (Bekker ~403a-b, line 99): the soul is separable from the body iff there exist functions/affections distinctively belonging to the soul (independent of body). The claim is foundational for the hylomorphic question of soul-body relation that the full De Anima develops. **First De Anima extraction** — ninth Aristotle work for the corpus (Categories, De Interpretatione, Prior Analytics, Posterior Analytics, Topics, Sophistical Refutations, Metaphysics, NE, De Anima). Aristotle now extracted across **nine works** with **thirteen extractions overall**.
extractorNote: **First De Anima extraction** — Aristotle now at **nine works** with **thirteen extractions overall** (Categories 001, De Interpretatione 001-004, Prior Analytics 001, Posterior Analytics 001, Topics 001, Sophistical Refutations 001, Metaphysics 001-002, NE 001-002, now De Anima 001). The Aristotle cluster is the largest single-author cluster in the corpus by a wide margin (13 of 38 extractions = 34%); Plato second at 9 (24%). The source file is heavily abridged (only 607 lines vs Wallace 1882's full ~250pp translation); the canonical Book II soul-definition (Bekker 412a 'first actuality of a natural body having life potentially') is not present in this source. The encoded I.1 conditional biconditional at Bekker ~403a-b is the most substantive philosophical claim accessible in this abridged source. Cross-extraction conceptual links flagged: Plato Phaedo 001 (Plato presupposes soul-separability where Aristotle conditionalizes it) and Aristotle NE 002 (shared function/ergon methodological pattern). Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the source-abridgment issue (De Anima Books II-III missing) is worth noting but not log.md-worthy per existing convention. The first-De-Anima / Aristotle-at-nine-works milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: Aristotle's claim is structured as a conditional biconditional argument: the soul's separability *follows from* the existence of body-independent distinctive functions. An nd extraction could carry the inferential character (premise: distinctive function ⇒ separability; premise: ¬distinctive function ⇒ ¬separability; conclusion: biconditional). The fol encoding compresses both directions into a single biconditional; nd would unpack the argument structure. Roughly co-equal; fol chosen for tightness.
The biconditional could be cast as a convertible-A pair, but the existential quantification over functions ('there be any of the functions which distinctively belong to it') would flatten.
Not epistemic — ontological / hylomorphic claim about soul-body relation.
Not dialogical.

reviewerNotes: Source: *De Anima* (On the Soul), Book I Chapter 1 (Bekker ~403a-b). Edwin Wallace 1882 translation (*Aristotle's Psychology*, Cambridge UP) per the work's manifest. Wikisource transcription. The source file is short (607 lines) and appears heavily abridged — it includes Wallace's editorial introduction and commentary alongside the translation; the full De Anima text is not present in this corpus version. Per the 2026-05-20 Phase B log entry, Wallace's 1882 *Aristotle's Psychology* is the PD English version available; Hicks 1907 was the original roadmap target but is not on Wikisource (redlink); Smith 1931 is rights-locked.

**The conditional biconditional** at line 99: 'If [there be any of the functions or affections of the soul which distinctively belong to it], it would be possible for the soul to exist in separation from the body: if, on the other hand, there be no functions or affections so belonging to it, the soul would not admit of separate existence.' The two-direction conditional structure constitutes a biconditional: soul-separable iff there exist distinctively-soul-belonging functions. Encoded as fol biconditional with the existential clause unpacked.

**OCR artifact in excerpt source**: line 99 contains '(^{ then there be any' at the start of the conditional — an OCR misread of 'If then there be any'. The chosen excerpt deliberately starts after this artifact, beginning with 'it would be possible for the soul to exist in separation from the body' (which is the *consequent* of the first conditional). The text is verbatim from line 99 starting at this point.

**Predicate naming**: `Soul(s)`, `Function(f)`, `DistinctivelyBelongsTo(f, s)`, `IndependentOfBody(f)`, `SeparableFromBody(s)`. The encoding captures Aristotle's claim that what makes the soul separable is the existence of functions/affections that *both* (a) distinctively belong to the soul AND (b) are independent of the body. The conjunction is essential — a function might distinctively belong to the soul yet still require body-cooperation (Aristotle's example: most affections, anger included, require body-cooperation; only thought is the candidate body-independent function).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Phaedo 001 (cyclical argument for soul immortality)**: Plato's argument *for* soul-separability presupposes the kind of inquiry Aristotle here formalizes (under what conditions is separability possible). Plato: souls exist in world below before/after embodiment (separability presupposed). Aristotle: separability *iff* body-independent function exists; Aristotle then in De Anima II-III explores whether thought (νοῦς) might be the body-independent function justifying separability of at least one soul-part. The Plato-Aristotle pair on soul-separability is a substantive conceptual structure: Plato presupposes; Aristotle conditionalizes.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics 002 (ergon/function argument)**: the NE argument uses 'function of man' to derive what counts as good for man. The De Anima soul-separability argument uses 'function distinctively belonging to soul' to derive what counts as separable from body. The two arguments share the *function* (ergon) move at their core, applied to different topics. The Aristotelian function-based reasoning is a recurring methodological pattern across his works.

**The wider De Anima context** (not in this source file): Books II-III develop the famous definition of soul as 'first actuality of a natural body having life potentially' (II.1, 412a), the doctrine of soul-faculties (nutritive, sensitive, locomotive, rational), and the famous treatment of nous in III.4-5 (the 'productive intellect' question). The corpus's source file doesn't contain Books II-III; future iterations needing the canonical soul-definition would need to source the full De Anima.

**Translation choices**: Wallace 1882 'soul' renders Aristotle's ψυχή (psyche) — standard. 'Distinctively belong' renders ἴδιον / proprium (which Topics 001 also encodes); 'separable' renders χωριστός (chōristos). The Edwin Wallace translation has Victorian style but is faithful.

**Reception**: the De Anima soul-separability question is the foundational text for the Aristotelian-tradition mind-body debate, the medieval scholastic dispute over the immortality of the rational soul, and modern philosophy-of-mind debates about mind-body dualism vs hylomorphism vs physicalism. The conditional structure Aristotle gives is the prototype for any contingent-separability argument.

sourceExcerpt: soul is the earlier perfect realization of a natural organic body
intent: Capture Aristotle's canonical hylomorphic definition of the soul from De Anima Book II, Chapter 1 (Bekker ~412a27-b6, line 249): soul is the first actuality (entelecheia) of a natural organic body. This is the most-cited definition in Aristotelian psychology — the foundation of the entire De Anima's analysis of soul-faculties, of the *Parva Naturalia*, and of the medieval Aristotelian-Christian synthesis (Aquinas, Avicenna, Averroes all build on this definition). **Second De Anima extraction**; **eleventh Aristotle work-extraction-count** (Aristotle now at 15 extractions across 10 works); **same-work different-formalism pair** with De Anima 001 (the soul-separability conditional from Book I).
extractorNote: **Second De Anima extraction** — Aristotle now at **15 extractions across 10 works** (Categories, De Interpretatione ×4, Prior Analytics, Posterior Analytics, Topics, Sophistical Refutations, Metaphysics ×2, NE ×2, De Anima ×2, Politics). Aristotle remains the corpus's most-extracted author at 15 of 47 = 32%. **Same-work different-span pair with De Anima 001**: 001 was the soul-separability conditional (Book I); 002 (this) is the soul-as-first-actuality definition (Book II). Third corpus instance of same-work-different-span pair pattern (Met 001/002, Hume 001/002, De Anima 001/002). **Tenth corpus conceptual opposition pair introduced**: Aristotle De Anima 002 (hylomorphic soul-as-form-of-body) vs Descartes Meditations 001 (dualist thinking-substance separable from body). Ten conceptual oppositions now in corpus. The De Anima 002 definition is foundational for the entire Aristotelian-scholastic psychology tradition (Aquinas, Avicenna, Averroes) and is the historical/philosophical alternative to Cartesian dualism. Twenty-fourth fol extraction; fol now at 24 of 47 = 51%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the same-work pair + tenth-opposition + Aristotle-at-fifteen-extractions milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The definition has A-form shape ('All souls are first-actualities of natural organic bodies') but the binary FirstActualityOf relation and the existential 'some natural organic body' nesting are not expressible in unary term logic. The aristotelian schema would need a composite predicate 'FirstActualityOfNaturalOrganicBody', flattening the relational hylomorphic structure.

Book II Ch. I builds to this definition through 7 paragraphs of preliminary argumentation (substance has three senses → bodies are real substances → natural bodies with life are composite substances → soul is the form of such bodies → soul is first actuality not second). An nd extraction could carry the argumentative climb to the definition. The fol extraction captures the definition itself; nd extraction would be the supporting argument.
Not dialogical. Aristotelian treatise.

reviewerNotes: Source: *De Anima* (On the Soul), Book II, Chapter I (Bekker ~412a27-b6). Same source as De Anima 001 — translator unidentified from the source.txt's manifest; the manifest should be checked for the De Anima translator. The Greek technical term ἐντελέχεια (entelecheia, 'actuality / fulfillment') is rendered by the translator as 'perfect realization'; 'first' (πρώτη) is rendered as 'earlier' or 'implicit' (vs 'later' / 'explicit' = second actuality = active exercise of the faculty).

**The first-actuality vs second-actuality distinction** is central to the definition. First actuality = the capacity / disposition / habit; second actuality = the active exercise of that capacity. The soul is the first actuality (the having-of-life-capacity) of a natural organic body, not the second actuality (the active using-of-life-capacities). This is why Aristotle clarifies (line 247): 'in reference to soul, it must evidently be understood in the former of these two senses: for the soul is present with us as much while we are asleep as while we are awake.'

**Predicate naming**: `Soul(s)` (definiendum); `NaturalOrganicBody(b)` (the kind of body souls are actualities of — natural bodies with organs, i.e. living-body-capable); `FirstActualityOf(s, b)` (binary relation: s is the first actuality of b — captures the hylomorphic form-of-matter relation in its specific first-actuality variant).

**The encoding as ∀-∃ biconditional**: outer ∀s quantifies over potential souls; inner ∃b captures that for every soul, there exists *some* natural organic body of which the soul is the first actuality. This preserves the relational structure of the definition — soul is *of* a body, not in isolation. The biconditional makes the definition strict: only souls are first-actualities-of-natural-organic-bodies; nothing else is.

**Cross-extraction same-work pair with De Anima 001 (same-work different-span)**: De Anima 001 (Book I Ch. I, line 99) extracted the soul-separability conditional — if the soul has functions independent of body, it can exist separately. De Anima 002 (this, Book II Ch. I, line 249) extracts the soul-as-first-actuality definition. The two extractions are structurally complementary: 001 leaves separability open as a conditional; 002 establishes that soul is the actuality *of* a body (suggesting non-separability in general, though Aristotle preserves the possibility for the *nous* / intellect). The same-work-different-span pair is the third corpus instance: Met 001/002 (PNC + LEM); Hume 001/002 (Copy Principle + argument); De Anima 001/002 (separability conditional + first-actuality definition).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Leibniz Monadology 001 (substance comparison)**: Leibniz's monad is a simple substance without parts; Aristotle's soul is a form-of-body, not a self-standing simple. The two substance ontologies are fundamentally different: Aristotle's soul is *of* a body (hylomorphic dependence); Leibniz's monad is *independent* of body (preestablished harmony with bodies). The Aristotle-Leibniz contrast is one of the canonical contrasts in modern substance metaphysics; both substances are 'individual substances' in some sense but with radically different ontologies.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Categories 001 (primary substance) and Metaphysics 001/002 (PNC/LEM)**: De Anima 002 applies the substance / form / matter framework from the Categories and Metaphysics to the specific case of living things. Soul is a *kind of form* — the form-of-a-body-with-life-capacity. The De Anima definition is parasitic on the broader Aristotelian substance metaphysics; the Categories/Metaphysics extractions are the philosophical foundation that De Anima 002 builds on.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes Meditations 001/002 (dualist contrast)**: Descartes's mind-body dualism (mind as thinking substance separable from body) is *directly opposed* to Aristotle's hylomorphism (soul is the form of body, not separable in general). **Tenth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle De Anima 002 (soul = first actuality of natural organic body; hylomorphic dependence) vs Descartes Meditations 001 (cogito = thinking substance; substance dualism). The Cartesian dualism marks the modern break from Aristotelian hylomorphism; the philosophical-historical break point is the 17th century.

**Reception**: De Anima Book II Ch. 1 is the foundational text of Aristotelian-scholastic psychology. Aquinas's *Quaestiones Disputatae de Anima* and *Summa Theologiae* I.75-89 (Treatise on Man) explicitly build on this definition. Avicenna's *Liber de Anima* and Averroes's *Long Commentary on De Anima* are the central Islamic-philosophical receptions. Modern revival via Aristotelian hylomorphism in contemporary philosophy of mind (Sokolowski, Anscombe, Hacker) takes this definition as the alternative to Cartesian dualism and materialist reductionism.

**Translation choices**: 'Perfect realization' for ἐντελέχεια is conventional; modern translations prefer 'actuality' (Hicks, Ross, Smith) or 'fulfillment' (Hamlyn). 'Earlier' for πρώτη ('first') in 'first actuality' is unusual; standard rendering is 'first actuality' (vs second actuality = exercise). 'Natural organic body' for σῶμα φυσικὸν ὀργανικόν is faithful; the 'organic' here means 'having organs' (instrumental-bodily-parts), not the modern biochemical sense.

attributions.sourceText: soul is the earlier perfect realization of a natural organic body

sourceExcerpt: Thus reason is, on the one hand, of such a character as to become all things, on the other hand of such a nature as to create all things
intent: Capture Aristotle's canonical active/passive intellect distinction from De Anima III.5 (line 515, Bekker ~430a10-25): the rational soul has both an intellect that *becomes all things* (passive / receptive / potential intellect) and an intellect that *creates all things* (active / creative / agent intellect). This is the **foundational text of medieval and modern philosophy of mind's nous-poietikos/nous-pathetikos distinction** — the most-controversial passage in the De Anima, generating distinct traditions (Alexander of Aphrodisias, Themistius, Avicenna, Averroes, Aquinas) on whether the active intellect is individual or universal/separate. **Third De Anima extraction**; **Aristotle now at 17 extractions across 10 works**; **second corpus three-extraction same-work coverage via different chapters** (joining Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003 three-essay coverage; Descartes Meditations 001-003 hybrid coverage — De Anima 001/002/003 is De Anima three-chapter coverage: Book I Ch. I + Book II Ch. I + Book III Ch. V).
extractorNote: **Third De Anima extraction** — Aristotle now at **17 extractions across 10 works**: Categories × 1, De Interpretatione × 4, Prior Analytics × 1, Posterior Analytics × 1, Topics × 1, Sophistical Refutations × 1, Metaphysics × 2, NE × 2, **De Anima × 3** (this), Politics × 2. Aristotle remains corpus's **most-extracted author** at 17 of 62 = 27%. **Second corpus three-extraction same-work coverage via different chapters/books**: De Anima 001 (Book I Ch. I, soul-separability) + De Anima 002 (Book II Ch. I, entelechy) + De Anima 003 (Book III Ch. V, active/passive intellect — this). Joins Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003 (three-essay coverage) and Descartes Meditations 001-003 (hybrid coverage) as three-extraction same-work coverages. **Four corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverages now**: Aristotle De Int 001-004 (four-extraction same-span via four formalisms); Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003 (three-essay); Descartes Meditations 001-003 (hybrid Med II × 2 + Med V × 1); Aristotle De Anima 001-003 (three-chapter, this) — fourth instance. **Twenty-ninth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle De Anima 003 (active intellect as eternal, immortal, separable) vs Nietzsche Genealogy 002 (interiority as historical-naturalistic byproduct). Twenty-nine conceptual oppositions now in corpus. **Plato Phaedrus 001 + Aristotle De Anima 001/002/003 vs Descartes Meditations 001/002/003 deepened to substantive 4-vs-3 conceptual opposition** (canonical ancient-vs-modern composite/structural soul vs simple thinking-substance opposition). **Aristotle psychology cluster now at three extractions**: De Anima 001 (separability) + 002 (entelechy) + 003 (active/passive intellect). The Aristotelian psychology is now substantively extracted across the full De Anima work, completing Aristotle's psychology in the corpus. Thirty-sixth fol extraction; fol now at 36 of 62 = 58%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the third-De-Anima + Aristotle-at-17-extractions + fourth-three-extraction-same-work-coverage + twenty-ninth-opposition + 4-vs-3-opposition-deepening + Aristotle-psychology-cluster-complete milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The claim has A-form shape ('All rational souls are passive-and-active-intellect-having'). Both predicates are unary. Term logic could carry the categorical conjunctive claim, but the *two-aspect conjunction* preserves the substantive philosophical distinction between the receptive (passive) and creative (active) aspects of reason; aristotelian flattening would obscure this.

Aristotle argues from a *general principle* (in nature there is both material substratum + causal-creative element — line 515 opening) to the specific application (reason has both aspects). A nd extraction could carry the abstract-general → soul-specific inferential move. The fol extraction captures the *conclusion* (rational soul has both aspects); nd would be the supporting analogical-argument.
Not dialogical. Aristotelian treatise.

reviewerNotes: Source: *De Anima* (On the Soul), Book III, Chapter V (Bekker ~430a10-25). Line 515 in source.txt. The famous active-passive intellect chapter is the shortest and most-controversial chapter of the De Anima.

**Predicate naming**: `RationalSoul(s)` for the Aristotelian *psyche logistikē* (rational soul, the highest soul-part exhibiting intellectual capacity); `HasIntellectThatBecomesAllThings(s)` for the *nous pathētikos* (passive/receptive intellect — capable of becoming all intelligibles via reception); `HasIntellectThatCreatesAllThings(s)` for the *nous poiētikos* (active/creative/agent intellect — productive of all intelligibles via illumination).

**The two-aspect conjunction**: captures Aristotle's *both* claim. The rational soul has *both* the receptive capacity (becomes-all-intelligibles via being impressed by them, like wax receiving seal) *and* the creative capacity (creates-all-intelligibles via illuminating the potential intelligibles in sense-images, like light making colors visible). The active intellect's analogy with light (line 515) is the canonical Aristotelian image: 'light also in a way creates actual out of potential colour.'

**The active intellect's distinctive features** (line 517): 'separate from and uncompounded with material conditions'; 'in its essential character fully and actually realized'; 'not subject to impressions from without'; 'this creative reason does not at one time think, at another time not think: [it thinks eternally:] and when separated from the body it remains nothing but what it essentially is: and thus it is alone immortal and eternal.' These features (separability, eternity, immortality) are what generate the **Averroist controversy** about whether the active intellect is *individual* (one per person) or *universal* (one separate intellect for all humans). The passive intellect, by contrast, is 'perishable.'

**The passive/receptive intellect** (also line 517): 'the receptive passive intellect (which is affected) is perishable, and can really think nothing without the support of the creative intellect.' The passive intellect is *dependent* on the active intellect for its operation; the active intellect is *independent* of the passive (and indeed of all bodily conditions). This asymmetry grounds the medieval Averroist reading that the active intellect is *one* (universal) while the passive intellects are many (individual).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to De Anima 001/002 (within-work three-extraction same-work coverage)**: De Anima 001 (Book I Ch. I, line 99 — soul-separability conditional) + De Anima 002 (Book II Ch. I, line 249 — entelechy hylomorphic definition) + De Anima 003 (Book III Ch. V, line 515 — active/passive intellect — this). Three De Anima extractions covering three different books with three different chapters. **Second corpus three-extraction same-work coverage via different chapters/books** (Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003 was three-essay coverage; Descartes Meditations 001-003 was hybrid coverage; De Anima 001-003 is three-book coverage). The De Anima coverage now substantively spans the full work: Book I (preliminary), Book II (soul-as-form), Book III (intellect).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Phaedrus 001 (substantive alliance reinforced via Book III)**: Plato Phaedrus's charioteer (rational element governing horses) and Aristotle De Anima Book III's intellect (separate, creative, eternal) are both *rational-element-as-distinctive* doctrines. Aristotle De Anima Book III in particular separates intellect (nous) from other soul-faculties as the *most distinctively rational* element — paralleling Plato's charioteer-as-distinctive-rational. The Plato-Aristotle alliance on rational-element-distinctiveness is now extended from De Anima 001/002 (general naturalistic soul-theory) to De Anima 003 (specifically rational-element separability). The **twelfth corpus alliance** (Plato Phaedrus + Aristotle De Anima) is now reinforced with three Aristotle De Anima extractions on the alliance side.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes Meditations 001/002/003 (substantive opposition deepened)**: Descartes's cogito and ontological argument presuppose a *simple* thinking substance; Aristotle's intellect at De Anima Book III is *bipartite* (active + passive) and the active intellect is *separable* from the rest of the soul. The Plato Phaedrus 001 vs Descartes Meditations 001/002/003 opposition (iter 61 twenty-eighth opposition) is now reinforced via Aristotle De Anima Book III's active/passive distinction — Aristotle joins Plato in opposing the Cartesian simple-thinking-substance reading. **Plato Phaedrus 001 + Aristotle De Anima 001/002/003 vs Descartes Meditations 001/002/003 is now a substantive 4-vs-3 conceptual opposition**.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hegel Phenomenology 002 (substantive partial-alliance — both bipartite-soul-structure)**: Hegel's mutual recognition account treats self-consciousness as requiring *otherness* (intersubjective bipartite structure); Aristotle's active/passive intellect treats reason as requiring *both* receptive and creative aspects (intra-soul bipartite structure). Substantive structural parallel: both philosophers treat the soul/mind as having *internal differentiation* (Hegel: self-vs-other; Aristotle: active-vs-passive intellect). Different domains, similar structural move.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Nietzsche Genealogy 002 (substantive opposition — eternal-immortal-creative-intellect vs historical-byproduct-internalization)**: Aristotle's active intellect is *eternal* and *immortal* (separable from body); Nietzsche's interiority is *historical-byproduct* of internalization. **Twenty-ninth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle De Anima 003 (active intellect as eternal, immortal, separable) vs Nietzsche Genealogy 002 (interiority as historical-naturalistic byproduct). The Aristotle-vs-Nietzsche opposition on soul-origin-and-nature is now substantively at *two oppositions* (twenty-seventh opposition was Plato Phaedrus 001 vs Nietzsche Genealogy 002; twenty-ninth is Aristotle De Anima 003 vs Nietzsche Genealogy 002).

**The Averroist controversy** (medieval reception): Averroes (Ibn Rushd, 1126-1198) interpreted De Anima III.5 as positing *one* active intellect shared by all humans (monopsychism), making individual immortality impossible. Aquinas in *Summa Theologiae* I.79.4-5 explicitly rejects Averroism and argues for individual active intellects. The Plato-Aristotle-Aquinas-Cartesian tradition on individual immaterial souls vs Averroist-monopsychist tradition is one of the canonical medieval-philosophical debates.

**Reception**: De Anima III.5 active/passive intellect is the most-controversial passage in Aristotle's psychology. Alexander of Aphrodisias's commentary (c. 200 CE) is the earliest reception. Themistius's paraphrase (4th c.) and Averroes's *Long Commentary on De Anima* (12th c.) generate the medieval Averroist tradition. Aquinas's response and the Renaissance Pomponazzi controversy continue the debate. Modern philosophy of mind (Burnyeat, Wedin, Kahn) continues to debate the meaning of the active intellect.

**Translation choices**: Source.txt uses 'reason' for *nous* (intellect/mind); 'creative' for *poiētikos* (active); 'passive' for *pathētikos* (receptive). Modern translations (Smith, Hett, Hamlyn) use 'agent intellect' / 'productive intellect' for active; 'patient intellect' / 'receptive intellect' for passive. The encoded predicate names use the source-translation idiom ('Becomes' / 'Creates') for fidelity to the verbatim excerpt.

attributions.sourceText: Thus reason is, on the one hand, of such a character as to become all things, on the other hand of such a nature as to create all things

sourceExcerpt: By the term 'universal' I mean that which is of such a nature as to be predicated of many subjects, by 'individual' that which is not thus predicated.
intent: Capture Aristotle's definition of 'universal' vs 'individual' at De Interpretatione 7 (Bekker 17a39-40) — a foundational definitional move that distinguishes terms predicable of multiple subjects (universals) from terms not so predicable (individuals). The third Aristotle extraction (after Categories 001 = Barbara worked instance, Prior Analytics 001 = Celarent schematic); first Aristotle extraction outside the Organon's syllogistic spine; first definitional (not inferential) Aristotle extraction.
extractorNote: **Third Aristotle extraction** — first outside the Organon's syllogistic spine (Categories and Prior Analytics are both about syllogism per se; De Interpretatione is about the proposition that syllogisms operate on). First *definitional* (rather than inferential) Aristotle extraction; Categories 001 = Barbara worked syllogism, Prior Analytics 001 = Celarent schematic syllogism, this = universal-vs-individual definition. Encoded as `fol` rather than `aristotelian` because the definitional biconditional is not a categorical proposition or syllogism — the schema's CategoricalProposition carries A/E/I/O quantifications, not definitions, and recasting as a pair of A-propositions would discard the *definitional* force. The dispositional/modal connotation of Aristotle's πεφυκός ('of such a nature as to be predicated') is flattened to a non-modal predicate `PredicableOfManySubjects`; a future kripke or temporal extraction of the same passage could exercise the modal mirror. The famous future-contingents argument in De Interpretatione 9 (sea-battle) is held as a strong future-target candidate for either the temporal or kripke mirrors. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria — no schema-gap recurrence, no novel anti-pattern, no formalism resistance.

assessments.reason: The text gives a definition (a biconditional between two predicates), not a categorical proposition or syllogism. The aristotelian schema's CategoricalProposition carries A/E/I/O quantifications but not biconditional definitions; encoding the biconditional as two A-propositions ('all universal is predicable-of-many' + 'all predicable-of-many is universal') would lose the definitional force.
assessments.distortionRisk: Casting a definition as a pair of A-propositions discards the *definitional* character of the move — Aristotle is *stipulating* what 'universal' means, not asserting two empirical universal claims.

assessments.reason: Not an inference. Definitional move, no premises-to-conclusion structure.
Not dialogical. Treatise definition.

reviewerNotes: Bekker reference 17a39-40 (De Interpretatione Chapter 7, opening). The Bekker marginal '40' appears inline in source.txt before 'an individual' — preserved verbatim in the broader line but not within this extraction's excerpt (which stops before the marker).
Translator: E. M. (Ella Mary) Edghill (1928 Oxford Ross) — same translator and same Oxford volume as the corpus's Categories. Per the work's manifest, Wikisource transcription.

**The dual definition** stated in the source: 'by \'individual\' that which is not thus predicated' (encoded outside this extraction's primary formula). The full partition is: ∀x. Universal(x) ↔ PredicableOfManySubjects(x); ∀x. Individual(x) ↔ ¬PredicableOfManySubjects(x). The two biconditionals together imply universals and individuals are exhaustive and mutually exclusive (modulo the implicit 'every term is either universal or individual' assumption). The primary formula encodes only the universal side; the individual side is the natural dual. A future companion extraction could land the individual side as a parallel `fol` formula; not done here to keep the primary formula tight.

**The worked examples** at the end of the line ('Thus "man" is a universal, "Callias" an individual') are not encoded in the AST — they are illustrative instances, not part of the definitional content. Standard convention from Categories 001 (the animal/man/individual-man worked instance was encoded as the syllogism's term strings) and Prior Analytics 001 (no worked instance in the schematic form).

**Predicate naming**: `Universal(x)` and `PredicableOfManySubjects(x)`. The Greek is καθόλου (kathólou, universal) and the gloss 'predicated of many subjects' encodes the τὸ ἐπὶ πλειόνων κατηγορεῖσθαι πεφυκός phrase. The Aristotelian definition is *modal* in spirit — 'that which is OF SUCH A NATURE AS TO BE predicated of many subjects' (πεφυκός), suggesting a dispositional / capacity-based reading rather than an actualist one. The encoded predicate `PredicableOfManySubjects` treats this as a flat predicate; a future modal-logic extraction could render it as ◇ predicated-of-many in a kripke or temporal frame. Recording the modal-flattening as a translation/encoding choice.

**Cross-treatise connection**: this is the same said-of relation that Categories Part 3 (lines 89-93 of categories/source.txt, extracted as Categories 001 = Barbara syllogism) and Prior Analytics I.4 (extracted as Prior Analytics 001 = Celarent schematic) both depend on. De Interpretatione 7 here gives the *definition* of what makes a term universal (= subjectable to said-of in the multi-subject case); Categories 001 and Prior Analytics 001 *use* said-of in syllogistic inference. Cross-extraction conceptual link (11th occurrence of cross-span citation in spirit; not re-flagging).

**Source-text artifacts** in the broader line (61) preserved verbatim per the Phase B Aristotle convention: the inline Bekker marker '40' between 'Callias' and 'an individual' is the standard Wikisource transcription convention. The chosen excerpt deliberately stops before this marker to avoid the artifact-in-excerpt issue from the Prior Analytics 001 ('IfA' / '40' / '26 a' case), but the broader span at line 61 contains it.

Chapter 7 vs Chapter 9: De Interpretatione 9 (lines 87, 108-110 of source.txt) contains the famous **future contingents / sea-battle argument** — the canonical text for branching-time / future-contingent modal logic. Chapter 7's universal/individual definition is foundational *for* the syllogistic framework but is far less philosophically distinctive than chapter 9. Chapter 7 was chosen as the first De Interpretatione extraction because it is a clean definitional move (single biconditional, fol-shaped) with no modal content the current mirrors cannot carry; chapter 9 is a natural future target that may stress the temporal/kripke mirrors.

attributions.sourceText: By the term 'universal' I mean that which is of such a nature as to be predicated of many subjects, by 'individual' that which is not thus predicated.

sourceExcerpt: A sea-fight must either take place to-morrow or not, but it is not necessary that it should take place to-morrow, neither is it necessary that it should not take place, yet it is necessary that it either should or should not take place to-morrow.
intent: Capture Aristotle's sea-battle argument from De Interpretatione 9 (Bekker 19a30-35) — the foundational text for branching-time / future-contingent modal logic. The claim: it is necessary that a sea-fight either take place tomorrow or not (excluded middle, necessarily), but it is not necessary that it take place, nor necessary that it not take place. The asymmetry between the necessity of the disjunction and the necessity of either disjunct is what motivates branching-future modality. **First `kripke` extraction in the corpus** — closes the third schema-zero gap (after `aristotelian` at iteration 1 and `indian` at iteration 9).
extractorNote: **First `kripke` extraction in the corpus** — closes the third schema-zero gap (after `aristotelian` at iteration 1 = Categories 001, and `indian` at iteration 9 = Nyāya Sūtras 001). The schema-zero-gap-closure pattern (analogous to iteration 9's pivot to Nyāya over Marcus Aurelius) once again outweighs the breadth-across-modern-Western-authors signal in the formalism-diversity bias from AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md. The corpus's three schema-zero-closure extractions all share a pattern: pick a canonical foundational passage that the schema was designed to carry (Categories 89-93 for term logic, Nyāya Sūtra I.1.5 for anumāna, De Int 9 for modal future-contingents). The sea-battle was the obvious target — flagged in iterations 9-11 SESSION_LOG entries as a strong candidate. The included Kripke model is a deliberate choice: the sea-battle argument *introduces* the branching-future frame, exactly the case the schema docstring identifies as warranting a model. Span is single-line (108); the wider Chapter 9 argument is held as a future companion extraction. The CTL secondary at 0.65 is the closest second-place; CTL would be the stronger primary if the argument required multi-step temporal nesting. The intuitionistic secondary at 0.55 represents a different philosophical reading (rejection of LEM rather than modal qualification); flagged but not chosen because the text's *form* is modal-affirmative, not constructively-restrictive.

assessments.reason: Branching-time CTL is structurally the closest fit: the sea-battle scenario has a present moment with multiple future branches. CTL could encode the claim as AX(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ EX(SF) ∧ EX(¬SF) — necessarily SF-or-not on all next-paths, but SF holds on some next-path and ¬SF on some other. The branching-time temporal modality is exactly what Aristotle's qualifier 'must... either... or... to-morrow' is invoking. Kripke chosen over CTL primarily because the schema's optional Kripke model already encodes a branching frame as a one-step accessibility relation, and the propositional modal □ / ◇ are sufficient for this single-step future. CTL would be the stronger primary choice if the argument required multi-step temporal nesting (e.g. 'at every future time, it is possible that...'). Recording the close-second-place fit.
assessments.distortionRisk: Kripke's □ treats accessibility uniformly; CTL distinguishes AX (next on all paths) from AG (always on all paths) etc., which would matter if the argument were about longer time-spans. For the single-step tomorrow case, the two are roughly equivalent.

assessments.reason: Linear temporal logic (LTL with X / F / G / U) assumes *linear* time — a single deterministic future. The sea-battle argument's force depends on branching futures (the affirmation and denial 'have the same character' precisely because reality is not yet determined). LTL cannot represent the branching; it would model 'X SF ∨ X ¬SF' but the disjunction would already be determinate-or-not at evaluation time, missing Aristotle's point that *neither* disjunct is yet determined.

An intuitionistic reading takes the sea-battle argument as a rejection of bivalence for future contingents: ¬(SF ∨ ¬SF) is not derivable, but neither is SF derivable nor ¬SF derivable. This is the intuitionistic stance — excluded middle fails because we lack a proof procedure for the future. The intuitionistic mirror could carry this reading via the absence of LEM. Not chosen as primary because Aristotle's text *affirms* the disjunction ('it is necessary that it either should or should not take place') while denying the necessity of each disjunct — a modal-classical reading, not an intuitionistic-LEM-rejection reading.
assessments.distortionRisk: The intuitionistic reading reframes Aristotle's modal qualifier as a constructive-proof concern, which is anachronistic — Aristotle has modal vocabulary but no proof-theoretic framework.

assessments.reason: First-order logic without modal operators cannot represent the asymmetry between □(SF ∨ ¬SF) and ¬□SF ∧ ¬□¬SF — that asymmetry is the philosophical content of the sea-battle. Encoding without modality would collapse the necessity-claim into mere truth-claim and lose the argument.
Not dialogical.

reviewerNotes: Bekker reference 19a30-35 (De Interpretatione 9, the closing example). The inline Bekker marker '30' at the start of line 108 in source.txt is preserved verbatim in the broader line but the chosen excerpt starts at 'A sea-fight' to avoid the artifact-in-excerpt issue from Prior Analytics 001.
Translator: E. M. Edghill (1928 Oxford Ross), per the work's manifest — same translator as De Interpretatione 001 and Categories 001.

**Cross-paragraph dependency** on the full Chapter 9 argument (source.txt lines 87-110, ~24 lines). Chapter 9 sets up the problem by considering the consequences of bivalence applied to future contingents (lines 89-103: if future propositions are determinately true or false, then everything is necessary, deliberation is pointless, fortuitous events impossible), then qualifies (lines 104-106: but deliberation IS causative; some things have potentiality in either direction), then arrives at the sea-battle illustration (line 108) and the closing rule (line 110: bivalence fails for future contingents — 'we cannot say determinately that this or that is false'). The single-line extraction here captures the illustrative example; the full argument is held as a candidate future companion extraction (likely `nd` with the sea-battle as one premise / conclusion among several).

**Modal-operator interpretation**: □ as 'necessarily' (in Aristotle's sense, roughly 'true in all real alternatives' / 'fixed by the nature of things'). The kripke schema's accessibility relation R interprets □ as 'true in all R-accessible worlds'; for the sea-battle, R is the temporal-branching relation from present (w0) to possible futures (w1, w2). This is a *one-step* branching — Aristotle's claim is about 'to-morrow' specifically, not about all future times. A more general future-contingents argument would require multi-step accessibility (CTL or temporal logic).

**The included Kripke model** illustrates the branching future. w0 is the present (today); w1 is the future-where-sea-fight-occurs; w2 is the future-where-sea-fight-doesn't-occur. Accessibility w0 → w1 and w0 → w2 (both futures are possible from now). At w0: ◇SeaFight (true via w1), ◇¬SeaFight (true via w2), hence ¬□SeaFight and ¬□¬SeaFight. □(SeaFight ∨ ¬SeaFight) holds at w0 because the disjunction is tautologically true at every accessible world. This is a worked counterexample / model that Aristotle's argument *introduces*, satisfying the schema docstring's criterion for when to include a model ('passages that argue *about* a specific frame').

**The argument's structure** is a conjunction of three modal claims, not an inference. Aristotle is *asserting* a complex modal proposition with three conjuncts, not deriving one from premises. This is why kripke (or ctl) is the natural formalism over nd — the load-bearing structure is the modal claim itself, not premises-to-conclusion reasoning.

**'A sea-fight' as a singular event** — encoded as a single propositional atom `SeaFight` rather than as a quantified claim about events. The Aristotelian Greek ναυμαχία ('sea-fight') refers to a specific definite event (tomorrow's possible naval engagement). A first-order encoding would type the variable and quantify, but the kripke schema is propositional. Schema-appropriate.

**The text's qualifier 'to-morrow'** (αὔριον) ties the modal claim to a specific future time. The kripke model's edges are temporal (today → tomorrow); a CTL extraction would use AX / EX explicitly. The single-step accessibility is what the kripke schema can carry; for richer temporal structure (always, eventually, until), CTL or LTL would be needed.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link** to De Interpretatione 001 (universal vs individual). Chapter 9's argument turns on the predicate-of-individual-subject case (the sea-fight is an individual event, not a universal kind); chapter 9 explicitly opens (line 89) with 'When the subject, however, is individual, and that which is predicated of it relates to the future, the case is altered.' De Int 001's definition of 'individual' (= ¬PredicableOfManySubjects) is exactly the case in which the sea-battle argument applies. Cross-extraction within the same work — natural conceptual companion. (13th cross-span citation in spirit; not re-flagging.)

**Philosophical reception**: the sea-battle is one of the most-debated single passages in the history of logic. Diodorus Cronus, the Megarians, Boethius (who preserves the Greek tradition's commentary in his *On Aristotle's De Interpretatione*), the medieval commentators (Aquinas, Scotus, Buridan), and 20th-century modal logicians (Łukasiewicz introducing three-valued logic specifically for future contingents, Prior's tense logic, Kripke's branching-time semantics, Belnap's STIT theory) have all responded to this passage. The extraction encodes the bare claim; the rich reception is reviewer-notes context.

attributions.sourceText: A sea-fight must either take place to-morrow or not, but it is not necessary that it should take place to-morrow, neither is it necessary that it should not take place, yet it is necessary that it either should or should not take place to-morrow.

sourceExcerpt: A sea-fight must either take place to-morrow or not, but it is not necessary that it should take place to-morrow, neither is it necessary that it should not take place, yet it is necessary that it either should or should not take place to-morrow.
intent: Capture Aristotle's sea-battle argument from De Interpretatione 9 (Bekker 19a30-35, line 108) as a CTL branching-time formula — same source span as De Int 002 (kripke, iter 13). The CTL encoding AX(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ EX(SF) ∧ EX(¬SF) makes the branching-future / path-quantification structure explicit, where kripke's □/◇ flatten the branching into a single accessibility relation. **First `ctl` extraction in the corpus**; closes the **fifth schema-zero gap** in the established pattern. **First same-span pair across modal formalisms** in the corpus (De Int 002 kripke + De Int 003 ctl on line 108).
extractorNote: **Fifth schema-zero-gap closure** in the established pattern (aristotelian iter 1 → indian iter 9 → kripke iter 13 → boolean iter 23 → ctl iter 24). Remaining zero-extraction mirrors: eg, epistemic, frege, intuitionistic, medieval, resolution, temporal — **seven of twelve still at zero**. **First same-span pair across modal formalisms** in the corpus (De Int 002 kripke + De Int 003 ctl on line 108) — extends the same-span pair pattern from the slave-boy 002/003 (fol + dialogical) original. The CTL encoding (AX(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ EX(SF) ∧ EX(¬SF)) is informationally equivalent to the kripke encoding for single-step branching but makes the path-quantification structure explicit. assessments.reason: **Same passage already extracted as kripke** at De Int 002 (iter 13). The kripke encoding □(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ ¬□SF ∧ ¬□¬SF is informationally equivalent to this CTL encoding AX(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ EX(SF) ∧ EX(¬SF), since AX = □ for one-step accessibility (and the dual ¬AX ψ ≡ EX ¬ψ holds in CTL). The two extractions form the corpus's **first same-span pair across modal formalisms** (kripke + ctl on the same source span 108). Both are equally faithful structurally; the choice between them tracks whether the corpus wants branching-time-explicit (ctl) or modal-□-flattened (kripke). The same-span pair captures both views.

The intuitionistic mirror could encode the failure-of-LEM (¬(SF ∨ ¬SF) is not derivable, but neither is SF nor ¬SF). LTL is linear and cannot represent branching futures — same loss as in De Int 002 (iter 13).
First-order logic without modal/temporal operators cannot represent the necessity-of-disjunction-vs-non-necessity-of-disjunct asymmetry.
Not dialogical.

reviewerNotes: Source: same as De Int 002 (Bekker 19a30-35; Ross/Edghill translation; Wikisource transcription). Span identical to De Int 002 (line 108).

**Same-span pair with De Int 002 (kripke)**. The two extractions encode the same source-text passage in two different modal formalisms. CTL's AX = kripke's □ for one-step accessibility; CTL's EX = kripke's ◇. The formulas are informationally equivalent at this level of detail. The same-span pair pattern was previously demonstrated by the slave-boy 002 (fol) / 003 (dialogical) pair (Meno 1781-1860); this is the **second corpus instance** of the same-span pair pattern, and the **first across modal formalisms**.

**CTL operator choice**: the encoding uses **AX** ('for every next state') and **EX** ('for some next state'), not AF/EF (eventually) or AG/EG (always). Aristotle's claim is about *tomorrow* specifically — a single-step temporal claim, not a long-run eventuality. The AX/EX choice tracks Aristotle's temporal scope precisely.

**The shared KripkeModel**: identical to the one in De Int 002. The schema design (`ctl_extraction.model: Optional[KripkeModel]`) lets ctl extractions reuse the kripke Kripke-model shape directly — the optional `model` field uses the same type in both wrappers. The 3-world branching frame (today w0; tomorrow-with-sea-fight w1; tomorrow-without-sea-fight w2; edges w0→w1, w0→w2) makes the modal claim's truth at w0 transparent.

**CTL and kripke equivalence note**: for single-step accessibility (one-step branching, as in the sea-battle), CTL's AX/EX are exactly the modal □/◇ operators. The CTL encoding becomes substantively *more* expressive than kripke when multi-step temporal nesting matters — AG (always on every path), AF (eventually on every path), A[φ U ψ] (until on every path). The sea-battle is the single-step case where the two encodings are equivalent; the corpus's choice to extract both is to instantiate the multi-modal-schema-pair pattern flagged in log.md iter 13 / iter 23.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to De Int 002 (kripke, iter 13)**: same source span 108; the two extractions are explicitly designed as a same-span same-passage pair across two modal formalisms. The kripke extraction was the schema-zero-gap closer for kripke (iter 13); this is the schema-zero-gap closer for ctl (iter 24). Together they exhaust the single-step branching-time encoding options in the current corpus schema set.

**Trade-off vs. classical-logic encoding**: as noted in De Int 002's reviewer notes, the intuitionistic mirror (zero extractions) could carry a fundamentally different philosophical reading — sea-battle as rejection-of-bivalence. A future intuitionistic extraction of De Int 9 would be the third same-span extraction (kripke + ctl + intuitionistic), demonstrating that a single Aristotelian passage can sustain multiple distinct logical readings. Flagged but not done; intuitionistic remains a candidate for the next schema-zero closure (sixth, after aristotelian/indian/kripke/boolean/ctl).

**Bekker reference**: 19a30-35 (same as De Int 002). The inline Bekker marker '30' at the start of line 108 in source.txt is preserved verbatim in the broader line but the chosen excerpt starts at 'A sea-fight' to avoid the artifact-in-excerpt issue (same convention as De Int 002).

**Aristotelian-vs-modern modal interpretation**: Aristotle's 'necessary' (ἀναγκαῖον) at De Int 9 likely tracks something like 'fixed by the nature of things' or 'true in all real alternatives' — neither identical to modern modal-logic □ (true in all R-accessible worlds) nor to alethic possibility (logically possible). The CTL encoding's AX/EX is the closest formal rendering; the Aristotelian flavor is not surfaced by the AST.

attributions.sourceText: A sea-fight must either take place to-morrow or not, but it is not necessary that it should take place to-morrow, neither is it necessary that it should not take place, yet it is necessary that it either should or should not take place to-morrow.

sourceExcerpt: A sea-fight must either take place to-morrow or not, but it is not necessary that it should take place to-morrow, neither is it necessary that it should not take place
intent: Capture the intuitionistic reading of Aristotle's sea-battle (De Int 9, line 108): the LEM instance SeaFight ∨ ¬SeaFight is not constructively forced at the present world, because the future is not yet determined. Same source span as De Int 002 (kripke) and De Int 003 (ctl) — the **third same-span extraction of De Int 9**, forming the corpus's first three-extraction same-span set. **First `intuitionistic` extraction** in the corpus; **seventh schema-zero closure** in the established pattern.
extractorNote: **Seventh schema-zero-gap closure** in the established pattern (aristotelian iter 1 → indian iter 9 → kripke iter 13 → boolean iter 23 → ctl iter 24 → temporal iter 25 → intuitionistic iter 26). Remaining zero-extraction mirrors: eg, epistemic, frege, medieval, resolution — **five of twelve still at zero**. **First three-extraction same-span set in the corpus**: De Int 002 (kripke) + De Int 003 (ctl) + De Int 004 (this, intuitionistic) — all on line 108 under three different philosophical readings (classical-modal-necessity / classical-branching-time / intuitionistic-constructive-failure-of-LEM). Substantive extension of the same-span pair pattern from doubles to triples. The intuitionistic encoding is the most philosophically distinct of the three — it shows the same passage *failing* to satisfy LEM in the constructive semantics, where the classical kripke and ctl encodings *affirm* the necessity of the disjunction. The historical-anachronism note (Aristotle did not have intuitionistic semantics) is documented in reviewer notes. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; schema-zero-closure milestone goes in log.md as continuation of the established pattern.
