assessments.reason: Same passage extracted as kripke at De Int 002 (iter 13) using the classical modal reading: □(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ ¬□SF ∧ ¬□¬SF. The kripke extraction *affirms* the modal disjunction's necessity; the intuitionistic extraction (this) shows that the same disjunction *fails to be forced* at the designated world in the pre-order Kripke frame — a fundamentally different philosophical reading of the sea-battle. The two are not equivalent (kripke has the disjunction holding necessarily; intuitionistic has it failing constructively); they encode two distinct ways of formalizing 'future contingent.' Recording the kripke 0.70 secondary acknowledges the model-shape similarity (both use 3-world branching frames) while distinguishing the philosophical readings.

Same as kripke for the classical branching-time reading (De Int 003, iter 24): AX(SF ∨ ¬SF) ∧ EX(SF) ∧ EX(¬SF). The CTL encoding affirms the next-state disjunction holds universally; the intuitionistic encoding here treats the disjunction as a constructive claim that fails. The 0.55 reflects same-model-shape but different-philosophical-stance.

Pure-propositional Boolean treats SF ∨ ¬SF as a tautology — exactly the classical reading the intuitionistic encoding *rejects* for unknown future propositions. Recording the boolean 0.25 to surface the contrast: classical-propositional logic forces LEM; intuitionistic semantics doesn't.

FOL without modal/temporal operators cannot represent the constructive-forcing semantics — the formula SF ∨ ¬SF is a classical tautology in fol, exactly the reading the intuitionistic encoding rejects.
Not dialogical.

reviewerNotes: Source: same as De Int 002 / 003 (Bekker 19a30-35; Edghill 1928 translation; Wikisource transcription). Span identical: line 108. Excerpt narrower than De Int 002/003 (stops before 'yet it is necessary that it either should or should not take place to-morrow') to highlight the *non-necessity-of-disjuncts* clause that motivates the intuitionistic reading. The closing necessary-disjunction clause is the *classical* reading captured by De Int 002 and 003.

**The intuitionistic schema** reuses the kripke Kripke-model shape (per schemas/intuitionistic.py docstring), interpreting it as a pre-order with reflexive + transitive accessibility and monotone atom valuation. The formula AST is the kripke fragment *minus* □/◇ (no modal operators in intuitionistic propositional logic). For the sea-battle: the formula is the LEM instance SF ∨ ¬SF; the model is the 3-world pre-order branching frame.

**The included pre-order Kripke frame**: w0 (today, no atoms — SeaFight not yet determined); w1 (tomorrow with sea-fight, SeaFight in atoms); w2 (tomorrow without sea-fight, no atoms — SeaFight remains undetermined in this branch). Edges: reflexive self-loops at w0, w1, w2 (pre-order reflexivity); w0 ≤ w1; w0 ≤ w2 (pre-order extending toward both branches). Monotonicity: atoms at w0 = ∅ ⊆ atoms at w1 = {SeaFight} and atoms at w0 = ∅ ⊆ atoms at w2 = ∅ — monotone propagation holds.

**Why LEM fails at w0 in this model**: in intuitionistic Kripke semantics, SF is forced at w iff SF is in atoms at every w' ≥ w. Since w1 ≥ w0 has SF and w2 ≥ w0 does not, SF is not forced at w0 (not at *every* successor). ¬SF is forced at w iff SF is not forced at any w' ≥ w. Since SF is forced at w1 ≥ w0, ¬SF is not forced at w0. Therefore SF ∨ ¬SF is not forced at w0. This is the intuitionistic counterexample to LEM — the same model-shape as De Int 002's kripke frame, but with a different *semantic* interpretation (constructive forcing rather than classical truth).

**The intuitionistic vs classical readings of the sea-battle**: De Int 002 (kripke) and De Int 003 (ctl) capture the classical reading — Aristotle affirms the necessity of the disjunction. This intuitionistic extraction captures the *alternative* philosophical reading — Aristotle's qualifier about the non-necessity of each disjunct suggests a pre-classical sense in which the future is not yet determined. Łukasiewicz's three-valued logic (1920) is one historical response that introduces an explicit 'indeterminate' truth-value; Brouwerian intuitionism (1907+) rejects LEM for unbounded sequences and futures. The intuitionistic encoding here aligns with the Brouwerian reading more than with Aristotle's own framework (Aristotle did not have intuitionistic semantics).

**Cross-extraction same-span triple**: De Int 002 (kripke), De Int 003 (ctl), De Int 004 (intuitionistic) — all encoding line 108 of De Interpretatione under three different philosophical readings of the same Aristotelian text. **First three-extraction same-span set in the corpus**. Distinct from the slave-boy 002/003 (fol+dialogical, two extractions same span) and Hobbes 001/002 (fol+temporal, two extractions same span) — same-span pairs both. The De Int 9 triple is a substantive extension: a single passage now anchors *three* formalisms in the corpus.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Metaphysics 002 (LEM)**: Metaphysics IV.7 (extracted as boolean P ∨ ¬P in iter 23) affirms LEM as a logical principle. De Int 9 (extracted as kripke / ctl / now intuitionistic) considers whether LEM applies to future contingents. Aristotle's overall position: PNC absolute (Met 001), LEM absolute (Met 002), but bivalence (the principle that every proposition is determinately true or false) limited for future contingents (De Int 002/003/004). The corpus now has explicit extractions for all three principles and their interactions — the **first triple-principle Aristotle logical-foundations cluster** with deliberate cross-references.

**The intuitionistic mirror is the seventh closure**; remaining zero-extraction mirrors: eg, epistemic, frege, medieval, resolution. **Five of twelve still at zero.** The intuitionistic closure was the most-flagged remaining target across iterations 13, 23, 24 (each surfaced intuitionistic as a 0.5+ secondary or candidate). This iteration finally lands it.

**Aristotle did not have intuitionistic semantics**: the encoded reading is an *anachronistic* application of 20th-century intuitionistic logic to a 4th-century-BCE Aristotelian text. The reading is *charitable* — Aristotle's reservations about future contingents map naturally to intuitionistic concerns about constructive proof — but the formal apparatus is not Aristotelian. Documenting this anachronism explicitly per the corpus convention (similar to the Dignagan-vs-Gotama-Sutra historical-stratigraphic note in Nyāya 001).

attributions.sourceText: A sea-fight must either take place to-morrow or not, but it is not necessary that it should take place to-morrow, neither is it necessary that it should not take place

sourceExcerpt: the same attribute cannot at the same time belong and not belong to the same subject in the same respect
intent: Capture Aristotle's statement of the **Principle of Non-Contradiction** (PNC) at Metaphysics IV.3, Bekker 1005b19-20 (line 317): the same attribute cannot at the same time belong and not belong to the same subject in the same respect. **The most foundational logical principle in Western philosophy**; Aristotle explicitly characterizes it as 'the most certain principle of all' (line 317) and notes it cannot be demonstrated (line 321). The corpus's first Metaphysics extraction; fourth Aristotle work extracted (after Categories, Prior Analytics, NE × 2, De Interpretatione × 2 = 5 Aristotle extractions); fifth Aristotle extraction overall.
extractorNote: **First Metaphysics extraction** — Aristotle's *Metaphysics* is among the corpus's foundational philosophical works (Wikisource transcription Books I-XIII + archive.org Book XIV per log.md 2026-05-20). **Fifth Aristotle extraction overall** across four works: Categories (Barbara worked instance), Prior Analytics (Celarent schematic), Nicomachean Ethics (× 2: arts-aim-at-good + ergon argument), De Interpretatione (× 2: universal/individual + sea-battle), now Metaphysics (PNC). The PNC is one of the three most-cited single principles in the history of Western logic (alongside Excluded Middle and Identity). Encoded as a fol universal-over-attribute-subject-pair formula — the simplified shape, with the temporal and respect qualifiers documented in reviewer notes rather than parameterized in the AST. The boolean secondary at 0.55 is the highest secondary score yet for a propositional-logic-shaped passage — the boolean mirror has zero extractions and PNC reframed propositionally would be a natural future first occupant. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the first-Metaphysics / fourth-Aristotle-work / PNC-extracted milestones go in SESSION_LOG. PNC underwrites both the syllogistic extractions (Categories 001, Prior Analytics 001) and the broader inferentialist target of the corpus.

assessments.reason: Propositional non-contradiction is the textbook law ¬(P ∧ ¬P) — the central law of classical Boolean propositional logic. A boolean extraction with atom 'BelongsTo(a,s)' treated propositionally could carry the formula directly. Not chosen as primary because Aristotle's claim is about attribute-subject relations (binary predicates with universal closure), not bare propositional atoms; fol carries the relational structure plus the universal quantification more faithfully. The boolean mirror is at zero extractions; this is a candidate for a future boolean extraction if reframed.
assessments.distortionRisk: Reframing as bare propositional ¬(P ∧ ¬P) would lose the universal-over-attribute-subject-pair quantification that Aristotle explicitly states ('the SAME attribute... the SAME subject').

assessments.reason: Term logic could cast PNC as 'No subject is both bearer-of-A and bearer-of-not-A' — an E-form proposition on a complement-pair. But term logic does not have a sentential negation operator; expressing ¬BelongsTo(a, s) as a term ('not-A') flattens the propositional negation into a term-negation, which is a different operation (Aristotle distinguishes the two carefully in De Interpretatione 10-11). The aristotelian schema's CategoricalProposition cannot represent the propositional structure of PNC.

PNC is *stated* in this passage, not derived. Aristotle explicitly says (later in the same chapter, lines 321+) that PNC cannot be demonstrated — any attempted demonstration would presuppose it. An nd extraction would have empty premises and PNC as the conclusion — degenerate.
Not dialogical. Treatise statement of the principle.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Metaphysics*, Book IV (Gamma), Chapter 3. Bekker reference 1005b19-20. W. D. Ross translation (1908 Oxford). PG note: Wikisource transcription of the 'deannotated' edition Books I-XIII; Book XIV is OCR from archive.org per log.md 2026-05-20.

**The 'at the same time' and 'in the same respect' qualifiers** are philosophically load-bearing. PNC without these qualifiers would be false: a coin is both heads-up (now) and tails-up (a moment later); a road can be uphill (in one respect, going north) and downhill (in another respect, going south). The qualifiers restrict PNC to a single time-and-respect, where contradiction is impossible. The encoded fol formula ∀a∀s. ¬(BelongsTo(a, s) ∧ ¬BelongsTo(a, s)) is the **simplified** form; the temporal and respect qualifiers are flattened into the implicit assumption that the predicate `BelongsTo(a, s)` is evaluated at a single (time, respect) point. A fuller encoding would parameterize: ∀a∀s∀t∀r. ¬(BelongsAt(a, s, t, r) ∧ ¬BelongsAt(a, s, t, r)) — but the additional parameters are documented here rather than encoded, per the formula-tightness convention.

**PNC is indemonstrable** — Aristotle states explicitly (lines 321-323): 'Some indeed demand that even this shall be demonstrated, but this they do through want of education... It is impossible that there should be demonstration of absolutely everything; there would be an infinite regress.' PNC is the *starting-point* of demonstration, not a derived conclusion. The fol encoding here captures the principle as a stated universal; the indemonstrability claim is meta-logical commentary, not directly encodable in the AST.

**Aristotle's 'elenctic refutation' of PNC's deniers** runs from lines 321-345+ (out of this span). Aristotle argues that someone who denies PNC nevertheless presupposes it whenever they assert *anything* meaningfully — to say 'A is and is not' meaningfully, one must distinguish A from not-A, which already invokes PNC. The argument is a candidate future companion extraction (likely dialogical or nd over the elenctic-refutation arc).

**Predicate naming**: `BelongsTo(a, s)` encodes Aristotle's 'belong to' relation (ὑπάρχειν) between attribute and subject. This is the same relation Aristotle uses throughout the Organon (Categories, Prior Analytics) for predication. Cross-extraction predicate-naming consistency note: the corpus's prior aristotelian extractions (Categories 001, Prior Analytics 001) use the syllogistic-form encoding (subject/predicate as term strings) rather than the BelongsTo relational encoding; Metaphysics 001 here uses BelongsTo because the principle is *about* the belong-to relation itself rather than instantiating it.

**Cross-work conceptual link to Prior Analytics 001 and Categories 001**: PNC underwrites the validity of syllogistic. Barbara (Categories 001) and Celarent (Prior Analytics 001) are valid because PNC holds — if subjects could both be and not be predicates, the syllogistic conclusion would not follow with necessity. PNC is the meta-principle behind the syllogistic-validity claims those extractions encode.

**Cross-work conceptual link to De Interpretatione 002 (sea-battle)**: De Int 9 considers whether bivalence (every proposition is determinately true or false) holds for future contingents. Bivalence is *not* equivalent to PNC: PNC says ¬(P ∧ ¬P), bivalence says P ∨ ¬P. Aristotle accepts PNC absolutely (here at Met IV.3) but considers limiting bivalence for future contingents (De Int 9). The two principles' divergence is a subtle and important Aristotelian commitment; future iterations might extract Metaphysics IV.7 or 8 (which states the law of excluded middle, LEM) to surface this distinction explicitly. (21st cross-span citation in spirit; not re-flagging.)

**Reception**: PNC is *the* most-discussed single principle in the history of logic. Aristotle's argument against its deniers (anti-Protagoras / anti-Heraclitus, the elenctic refutation) is the canonical defense; medieval logic (Aquinas, Buridan) systematized it; Hegel and dialetheism (Priest, Routley) are the main historical challenges. Łukasiewicz's *Über den Satz des Widerspruchs* (1910) is the major modern reconstruction. Brandom's *Articulating Reasons* extends the discussion to material-incompatibility relations as the semantic basis for normative content — the corpus's long-horizon target. PNC underwrites the inferentialist project at the basic level.

**Translation choices**: Ross's 'the same attribute cannot at the same time belong and not belong to the same subject in the same respect' renders τὸ γὰρ αὐτὸ ἅμα ὑπάρχειν τε καὶ μὴ ὑπάρχειν ἀδύνατον τῷ αὐτῷ καὶ κατὰ τὸ αὐτό. The standard alternative is 'it is impossible for the same thing to belong and not to belong at the same time to the same thing and in the same respect.' Both faithful; Ross's reads more naturally in English.

**The first chapter of Metaphysics IV (Gamma)** introduces metaphysics as the science of being-qua-being. PNC is the central logical-metaphysical principle defended in IV.3-4. The PNC chapter (IV.3) opens with the search for 'the most certain principle of all' (line 317 of source.txt) and identifies PNC as the answer.

attributions.sourceText: the same attribute cannot at the same time belong and not belong to the same subject in the same respect

sourceExcerpt: there cannot be an intermediate between contradictories, but of one subject we must either affirm or deny any one predicate
intent: Capture Aristotle's statement of the **Law of Excluded Middle** (LEM) at Metaphysics IV.7, Bekker 1011b23-24 (line 394): there cannot be an intermediate between contradictories. The canonical dual to PNC (Metaphysics 001) — together PNC and LEM are the two foundational principles of classical logic. **First `boolean` extraction in the corpus**; closes the **fourth schema-zero gap** following the pattern from iters 1 (aristotelian), 9 (indian), 13 (kripke). Same-work companion to Metaphysics 001.
extractorNote: **Fourth schema-zero-gap closure** following the pattern from iters 1 (aristotelian, Categories 001 = Barbara), 9 (indian, Nyāya Sūtras 001 = smoke-fire anumāna), and 13 (kripke, De Int 002 = sea-battle). Each closure picks the canonical foundational passage for the zero-extraction schema; LEM is the textbook propositional-logic schema-instance. Remaining zero-extraction mirrors: ctl, eg, epistemic, frege, intuitionistic, medieval, resolution, temporal — **eight mirrors still at zero**. **Second Metaphysics extraction**, forming a **same-work multi-extraction pair** with Metaphysics 001 (PNC) — this is a new pair type for the corpus: same-work, different-span (line 317 vs line 394), different-formalism (fol vs boolean). PNC + LEM together define classical bivalent logic; the corpus now has both foundational principles. The boolean encoding is a *schema instance* (P ∨ ¬P for atom P) rather than the universal claim Aristotle states; the universal-over-attribute-subject-pair encoding (the natural dual to PNC in fol) is documented in reviewer notes. This is the corpus's first deliberate trade-off between *schema-zero closure* and *most-faithful-formalism* — chose boolean to close the gap, knowing fol would preserve the universal quantifier better. Recording the trade-off explicitly; routine extraction by criteria but the milestone goes in log.md (fourth schema-zero closure extends the established pattern).

assessments.reason: Aristotle's claim is universal: 'of ONE subject we must EITHER affirm OR deny ANY ONE predicate.' Fully first-order: ∀a∀s. (BelongsTo(a, s) ∨ ¬BelongsTo(a, s)) — the natural dual to PNC (Metaphysics 001). A fol extraction would preserve the universal-over-attribute-subject-pair character that boolean (being propositional) cannot. Not chosen as primary because the boolean mirror has zero extractions and LEM is paradigmatic propositional content; the corpus benefits more from opening the boolean mirror than from another fol formula. The fol-encoded LEM (and its symmetry with the fol-encoded PNC of Metaphysics 001) is documented in reviewer notes.
assessments.distortionRisk: The boolean encoding loses the universal quantification. The schema-instance reading (LEM as a schema instantiated for an arbitrary atom P) preserves the *form* of the law but not the explicit ∀.

assessments.reason: LEM has a categorical-shape rendering: 'No subject is a non-affirmer-and-non-denier of a given predicate' — but as with PNC, term logic does not have sentential negation, and the propositional structure of LEM (P ∨ ¬P with sentential not) gets flattened into term-negation. Categories 001-style aristotelian encoding loses the operator-level disjunction.

Intuitionistic propositional logic *rejects* LEM as a general theorem — for the intuitionist, P ∨ ¬P is derivable only when one has a proof of P or a proof of ¬P. The intuitionistic mirror could encode LEM as a *non-theorem* / *rejected schema*, which is a different philosophical move from Aristotle's *affirmation* of LEM. Recording as a candidate cross-extraction comparison: Aristotle 002 (LEM as theorem) vs a future intuitionistic extraction (LEM as non-theorem) would be a substantive philosophical pair.
assessments.distortionRisk: Encoding LEM in the intuitionistic mirror would reframe Aristotle's positive claim into the intuitionistic framework that rejects it.

assessments.reason: LEM is *stated* in this passage, not derived. Aristotle's *defense* of LEM against deniers (the surrounding context at lines 335+, 400+) is long-form argument; this extraction captures only the bare statement.
Not dialogical.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Metaphysics*, Book IV (Gamma), Chapter 7. Bekker reference 1011b23-24. W. D. Ross translation (1908 Oxford). Wikisource transcription. Same translator and source as Metaphysics 001 (PNC at IV.3, line 317).

**The `boolean` encoding represents LEM as a schema instance** for an arbitrary propositional atom P. Aristotle's actual claim is universal — '*of one subject* we must either affirm or deny *any one predicate*' — which fully first-order reads ∀a∀s. (BelongsTo(a, s) ∨ ¬BelongsTo(a, s)). The boolean schema is purely propositional (no quantifiers, no predicates, no terms), so the schema-instance encoding (P ∨ ¬P for atom P) is the closest faithful rendering in this mirror. The schema-instance reading is standard in propositional-logic pedagogy: 'P ∨ ¬P' is *the* statement of LEM in introductory logic, even though the universal claim is what's substantively meant.

**Same-work companion to Metaphysics 001 (PNC)**. PNC: ¬(P ∧ ¬P); LEM: P ∨ ¬P. Together they define classical bivalent logic. Aristotle treats PNC at IV.3 (line 317) and LEM at IV.7 (line 394) as the two most certain logical principles. Encoding PNC as fol (Metaphysics 001) and LEM as boolean (this extraction) is a deliberate **same-work multi-formalism pair** — the formalism choice tracks the schema-zero-closure pattern. A fol encoding of LEM (the dual to PNC) would be ∀a∀s. (BelongsTo(a, s) ∨ ¬BelongsTo(a, s)); this is documented here rather than as the primary AST, in line with the iter-13 multi-modal-schema-viability flag (kripke 002 sea-battle could have been ctl or temporal; here LEM could have been fol but is boolean to close the schema-zero gap).

**Cross-paragraph dependency**: Metaphysics IV.7 (this LEM passage) follows Aristotle's elenctic refutation of PNC-deniers in IV.3-6 (lines 321+). The full LEM defense at lines 394-410+ explicitly distinguishes 'contradictories' (which admit no intermediate) from 'contraries' (which may — grey between black and white). The bare LEM statement at line 394 is the canonical formulation; the surrounding defense is held as future companion candidate. 23rd cross-span citation in spirit; not re-flagging.

**'Contradictories' vs 'contraries'**: Aristotle is careful (line 394 and later, line 797+) to distinguish (a) contradictories — P and ¬P, which admit no intermediate (LEM applies) — from (b) contraries — extreme opposites like black and white, which may admit intermediates like grey. LEM applies to contradictories, not to contraries. The encoded P ∨ ¬P captures the contradictory case; the contrary-with-intermediate case is documented here as the Aristotelian qualification.

**Intuitionistic-logic contrast**: Brouwer's intuitionistic logic (~1907) rejects LEM as a general theorem on the grounds that P ∨ ¬P is derivable only when one has a constructive proof of P or of ¬P. The `intuitionistic` mirror (in this corpus's schema set, currently at zero extractions) could carry LEM as a *rejected schema* — a substantive philosophical pair with this Aristotle extraction. Flagged as a candidate future extraction.

**Three-valued logic**: Łukasiewicz's three-valued logic (1920) introduces an intermediate truth-value 'indeterminate' specifically to handle the future-contingents case Aristotle considers at De Int 9 (extracted as De Int 002 in iter 13). In Łukasiewicz's framework, LEM fails for future-contingent propositions but holds for present/past. Aristotle himself takes a different route (limiting bivalence rather than LEM) — but the distinction is subtle and the modern reception sometimes conflates the two.

**Reception**: LEM is, like PNC, a most-discussed single principle in logic. Brouwer's intuitionistic rejection (1907+), Heyting's formalization (1930), Kripke's intuitionistic-Kripke semantics (1965), and computational interpretations via the Curry-Howard correspondence are the main 20th-century reception. Dialetheism (Priest, Routley) accepts true contradictions but typically retains LEM, distinguishing it from the dialetheist's rejection of PNC.

**Cross-extraction conceptual pair with Metaphysics 001 (PNC)**: PNC + LEM together define classical bivalent logic. The corpus now has both principles extracted from the same Aristotle work — the **first same-work multi-extraction pair** in the corpus (Metaphysics 001 = PNC at line 317; this = LEM at line 394). Distinct from the same-span pair pattern (slave-boy 002/003 share lines 1781-1860 with different formalisms) and the different-span pair pattern (Euthyphro 700-741 / 743-792 within same dialogue). This is **same-work, different-span, different-formalism**: a new pair type.

sourceExcerpt: there is something which always moves the things that are in motion, and the first mover must itself be unmoved
intent: Capture Aristotle's foundational Unmoved Mover claim from Metaphysics IV (line 402, anticipating the fuller development in Book XII / Lambda): there exists a first mover that is itself unmoved. This is the **canonical Aristotelian first-cause argument** — the foundational text of cosmological-argument tradition (Aquinas's First Way) and the conceptual ancestor of Western philosophical theology's necessity-of-an-unmoved-first-mover reasoning. **Third Metaphysics extraction**; **Aristotle now at 18 extractions across 10 works**; **completes Aristotelian metaphysics cluster with Met 001 (PNC) + Met 002 (LEM) + Met 003 (Unmoved Mover — this)**.
extractorNote: **Third Metaphysics extraction** — Aristotle now at **18 extractions across 10 works**: Categories × 1, De Interpretatione × 4, Prior Analytics × 1, Posterior Analytics × 1, Topics × 1, Sophistical Refutations × 1, **Metaphysics × 3** (PNC + LEM + Unmoved Mover — this), NE × 2, De Anima × 3, Politics × 2. Aristotle remains corpus's **most-extracted author** at 18 of 66 = 27%. **Sixth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**: Aristotle De Int 001-004; Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003; Descartes Meditations 001-003; Aristotle De Anima 001-003; Hegel Phenomenology 001-003; Aristotle Metaphysics 001-003 (this — sixth instance). Aristotle has now contributed **three three-or-more-extraction same-work coverages**: De Int (four-extraction same-span via four formalisms), De Anima (three-chapter three-book), Metaphysics (three-from-same-book — this). The Metaphysics coverage is structurally distinctive: three extractions all from Book IV (Γ), exhausting Book IV's foundational metaphysical principles (PNC + LEM + Unmoved-Mover-corollary). **Two new corpus conceptual opposition pairs**: (33) Aristotle Met 003 vs Spinoza Ethics 002 (transcendent first cause vs immanent self-caused substance); (34) Aristotle Met 003 vs Hume Dialogues 001 (cosmological positive-inference vs Problem-of-Evil negative-challenge). **Thirty-four conceptual oppositions now in corpus**. **Sixteenth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle Met 003 + Descartes Meditations 003 + Berkeley Three Dialogues 002 as positive theistic inferences (cosmological + ontological + perception-based methods). **Sixteen alliances now documented**. **Philosophy-of-religion strand now substantively 5-vs-1**: 5 positive-theistic-inference extractions (Aristotle Met 003 + Descartes 001/003 + Berkeley 002 + Marcus Aurelius 002 + Leibniz × 2) vs Hume Dialogues 001 as dissenting voice. **Aristotelian metaphysics cluster complete**: Book IV's three foundational principles (PNC + LEM + Unmoved Mover) all extracted. Fortieth fol extraction; fol now at 40 of 66 = 61%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the third-Metaphysics + Aristotle-at-18-extractions + sixth-three-or-more-extraction-same-work-coverage + Aristotle-Met-Book-IV-three-extractions-complete + two-new-oppositions + sixteenth-alliance + philosophy-of-religion-5-vs-1 milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The wider passage develops the unmoved-mover argument inferentially: assume all things are in motion → contradiction (statements become unstable); assume all things at rest → contradiction (change demonstrably occurs); therefore something must always move things in motion AND be unmoved itself. A nd extraction could carry this exclusion-of-extremes argument structure. The fol extraction captures the existence claim; nd would be the supporting argument.
Existential quantification (∃m. FirstMover(m) ∧ Unmoved(m)) not expressible in unary term logic. Categorical structure cannot carry the existence claim.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Metaphysics*, Book IV (Γ), section on the impossibility of universal-motion or universal-rest, line 402. The Unmoved Mover argument receives its fullest development in Book XII (Lambda) at Bekker 1072a-b, but appears in compressed form here in Book IV. Source.txt from Ross 1928 translation per Met 001/002 manifest.

**Predicate naming**: `FirstMover(m)` for the Aristotelian *to prōton kinoun* (the first mover — first in the causal-priority sense, not temporally first); `Unmoved(m)` for the *akinēton* (unmoved — not subject to motion or change).

**The existential encoding**: captures Aristotle's *existence* claim — there exists such a first-and-unmoved mover. The fuller argument (in Book XII) derives this via the impossibility of an infinite regress in the causal chain of movers: if every mover is itself moved, then there is an infinite regress with no first; but a first mover is required to ground the chain; therefore the first mover must itself be unmoved.

**The Book IV vs Book XII formulation**: Book IV (this extraction) gives the Unmoved Mover claim as a *corollary* of the rejection of universal-motion-or-universal-rest theories (Heraclitean flux vs Parmenidean rest). Book XII gives the more substantive Unmoved Mover argument as a *cosmological* claim about the structure of the universe (the prime mover causes circular motion of the heavens via being a *final cause* / object of desire). The Book XII development is a future-extraction candidate.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Met 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage)**: Met 001 (Principle of Non-Contradiction, Book IV) + Met 002 (Law of Excluded Middle, Book IV) + Met 003 (Unmoved Mover, Book IV — this). Three Metaphysics extractions all from Book IV (Γ — On Being qua Being), exhausting the foundational metaphysical principles of Book IV. **Sixth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage** (joining Aristotle De Int 001-004; Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003; Descartes Meditations 001-003; Aristotle De Anima 001-003; Hegel Phenomenology 001-003). The Aristotelian Met three-extraction coverage is *same-book* (all in Book IV) rather than *across-multiple-books* like De Anima — a structural sub-variant. **Aristotle has now contributed two three-extraction same-work coverages** (De Anima three-book; Metaphysics three-from-same-book).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes Meditations 003 (substantive alliance — both positive God-existence inferences)**: Descartes's Ontological Argument (Meditations V §10) infers God's existence from essence; Aristotle's Unmoved Mover argument (this) infers the existence of a first-mover from the impossibility of infinite causal regress. Both are *positive* theological inferences (existence of God/divine first mover). **Sixteenth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle Met 003 + Descartes Meditations 003 + Berkeley Three Dialogues 002 as positive theistic inferences (cosmological + ontological + perception-based methods). The philosophy-of-religion strand's positive-theistic-inference side now has *three* extractions, all opposing Hume Dialogues 001 (Problem of Evil).

**Philosophy-of-religion strand now structured 5-vs-1** with Hume Dialogues 001 as dissenting voice**: Aristotle Met 003 (Unmoved Mover) + Descartes Meditations 001/003 (cogito-for-God + Ontological Argument) + Berkeley Three Dialogues 002 (God-from-perception) + Marcus Aurelius 002 (Stoic providence) + Leibniz Monadology + Discourse 001 (theodicy framework) = 5 positive-theistic-inference extractions vs Hume Dialogues 001 (Problem of Evil). The corpus's dissenting-voice structure on theology is now substantively documented.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Posterior Analytics 001 (substantive within-author alliance — necessity-via-first-causes)**: Aristotle's *epistēmē* (scientific knowledge) requires knowing the cause + necessity. The Unmoved Mover argument is the *cosmological* application of cause-based reasoning: the first cause (unmoved mover) grounds all motion. Within-author conceptual alignment: Aristotle's epistemology and cosmology converge on cause-as-foundational.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza Ethics 002 (substantive opposition — Aristotelian first-cause vs Spinozistic substance-as-self-caused)**: Aristotle's Unmoved Mover is a *first cause distinct from* the moved things (transcendent cosmological structure); Spinoza's substance-monism (Ethics 002) is *causa sui* (self-caused) and *identical with* Nature (immanent cosmological structure). **Thirty-third corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle Met 003 (transcendent first cause) vs Spinoza Ethics 002 (immanent self-caused substance). The Aristotle-Spinoza contrast on the structure of cosmological causation is canonical.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Dialogues 001 (substantive opposition — cosmological first-cause vs Problem-of-Evil)**: Aristotle Met 003 is a *positive theological* inference (first cause exists); Hume Dialogues 001 is a *negative theological* argument (problem of evil challenges traditional theism). **Thirty-fourth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle Met 003 (cosmological Unmoved Mover positive inference) vs Hume Dialogues 001 (Problem of Evil negative challenge). The philosophy-of-religion 5-vs-1 structure is now substantively documented with Aristotle Met 003 as the fifth positive-inference extraction.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle De Anima 003 (substantive within-author parallel — eternal-and-unmoved active intellect parallels Unmoved Mover)**: Aristotle's active intellect (De Anima III.5, Met 003 active intellect) is eternal, separable, and unaffected by external impressions. The Unmoved Mover (Met 003) is also eternal and unmoved. Both are *eternal-and-unaffected* substances in Aristotle's framework. Substantive within-author conceptual parallel on the structure of eternal-and-unaffected substances. The Aristotelian metaphysics-and-psychology connection is now corpus-grounded across multi-extraction coverage.

**Reception**: Aristotle's Unmoved Mover is the foundational text of Western cosmological-theological argument. Aquinas's *Summa Theologiae* I.2.3 First Way (the argument from motion) explicitly inherits the Aristotelian Unmoved Mover argument. The medieval Islamic Aristotelian tradition (Al-Farabi, Avicenna, Averroes) develops the first-cause framework. The modern cosmological argument (Leibniz Principle of Sufficient Reason, Clarke, contemporary Craig Kalam argument) traces to the Aristotelian first-cause framework. Critics: Hume *Dialogues* explicitly targets the cosmological argument; Kant *Critique of Pure Reason* refutes it via the antinomies.

**Translation choices**: Ross 1928 'first mover' for *prōton kinoun* and 'unmoved' for *akinēton* is standard. The Greek technical phrase *to prōton kinoun akinēton* = 'the first unmoved mover' is the canonical formulation; the encoding faithfully captures the existence claim with both attributes.

sourceExcerpt:
Every art, and every science reduced to a teachable form, and in like
manner every action and moral choice, aims, it is thought, at some
good
intent: Capture the opening sentence of the Nicomachean Ethics — the universal claim that every art, every (teachable) science, every action, and every moral choice aims at some good — as a single quantified FOL formula.
extractorNote: Single-formula FOL render of one hedged universal. Lossy in (1) the modal hedging of 'it is thought' and (2) the teleological flavour of 'aims at', which is reduced to a flat binary relation. Compact and tractable; primarily exercises ∀x → ∃y nesting and a four-way disjunction in the antecedent — neither of which the Meno extraction touched.

assessments.reason: On the surface 'all arts/sciences/actions/choices aim at some good' looks categorical and A-form. But the predicate 'aims at some good' hides a binary relation (AimsAt(x,y)) plus an existential over the aim. Term logic forces this into a unary predicate 'aimer-at-some-good', losing the relation between an art and its specific end — which is the whole point of the opening of NE.
assessments.distortionRisk: Collapsing AimsAt(x,y) into a unary predicate cannot subsequently support Aristotle's chained reasoning (Chapter I) about hierarchies of ends and master-arts.

assessments.reason: The sentence asserts a single (hedged) universal — there is no inferential step from premises to conclusion. nd would treat this either as a premise-only argument (degenerate) or invent inferential structure that is not in the text.
assessments.distortionRisk: Premise/conclusion machinery invites spurious reconstruction of an argument where the text only asserts.

reviewerNotes: Speaker: Aristotle, expository. Hedged with 'it is thought' (δοκεῖ in the Greek), positioning the claim as endoxic / received opinion rather than a unilateral assertion. The hedge is not represented in the AST.

Restriction 'every science reduced to a teachable form' encoded as a conjunction Science(x) ∧ TeachableForm(x). This plausibly tracks Aristotle's distinction between technai/epistēmai with explicit method and looser uses of 'science'; the encoding does not preserve that further interpretive layer.

'Some good' rendered with an existential ∃y. Good(y); the question whether each art aims at a different good or all aim at one common Chief Good is not yet in view in this sentence and is therefore not encoded. The very next clause (lines 844–845, not in this extraction) connects the universal to the conventional description of the Chief Good as 'that which all things aim at'.

'Aims at' rendered as binary predicate AimsAt(x,y); the teleological background of 'telos' / 'end' that this verb carries elsewhere in the text is elided. A future Davidsonian event-semantics extraction mode (flagged in docs/formal_argument_parsing.md as not yet mirrored) would represent the 'aiming' as an event with goal/telos roles rather than a flat binary predicate.

Project Gutenberg edition uses 'art' for technē; 'science' for epistēmē; 'moral choice' for prohairesis. Predicate names follow the English translation rather than transliteration — this is a translation-layer decision and should be revisited if a multi-translation extraction strategy emerges.

sourceExcerpt:
the work of any given subject, and of that subject good of its kind,
are the same in kind
intent: Capture Aristotle's function/ergon argument at NE I.5 (Chase translation, NE I.7 in standard numbering): given that the good of any kind consists in doing its characteristic work well, and that the work of Man is rational activity of soul, the Good of Man is rational activity of soul performed excellently.
extractorNote: Compact nd with three FOL premises and one FOL conclusion — exercises function symbols (WorkOf, GoodOf, DoneWell), equality, and a universal premise instantiated at a constant (Man). Lossy in (1) the harp-player analogy that motivates the universal premise, (2) the modal hedge 'or at least not independently of reason', and (3) the superlative qualifier on Excellence. No Fitch proof attached: the nd-types.ts mirror is presently propositional only (no quantifier or equality rules), so a proof of this argument would require an extension. The argument structure is recorded; the proof gap is itself a finding.

assessments.reason: The passage's central inference turns on identity claims (GoodOf(Man) = DoneWell(WorkOf(Man))) and on applying a function (WorkOf, GoodOf) to a particular term (Man). Term logic carries neither: it has no equality and no function-application structure.
assessments.distortionRisk: Forcing into syllogistic shape would require reifying 'good of x' as a unary predicate 'is-good-of-its-kind' and dropping the identity with 'doing the work well' — losing the very content of the argument.

assessments.reason: FOL is the formula language used here, but a single FOL formula collapses the explicit 'if-then... if... then... if all this is so, then...' structure that the prose deploys. nd records the inferential step as primary; FOL is its formula language.
assessments.distortionRisk: Compressing into one big implication loses the staged conditional structure Aristotle actually writes.

reviewerNotes: Speaker: Aristotle, expository; the prose stages the argument as 'if then... if... then... if all this is so, then...'. The three premises here are extracted from that staged conditional; they are not labelled as such in the text.

Premise 1 ('∀x. HasWork(x) → GoodOf(x) = DoneWell(WorkOf(x))') is reconstructed from two adjacent claims in the text: 'the work of any given subject, and of that subject good of its kind, are the same in kind' (1244-1245) and 'adding to the work eminence in the way of excellence' (1246-1247). The harp-player and good-harp-player example (1245-1248) is the analogical evidence Aristotle gives for this premise; it is dropped from the AST.

Premise 2 ('WorkOf(Man) = WorkingOfSoulWithReason') is hedged in the text by 'or at least not independently of reason' (1242-1243). The hedge — a modal weakening of the identity — is not encoded; encoding it would need a graded or modal extension.

Premise 3 ('HasWork(Man)') is not stated in this exact passage — it is established earlier in the chapter (lines 1225-1230, the 'Man as Man' has a work argument). It is included here as an explicit premise because the conclusion does not follow without it.

The conclusion's qualifier 'if Excellence admits of degrees, in the way of the best and most perfect Excellence' (1254-1255) is not encoded. It introduces graded / superlative structure that flat FOL does not naturally carry; a Kripke-style modal or comparative extension would be needed.

Translation note: Chase's 'work' renders Aristotle's ergon, sometimes also translated 'function'. 'GoodOf' and 'DoneWell' are translation-layer names following Chase's vocabulary ('good of its kind', 'do these things well and nobly'). A multi-translation extraction strategy would normalise these.

Standard Stephanus / Bekker numbering for this passage is approximately NE 1098a7-18 (Chapter I.7 in the Bekker chapter division); the Chase edition labels it Chapter V because it merges some chapters earlier in Book I.

sourceExcerpt: a natural production, and that man is naturally a political animal
intent: Capture Aristotle's iconic dual claim from Politics I.2 (Bekker 1253a, line 593): the city is a natural production AND man is naturally a political animal. One of the foundational claims of Western political philosophy. **First Politics extraction** — tenth Aristotle work for the corpus. Aristotle now extracted across **ten works** with **fourteen extractions overall** (Aristotle is the corpus's most-extracted author by a substantial margin).
extractorNote: **First Politics extraction** — **tenth Aristotle work** for the corpus. Aristotle now extracted across **ten works** (Categories, De Interpretatione, Prior Analytics, Posterior Analytics, Topics, Sophistical Refutations, Metaphysics, Nicomachean Ethics, De Anima, Politics) with **fourteen extractions overall = 35% of corpus's 39 successful extractions** (after the iter 39 STUCK on Kant CPR encoding). Aristotle is the corpus's most-extracted author by a wide margin; Plato second at 9 (23%). The political-animal claim is one of the most-cited single sentences in Western political philosophy. **Third corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle Politics 001 (humans naturally political) vs Hobbes 001 (state-of-nature war). The substantive political-society foundations cluster now includes Republic 001 (Thrasymachus), Hobbes 001 (WARRE), Politics 001 (political animal) — three distinct views of political society's foundations. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the first-Politics / Aristotle-at-ten-works / third-opposition-pair / political-society-cluster milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The two claims have A-form structure: 'All cities are natural productions' and 'All men are naturally political animals.' Term logic could carry the pair as two A-propositions, and Aristotle's *Politics* is in part a defense of these as universal categorical truths about human and political nature. The fol encoding preserves the conjunction; aristotelian would carry the categorical character but flatten the structural relation between the two claims (city as natural + man as political-animal, both from the same teleological argument). Roughly co-equal score; fol chosen for tighter encoding of the universal-conditional structure.
assessments.distortionRisk: Encoding as two A-form propositions would preserve term-logic character but lose the conjunction-as-single-claim structure that the source presents.

assessments.reason: The claim is the *conclusion* of a multi-premise teleological argument (preceding lines 580-592): nature does nothing in vain; what every being is in its perfect state is its nature; the perfect state of human association is the city; therefore the city is a natural production and man is naturally a political animal. An nd extraction of the wider teleological argument would carry the inferential structure; this extraction encodes only the conclusion.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Politics*, Book I Chapter 2 (Bekker 1253a). William Ellis 1776 translation (reissued 1912 J. M. Dent Everyman). PG #6762. The Ellis translation is older than Jowett 1885, but Jowett's *Politics* is not on PG; Ellis is what's available.

**The dual claim**: Aristotle in this passage states both (1) 'the city is a natural production' and (2) 'man is naturally a political animal' (ζῷον πολιτικόν / zōon politikon). The two are part of the same teleological argument and are conjoined in the source text by 'and that.' The encoding preserves both as conjoined universal-conditionals.

**Predicate naming**: `City(x)`, `NaturalProduction(x)`, `Human(x)`, `NaturallyPoliticalAnimal(x)`. The Greek 'zōon politikon' is rendered by Ellis as 'political animal'; modern translations (Reeve, Lord) also use 'political animal' or 'animal that lives in a polis.' The 'naturally' qualifier (φύσει / phusei) is essential — Aristotle's claim is that being-political is part of human *nature*, not a convention. Encoded as part of the predicate name (NaturallyPoliticalAnimal); a richer encoding could use a modal/dispositional operator.

**The teleological argument** preceding the conclusion at lines 580-592 establishes the conclusion: nature does nothing in vain (588-589, 599-600); what every being is in its perfect state is its nature (588); a complete government is the perfect state / final cause of human association (591); therefore the city is a natural production and man is naturally a political animal. A future companion extraction in nd could carry this 5-premise teleological derivation; the current extraction encodes only the conclusion as Aristotle states it.

**The forward-direction reading**: encoded as ∀x. Human(x) → NaturallyPoliticalAnimal(x), not as a biconditional. Aristotle in the immediately-following text (lines 594-598) qualifies that 'whosoever is naturally and not accidentally unfit for society, must be either inferior or superior to man' — i.e., some entities lack the political-animal character but ARE NOT humans (they are 'inferior or superior'). The biconditional reading would say political-animal-ness ⊃ humanity; this is roughly what Aristotle accepts (only humans, of natural beings, are political animals in the relevant sense), but the forward direction is the load-bearing claim Aristotle states explicitly at 593.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics 001 (every art aims at some good) and 002 (function argument)**: the NE's function argument concludes that the good of man is virtuous activity-of-soul-according-to-reason. The Politics's political-animal claim is the social-political analog: man's complete actualization is in the city / political community, not as solitary individual. The two together form Aristotle's broader anthropology: man's nature is rational-and-political; man's good is virtuous-rational-political activity. **Eighteenth corpus conceptual link** between extractions (cumulative count across iterations).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hobbes 001 (WARRE definition)**: Hobbes 001 encodes the social-contract state-of-nature claim (war = known disposition to contend by battle). Aristotle's political-animal claim is the **explicit conceptual opposition** to the Hobbesian state-of-nature picture. Aristotle: humans are naturally political (city precedes individual; humans are unfit for solitary life). Hobbes: humans are naturally in war-of-all-against-all in the absence of common power; political society is a contractual construction. The corpus now has both poles of one of the most-debated questions in political philosophy. **Third corpus conceptual opposition pair** (after Aristotle PNC vs Hegel dialectical-truth iter 33; Leibniz monadological-pluralism vs Hegel holistic-truth iter 34).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Republic 001 (Thrasymachus's challenge)**: Republic 001 has Thrasymachus argue justice is the interest of the stronger — a deflationary / power-based view of political society. Aristotle's natural-political-animal claim is the teleological / essential-nature alternative. Three corpus extractions now on political-society foundations: Republic 001 (Thrasymachus), Hobbes 001 (state-of-nature), Aristotle Politics 001 (this). Substantive thematic cluster forming.

**Reception**: 'man is by nature a political animal' is one of the most-cited single sentences in Western political philosophy. The medieval scholastic tradition (Aquinas, Ockham), the early-modern republican tradition (Machiavelli, Harrington), and modern communitarianism (MacIntyre, Sandel) all engage with the Aristotelian claim. Hannah Arendt's *The Human Condition* (1958) is the canonical 20th-century reconstruction.

**Translation choices**: Ellis's 'political animal' is standard for zōon politikon. 'Natural production' for the city is more idiosyncratic — modern translations (Reeve) prefer 'by nature' or 'natural.' The claim is that the city is among the things that exist by nature (φύσει), not by convention (νόμῳ).

sourceExcerpt:
There is nothing that more characterises a complete
citizen than having a share in the judicial and executive part of the
government.
intent: Capture Aristotle's foundational definition of the complete citizen from Politics III.1 (lines 2736-2738, ~Bekker 1275a): a complete citizen is one who has a share in the judicial and executive part of government. This is the **canonical Aristotelian participation-based definition of citizenship** — citizenship is constituted by *political participation* (deliberative and judicial roles), not by birth, residence, legal status, or property. **Second Politics extraction**; **same-work different-span pair** with Politics 001 (Book I.2 man-as-political-animal). The pair captures Aristotle's political philosophy's *constitutive* claims: 001 establishes humans-as-naturally-political (anthropological); 002 establishes citizens-as-participants (institutional). **Aristotle now at 16 extractions across 10 works** — most-extracted author at 30% of corpus's 55 extractions.
extractorNote: **Second Politics extraction** — Aristotle now at **16 extractions across 10 works**: Categories ×1, De Interpretatione ×4, Prior Analytics ×1, Posterior Analytics ×1, Topics ×1, Sophistical Refutations ×1, Metaphysics ×2, NE ×2, De Anima ×2, Politics ×2. Aristotle now at 16 of 55 = **29% of corpus** — remains the most-extracted author by a substantial margin. **Tenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**: Met 001/002 + Hume 001/002 + De Anima 001/002 + Locke 001/002 + Hegel 001/002 + Wittgenstein 001/002 + Russell 001/002 + Nietzsche 001/002 + Berkeley 001/002 + Politics 001/002 (this). Pattern now at ten instances — substantially the most-recurrent corpus structural feature, exceeding 9 of 10 multi-extraction works in the corpus. **Third corpus 2-vs-2 conceptual opposition**: Aristotle Politics 001/002 (naturalism + participation-citizenship) vs Hobbes Leviathan 001/002 (state-of-nature contractualism). Three 2-vs-2 oppositions now in corpus (joining Spinoza-vs-Leibniz substance, Locke-vs-Berkeley empiricism). **Aristotle-vs-Plato-Republic opposition now at 3 extractions on one side**: Politics 001 + Politics 002 oppose Republic 001 (Thrasymachus deflationary view). **Political-philosophy cluster now at 10 extractions across 4 authors**: the corpus's deepest substantive cluster. **Aristotelian ethical-political framework**: NE 001 + NE 002 + Politics 001 + Politics 002 = 4-extraction within-author across-work coherence (ethics + politics integrated per Aristotle's own conception). Thirty-first fol extraction; fol now at 31 of 55 = 56%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the tenth-same-work-pair + third-2-vs-2-opposition + political-philosophy-cluster-at-ten-extractions + Aristotelian-ethical-political-four-extraction-coherence + Aristotle-at-16-extractions-29%-of-corpus milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The biconditional has convertible-A shape ('All complete citizens are sharers in judicial-and-executive government; all sharers in judicial-and-executive government are complete citizens'). Both predicates are unary; term logic could carry it cleanly. fol is preferred only marginally — preserves the biconditional structure directly; aristotelian could express the same content via convertible-A pair.

Book III Ch. I develops the citizenship definition by eliminating insufficient criteria (residence, legal privileges, hereditary status); the definition emerges as the surviving sufficient-and-necessary criterion. A nd extraction could carry the elimination-based inferential structure with the definition as conclusion. The fol extraction captures the definition itself; nd would be the supporting reductive argument.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Politics*, Book III, Chapter I (Bekker ~1275a17-23). Ellis 1776 translation, PG #6762.

**Predicate naming**: `CompleteCitizen(p)` for the Aristotelian *polites* in its strict/complete sense (vs honorary citizens, sojourners, slaves, boys, old men past military service — all of whom are *incomplete* citizens or non-citizens); `HasShareInJudicialAndExecutiveGovernment(p)` for the constitutive participatory criterion. The Greek technical term is *metoche en archē* (having a share in office/rule); 'judicial' = *krisis* (judgment-roles, especially as juror); 'executive' = the deliberative-and-administrative roles (member of assembly, magistrate).

**The biconditional encoding**: captures the *definitional* status of the participatory criterion. Aristotle explicitly eliminates alternative criteria (residence — slaves and sojourners reside but are not citizens; legal privileges — extended to foreigners by treaty; hereditary status — sons of citizens not yet adults are incomplete citizens). The remaining surviving criterion is participation in judicial and executive functions — *and only this* characterizes the complete citizen. The biconditional is strict because both directions hold: every participant is (in this respect) a citizen; every citizen (completely so) participates.

**The constitutional-relativity caveat** (line 2715-2716): Aristotle himself notes that 'that man who would be a citizen in a republic would very often not be one in an oligarchy.' Citizenship is constitution-relative: different *politeiai* (constitutional forms) define participation differently. The encoded definition captures the *general* participatory criterion; the constitution-specific extension of who counts as a participant varies. This caveat is preserved in reviewer notes; the AST predicate captures the cross-constitutional general definition.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Politics 001 (within-work same-author same-formalism pair)**: Politics 001 (Book I.2, line 593) extracted the *anthropological* claim — humans are naturally political animals; the city is a natural production. Politics 002 (this, Book III.1, line 2736) extracts the *institutional* claim — citizens are constituted by political participation. The two extractions are complementary Aristotelian moves: 001 = humans' natural political-animal-character; 002 = citizens' constitutive participation-in-government. Together they capture Aristotle's political philosophy's two foundational claims: *that* political society is natural (001) and *what* political membership consists in (002). **Tenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Republic 001 (substantive opposition extended)**: Republic 001 (Thrasymachus 'justice is the interest of the stronger') presents the *deflationary* view of political authority — political power is just dominance. Aristotle Politics 002's participation-based definition is *constructive* — political authority is constituted by shared deliberative-judicial participation, not dominance. **The Aristotle-vs-Plato Republic opposition is now at three corpus extractions**: Politics 001 (Aristotle naturalism) + Politics 002 (Aristotle participation-definition) opposing Republic 001 (Thrasymachus deflationary view). The Aristotle-vs-Thrasymachus political-philosophy contrast is the strongest multi-extraction internal contrast in the political-philosophy cluster.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Rousseau Social Contract 001 (substantive partial-alliance / partial-tension)**: Rousseau's *convention-only* legitimacy framework rejects natural and force-based authority. Aristotle's participation-based citizenship is a *form* of convention (constitutional politeia defines who participates); but Aristotle grounds political society in *nature* (Politics 001 — humans naturally political), whereas Rousseau insists no natural authority obtains. Substantive partial-alliance on participation-as-constitutive-of-political-membership; substantive opposition on whether political society itself is natural or conventional.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hobbes Leviathan 001/002 (substantive opposition extended)**: Hobbes's contractualist account treats political society as artificial (state-of-nature pre-political; sovereignty constituted by covenant). Aristotle Politics 001 + 002 treats political society as natural and citizenship as participatory. **The Aristotle-Politics-vs-Hobbes opposition** (logged at iter 40) is now at multi-extraction depth: Politics 001 + Politics 002 (this) opposes Hobbes 001 + Hobbes 002. **Third corpus 2-vs-2 conceptual opposition** (joining Spinoza-vs-Leibniz substance-monism-vs-pluralism, Locke-vs-Berkeley empiricist-realism-vs-idealism).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Statesman 001 (substantive opposition extended)**: Statesman 001 identifies statesman with householder/master/king (all one science). Aristotle Politics I.2 (Politics 001) explicitly rejects this synonymy; Politics III.1 (this, 002) further distinguishes citizen-as-participant from household-member (slaves and women are not complete citizens in Aristotle's Greek polis, but their household-membership role is distinct from citizen-role). Aristotle's anti-Platonic move on household-vs-political-society is now substantively documented across both Politics extractions.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 001/002 (within-author across-work coherence)**: NE 001 (arts/sciences/actions aim at some good) + NE 002 (function-argument: good-of-man = working-of-soul-with-reason) + Politics 001 (humans naturally political) + Politics 002 (citizenship as participation) form a coherent Aristotelian ethical-political framework. The good-of-man (NE 002) is realized in the activities-of-soul-with-reason; political participation (Politics 002) is one *form* of such activity (the practical-political form, vs the contemplative-philosophical form of NE Book X). Substantive within-author cross-work coherence; four-extraction Aristotelian ethical-political cluster.

**Political-philosophy cluster substantially deepened**: Plato (Republic 001, Statesman 001) + Aristotle (Politics 001, Politics 002 — this) + Hobbes (Leviathan 001, Leviathan 002) + Rousseau (Social Contract 001) = **10 extractions across 4 authors** with multiple cross-extraction oppositions and one alliance. The political-philosophy cluster is the corpus's deepest substantive cluster. With Politics 002, Aristotle and Hobbes now both have two political-philosophy extractions; the multi-extraction-on-both-sides structure makes the Aristotle-Hobbes opposition a third 2-vs-2 corpus opposition.

**Reception**: Aristotle's participation-based citizenship definition is the foundational text of Western republican / civic-republican political theory. Cicero's *De Re Publica*, Rousseau's *Social Contract* (which inherits the participation criterion while rejecting the natural-society framework), Hannah Arendt's *The Human Condition* (1958) explicitly draws on the Aristotelian participation framework. Contemporary civic-republicanism (Skinner, Pettit) traces directly to this definition.

**Translation choices**: Ellis 1776 'judicial and executive part of the government' for *metoche en archē kritikē kai bouleutikē* is faithful; modern translations (Reeve, Lord) use 'deliberative and judicial office' or 'judicial and deliberative function'. The Greek technical distinction is *krisis* (judgment, judicial) and *bouleusis* (deliberation, executive-deliberative-administrative).

attributions.sourceText:
There is nothing that more characterises a complete
citizen than having a share in the judicial and executive part of the
government.

sourceExcerpt: WE think that we know each thing singly, (and not in a sophistical manner, according to accident,) when we think that we know the cause on account of which a thing is, that it is the cause of that thing, and that the latter cannot subsist otherwise
intent: Capture Aristotle's foundational definition of scientific knowledge (epistēmē) from Posterior Analytics I.2 (line 299): we have scientific knowledge of a thing when we know its cause, that the cause is the cause, and that the thing cannot be otherwise. **First Posterior Analytics extraction** — fourth Aristotle work for the corpus (after Categories, Prior Analytics, NE × 2, De Interpretatione × 4, Metaphysics × 2 = nine Aristotle extractions across six works). The definition is one of the most-cited single passages in Aristotelian epistemology and underwrites Aristotle's broader theory of demonstrative science.
extractorNote: **First Posterior Analytics extraction** — the corpus now has Aristotle across **six works** (Categories, Prior Analytics, Posterior Analytics, NE, De Interpretatione, Metaphysics) with **ten extractions overall** (Categories 001, Prior Analytics 001, NE 001+002, De Int 001+002+003+004, Metaphysics 001+002, now Post. An. 001). Aristotle is now extracted across substantially the full Organon-plus-Metaphysics-plus-Ethics core. The Posterior Analytics extraction adds the *epistemological* dimension to the Aristotle logical-foundations cluster — Met 001 (PNC), Met 002 (LEM), De Int 002/003/004 (sea-battle as bivalence-fails-for-future), Categories 001 / Prior Analytics 001 (syllogistic), and now Post. An. 001 (scientific knowledge as cause-known-plus-necessity). Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes 002 (epistemic cogito) — both characterize knowledge as grasp of necessity; **fifth conceptual lineage in the corpus**. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; milestone (first Posterior Analytics, Aristotle at six works) goes in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: Aristotle's definition of scientific knowledge (epistēmē) is paradigmatically epistemic — it characterizes what *knowing* a thing requires. An epistemic encoding using K_I (the knower knows that p) would surface the agent-indexing directly. The fol encoding flattens this to a `ScientificKnowledge(I, p)` predicate; the epistemic encoding would unpack it as K_I(Cause(c, p) ∧ Necessary(p)). Not chosen as primary because the load-bearing structure is the *definitional biconditional* (scientific-knowledge IFF cause-known AND necessary-truth), which fol carries directly. The epistemic encoding would make the K_I explicit but the biconditional remains the core.
assessments.distortionRisk: An epistemic-only encoding would lose the explicit biconditional structure that frames Aristotle's definition; the encoding here keeps the biconditional but encodes the knowledge-modal as a fol predicate.

assessments.reason: Aristotle's definition is *stated* in this passage, not derived. The wider Posterior Analytics develops the consequences (demonstrative science, the structure of demonstration) — those would be nd-shaped arguments.

Not a categorical proposition or syllogism. The definitional biconditional binds three predicates (ScientificKnowledge, KnowsCauseOf, CannotBeOtherwise) and term logic cannot represent the conjunctive consequent.
Not dialogical. Treatise definition.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Posterior Analytics*, Book I, Chapter 2 (Bekker ~71b9-12). Owen 1853 translation from Bohn's Classical Library. Wikisource transcription. Same translator as Posterior Analytics's broader Organon collection from this corpus's iter-23 commitments.

**The three-component definition**: Aristotle requires three things for scientific knowledge: (1) knowing the cause on account of which the thing is; (2) knowing that the cause IS the cause of that thing (and not some other cause); (3) knowing that the thing cannot subsist otherwise. The encoded formula uses three predicates: `KnowsCauseOf(I, p)` (component 1), `KnowsThatCauseIsCauseOf(I, p)` (component 2), `CannotBeOtherwise(p)` (component 3). The conjunction of all three is the biconditional consequent.

**Predicate naming**: 'I' as the knower (consistent with Descartes 001/002 const 'I' and Kant Groundwork 001 const 'I'). Aristotle's framing is more impersonal ('we think that we know'); the first-person reading is a charitable convention. A more impersonal encoding would use ∀x. (Knower(x) ∧ ScientificKnowledge(x, p)) but loses the indexical character.

**'Cannot subsist otherwise'** is encoded as the unary predicate `CannotBeOtherwise(p)` rather than as modal □p. The corpus convention from Hobbes 001 (temporal-disposition flattened to unary) and De Int 001 (πεφυκός-dispositional flattened to unary) is to flatten modal/dispositional content to bare predicates when the surrounding formula doesn't require modal operators. A future modal extraction (kripke/intuitionistic) of this passage could surface the necessity-claim as □ explicitly.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Metaphysics 001 (PNC) and 002 (LEM)**: Posterior Analytics 001's 'cannot subsist otherwise' clause is the modal-necessity component of scientific knowledge; PNC and LEM provide the logical foundation that makes 'cannot be otherwise' meaningful. The Posterior Analytics extraction integrates with the Aristotle logical-foundations cluster (Met 001 PNC, Met 002 LEM, De Int 002/003/004 sea-battle, Categories 001 Barbara, Prior Analytics 001 Celarent) and adds the *epistemological* dimension to what was previously a purely-logical cluster.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes 002 (epistemic cogito)**: both extractions characterize what *knowing* requires. Aristotle: knowledge = grasp of cause + grasp of necessity. Descartes: knowledge = indubitable / impossible-to-be-otherwise. Both make modal-necessity central to knowledge. The Aristotle 'cannot subsist otherwise' clause and the Cartesian 'necessarily true each time expressed' parallel each other historically — Descartes's cogito-as-necessarily-true draws on the Aristotelian epistēmē tradition (necessity as a knowledge-condition). **Fifth conceptual lineage in the corpus**: Aristotle epistēmē → Cartesian cogito (knowledge as grasp of necessity).

**Aristotle's broader theory of demonstration**: the rest of Posterior Analytics I.2 develops the conditions on premises for demonstrative science (true, immediate, first, prior, more known, causes of conclusion). These six conditions are the standard 'principles of demonstration' Aristotle elaborates in I.3-12. A future extraction of I.2's full demonstration-conditions list would be a substantial nd-shaped extraction (Aristotle giving the conditions as enumerated requirements).

**Translation choices**: Owen 1853 'scientific knowledge' renders Aristotle's ἐπιστήμη (epistēmē). The modern Barnes (1975) and Tredennick (1960) translations use 'knowledge' or 'understanding' or 'scientific knowledge' similarly. 'Cause' renders αἰτία (aitia), Aristotle's general term for explanatory factor (broader than modern 'cause' — covers material, formal, efficient, final causes per the four-causes doctrine). The encoded `KnowsCauseOf(I, p)` is the simplest encoding; a richer extraction could parameterize the cause-type (material vs formal vs efficient vs final).

**Reception**: Aristotle's epistēmē-as-cause-known definition is the foundational text of Western philosophy of science. Aquinas's commentary, the medieval scholastic tradition, and modern philosophy of science (Hempel's deductive-nomological model, contemporary scientific-explanation debates) all engage with the Aristotelian framework. The encoded definition captures the bare claim; the rich reception is reviewer-notes context.
