sourceExcerpt: this is the nature of an individual substance or of a complete being, namely, to afford a conception so complete that the concept shall be sufficient for the understanding of it and for the deduction of all the predicates of which the substance is or may become the subject
intent: Capture Leibniz's foundational complete-concept doctrine from Discourse on Metaphysics §VIII (line 40): an individual substance is a being whose concept is so complete that every predicate that can be truly predicated of it (past, present, future) is deducible from its concept. This is the in-esse principle in its definitional form — the predicate-in-subject doctrine that underwrites Leibnizian substance metaphysics, the windowless-monad doctrine, the pre-established harmony, and the famous Alexander-the-Great example that follows in the same paragraph. **Second Leibniz extraction**; **first Discourse on Metaphysics extraction**; **cross-work conceptual pair with Monadology 001** (this defines individual substance via completeness-of-concept; Monadology 001 defines monad via simplicity-without-parts — two complementary entries into Leibnizian substance metaphysics, from his two most-cited works).
extractorNote: **Second Leibniz extraction** — first Discourse on Metaphysics extraction; second Leibniz work. Leibniz now at two extractions across two works (Monadology 001 + Discourse §VIII). **Cross-work conceptual pair**: Discourse 001 (individual substance via completeness-of-concept) + Monadology 001 (Monad via simplicity-without-parts) — same author, two different works, complementary definitions of the *same fundamental metaphysical object* via two distinct routes (logical concept-containment vs metaphysical mereological simplicity). This is a **new pair-type variant** in the corpus: cross-work conceptual pair (distinct from same-span pairs like slave-boy 002/003, same-work-different-formalism pairs like Hume 001/002, instance-and-typology pairs like Nyāya 001/002, cross-school-typology pairs like Nyāya 002 + Dasgupta 001, argument-for-principle pairs like Hume 001/002, conceptual lineage chains, and conceptual oppositions). The corpus now has substantial cross-extraction structural variety. Rationalist tradition now substantially anchored: Descartes (001/002), Spinoza (001), Leibniz (Monadology 001 + Discourse 001) = four-extraction strand across three core rationalist authors. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the cross-work conceptual pair pattern is novel enough to note in SESSION_LOG but does not require a log.md entry (single-instance pattern observation, not a schema-gap recurrence).

assessments.reason: The §VIII body has explicit argumentative structure leading to the substance definition: in-esse principle (every true predicate is virtually contained in the subject) → therefore content of subject must include content of predicate → therefore individual substance is one whose concept is complete. A nd extraction would carry that three-step move with the definition as conclusion. The fol extraction carries the definition itself as the load-bearing content; the nd extraction would carry the supporting in-esse argument. Both layers are present; fol picked because the climactic sentence is a single biconditional definition, not a numbered-premises chain.

The biconditional 'individual substance iff every predicate is contained in its concept' has categorical shape but the inner ∀p quantification over predicates and the binary CanBeTruePredicateOf relation are not expressible in unary term logic.
Not dialogical. Discursive treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Discourse on Metaphysics* (1686, published posthumously), §VIII. Montgomery 1908 English translation from *Leibniz: Discourse on Metaphysics, Correspondence with Arnauld, and Monadology* (Open Court Publishing Co.). PD pre-1929. Wikisource transcription via Wikisource parse API (per manifest.yaml).

**The in-esse principle** (Latin: 'in-esse', 'being-in') is Leibniz's technical term for the predicate-in-subject doctrine: every true predication is grounded in the subject's nature, such that if you understand the concept of the subject perfectly, you can deduce every predicate that will ever belong to it. The complete-concept definition of individual substance is the in-esse principle applied to substances specifically.

**Predicate naming**: `IndividualSubstance(s)` for the definiendum. `CanBeTruePredicateOf(p, s)` captures 'predicates of which the substance is or may become the subject' — the modal 'may become' is encoded as the 'Can' prefix, capturing past + present + future + counterfactual predicates. `DeducibleFromConceptOf(p, s)` captures the conclusion 'the concept shall be sufficient for...the deduction of all the predicates.' The Leibnizian original French is 'estre suffisant pour comprendre, et pour en faire deduire'; Latin is 'sufficiens ad omnia praedicata deducenda'.

**The encoding as ∀-∀ biconditional**: the outer ∀s quantifies over potential substances; the inner ∀p quantifies over potential predicates. The biconditional makes the definition strict — *only* those substances whose every-possible-predicate is deducible-from-concept qualify as individual substances. Modes / accidents / abstractions (like 'king-ness' applied to Alexander) fail this test — they don't carry the full hæcceity.

**The Alexander-the-Great example** (immediately following in line 40) is Leibniz's worked instance: God, seeing the complete concept of Alexander, sees a priori that Alexander will conquer Darius and Porus — even whether he will die by poison. This is the strongest form of the doctrine: individual concepts contain not only essential properties but every accident, every contingent fact. Future extraction candidate as a `nd` argument (in-esse principle + completeness → a priori derivability of all events).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Monadology 001 (cross-work conceptual pair)**: Discourse §VIII defines individual substance via *completeness of concept* (the predicate-in-subject doctrine); Monadology §1 defines monad via *simplicity without parts* (the no-divisible-parts doctrine). The two are Leibniz's complementary entries into substance metaphysics — the Discourse approaches substance via *logic* (concept-containment of predicates), the Monadology approaches substance via *metaphysics* (simplicity of parts). The same metaphysical object (the individual substance / monad) is defined twice via two distinct routes. This is a **cross-work conceptual pair** — a new pair pattern variant in the corpus: same author, two different works, complementary definitions of the same fundamental concept.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Categories 001 (primary substance)**: Aristotle distinguishes primary substance (the individual) from secondary substance (species/genus). Leibnizian individual substance inherits the Aristotelian primary-substance role (the irreducible individual) while sharpening its identity criterion: for Aristotle, primary substance is defined by being neither said-of nor in a subject; for Leibniz, it is defined by having a complete concept. The Leibniz refinement is the addition of the predicate-in-subject criterion as the identity condition of the individual.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza 001 (substance and causation)**: Spinoza's Prop. III defines causation via what substances have in common; Leibniz's complete-concept doctrine implies that no two substances can be exactly alike (PII — Principle of the Identity of Indiscernibles), since each has a unique complete concept. The two rationalist substance-metaphysics are complementary but conflicting: Spinoza one-substance monism vs Leibnizian monadological pluralism, each derived from a distinct first principle of substance.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes 001/002 (cogito as substantial self-awareness)**: Descartes's cogito establishes the existence of the thinking I; Leibniz's complete-concept doctrine specifies what an individual substance *is*, of which the Cartesian I is one instance. The rationalist triad (Descartes 001/002 + Spinoza 001 + Leibniz monadology 001 + this) now has four extractions covering the foundational moves: skeptical-foundation, geometric-axiomatic-deduction, monadological-substance, and complete-concept-substance.

**The contingency tension**: Leibniz §XIII (line 60 in source.txt) addresses the worry that the complete-concept doctrine entails necessitarianism — if Alexander's concept already contains his conquest of Darius, isn't his conquest necessary? Leibniz's response is the contingency-via-infinite-analysis doctrine: contingent truths require infinite analysis (only God can perform), so they are *certain* but not *necessary*. This is one of Leibniz's most distinctive moves and would be a future kripke / temporal extraction.

**Reception**: the complete-concept doctrine is the single most-discussed Leibnizian thesis in 20th-century analytic philosophy of substance. Russell's *Critical Exposition of the Philosophy of Leibniz* (1900) argued that the doctrine entails Leibniz's monadology, his pre-established harmony, his theory of relations — basically all his distinctive metaphysics. Mates's *The Philosophy of Leibniz* (1986) and Adams's *Leibniz: Determinist, Theist, Idealist* (1994) are the canonical late-20th-century treatments. Plantinga's modal-realist response to Leibniz (transworld identity via essences) is a direct 20th-century descendant.

**Translation choices**: 'individual substance' for Leibniz's 'substance individuelle' (French) / 'substantia individua' (Latin) is standard. 'In-esse' (Latin) is preserved untranslated in Montgomery as the philosophical technical term; some translations render as 'in-being' or 'inherence'. Both preserve the in-esse principle's reference to predicates inhering in subjects.

attributions.sourceText: this is the nature of an individual substance or of a complete being, namely, to afford a conception so complete that the concept shall be sufficient for the understanding of it and for the deduction of all the predicates of which the substance is or may become the subject

sourceExcerpt: a particular substance never acts upon another particular substance nor is it acted upon by it
intent: Capture Leibniz's canonical pre-established harmony claim from Discourse on Metaphysics §XIV (line 66): a particular substance never acts upon another particular substance nor is it acted upon by it. This is the **foundational claim of Leibnizian pre-established harmony / windowless monad doctrine** — substances are causally isolated; their apparent interaction is the result of God's pre-arranged correspondence of their internal states. **Second Discourse extraction**; **Leibniz now at 3 extractions across 2 works**: Monadology 001 (Monad definition) + Discourse 001 (complete-concept individual substance) + Discourse 002 (no-substance-interaction — this). Discourse 001/002 is a **same-work different-span pair** capturing two foundational claims of Leibnizian substance metaphysics.
extractorNote: **Second Discourse extraction** — Leibniz now at **3 extractions across 2 works**: Monadology 001 (Monad simplicity) + Discourse 001 (complete-concept substance) + Discourse 002 (no-substance-interaction / pre-established harmony — this). The Leibniz strand now has substantive depth: three foundational substance claims (mereological + logical + causal). **Fourteenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**: Met 001/002, Hume Treatise 001/002, De Anima 001/002, Locke 001/002, Hegel 001/002, Wittgenstein 001/002, Russell 001/002, Nietzsche 001/002, Berkeley 001/002, Politics 001/002, Republic 001/002, Groundwork 001/002, Prolegomena 001/002, Discourse 001/002 (this). Pattern now at fourteen instances. **Thirty-fifth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Leibniz Discourse 002 (pre-established harmony — no direct inter-substance causation) vs Hume Treatise 002 (causation as constant conjunction observed empirically). Thirty-five conceptual oppositions now in corpus. **Leibniz substance-metaphysics strand now substantively coherent**: Monadology 001 (mereological — simplicity of monad) + Discourse 001 (logical — complete concept) + Discourse 002 (causal — no inter-substance interaction). The three Leibniz extractions form a coherent multi-aspect Leibnizian substance theory. Forty-first fol extraction; fol now at 41 of 67 = 61%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the second-Discourse + Leibniz-at-3-extractions-2-works + fourteenth-same-work-pair + thirty-fifth-opposition + Leibniz-substance-three-aspect-coherence + fol-at-61%-stable milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: §XIV develops the pre-established harmony argument: substances express the whole universe in their own manner; their phenomena intercorrespond via God's pre-arrangement; therefore direct inter-substance interaction is unnecessary and indeed impossible. A nd extraction could carry the explanatory inferential chain. The fol extraction captures the no-direct-interaction claim itself; nd would be the supporting harmony-explanation argument.
Binary `DirectlyActsUpon(x, y)` relation and equality (¬(x=y)) not expressible in unary term logic. Categorical structure cannot carry the relational claim.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Discourse on Metaphysics* (1686, published posthumously), §XIV (line 66 in source.txt). Montgomery 1908 translation. Wikisource via parse API.

**Predicate naming**: `Substance(x)` for the Leibnizian individual substance (the monad); `DirectlyActsUpon(x, y)` for direct causal influence (which the Leibnizian framework denies between substances). The 'directly' qualifier is important: substances *appear* to interact (we perceive other minds, see external objects affect us), but this is the pre-established harmony at work, not direct causation.

**The equality-and-inequality encoding**: ¬(x=y) ensures the claim applies to *distinct* substances; a substance does (trivially) 'act on itself' in the sense that its own internal states unfold causally. The encoded constraint correctly restricts the no-interaction claim to distinct-substance pairs.

**The pre-established harmony framework**: §XIV explains the *positive* counterpart of the no-interaction claim — God has arranged all substances such that their internal states *correspond* (intercorrespond) without direct causal interaction. The metaphor (line 66): 'as when several have agreed to meet together in a certain place on a set day' — they appear to coordinate via direct planning, but actually each follows their own independent path that *happens* to converge by prior arrangement.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Leibniz Monadology 001 (intra-author across-work alignment)**: Leibniz Monadology 001 (Monad as simple substance without parts; the simplicity-without-parts definition) + Leibniz Discourse 001 (complete-concept individual substance) + Leibniz Discourse 002 (no-substance-interaction — this). Three Leibniz extractions across two works form a coherent Leibniz substance-metaphysics strand: 001 (Monadology) = mereological simplicity of substance; Discourse 001 = logical concept-completeness of substance; Discourse 002 (this) = causal isolation of substances. Together they capture Leibniz's three foundational substance claims.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Leibniz Discourse 001 (within-work same-author pair)**: Discourse 001 (line 40, complete-concept individual substance: every predicate true of a substance is contained in its concept) and Discourse 002 (this, line 66, no direct interaction between substances) are *jointly* the foundation of Leibniz's pre-established harmony doctrine. If every substance's predicates are *already* contained in its complete concept (Discourse 001), then its unfolding internal states are *predetermined* by its concept; no external causal influence is *needed*. Discourse 002 makes this explicit: no inter-substance causation actually occurs. **Fourteenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern** (Met 001/002, Hume Treatise 001/002, De Anima 001/002, Locke 001/002, Hegel 001/002, Wittgenstein 001/002, Russell 001/002, Nietzsche 001/002, Berkeley 001/002, Politics 001/002, Republic 001/002, Groundwork 001/002, Prolegomena 001/002, Discourse 001/002 — this).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza Ethics 001 (substantive opposition deepened)**: Spinoza Ethics 001 (Prop. III) — things which have nothing in common cannot be one the cause of the other (no causation without commonality). Leibniz Discourse 002 (this) — no substance can be the direct cause of another substance, period (no inter-substance causation). Both Spinoza and Leibniz restrict inter-substance causation, but for radically different reasons: Spinoza's restriction is the consequence of *one-substance monism* (everything is a mode of the single substance God/Nature, so 'causation between substances' is meaningless); Leibniz's restriction is the consequence of *many-substance pluralism* with pre-established harmony (substances are real and many, but causally isolated by God's design). The Spinoza-Leibniz substance opposition (originally documented as fifth conceptual opposition iter 44) is now substantively deepened — both philosophers restrict substance-interaction but for opposed substance-ontological reasons.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Treatise 002 (substantive opposition — pre-established harmony vs constant-conjunction causation)**: Hume Treatise 002 (inductive argument for Copy Principle via constant conjunction) and Leibniz Discourse 002 (this — no substance-interaction; apparent causation is pre-established harmony) are *fundamentally opposed* on the structure of causal claims. Hume *grounds* causal claims in observed constant conjunction (empiricist, this-worldly causation); Leibniz *denies* the constant-conjunction-cum-direct-causation framework, replacing it with non-causal-correlation-via-divine-pre-arrangement. **Thirty-fifth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Leibniz Discourse 002 (pre-established harmony — no direct inter-substance causation) vs Hume Treatise 002 (causation as constant conjunction observed empirically).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes Meditations 001/002/003 (substantive opposition — interactionist vs harmony)**: Descartes's substance dualism (mind and body as distinct substances) traditionally faces the *mind-body interaction problem* (how does mind affect body and vice versa?). Descartes himself attempts a pineal-gland interactionist solution. Leibniz's pre-established harmony is *one classical response* to the Cartesian interaction problem — there is no real interaction; the appearance is divine-pre-arrangement. **Sixteenth corpus cross-extraction alliance reframed as pair-with-opposition**: Leibniz Discourse 002 (no-substance-interaction via pre-established harmony) responds to and partially-opposes the Cartesian interactionist framework. Substantive conceptual link with complex valence.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Met 003 (substantive opposition — Unmoved Mover causes vs God-orchestrates-pre-established-harmony)**: Aristotle's Unmoved Mover *causes* motion (as final cause / object of desire); Leibniz's God *pre-arranges* the substances' internal states without direct causation. Both Aristotle and Leibniz involve God-as-cosmic-orchestrator, but Aristotle's God is *causally efficacious* while Leibniz's God is *pre-arrangement-orchestrator-not-direct-cause* within the substance-system. Substantive structural parallel-with-difference; not a clean opposition but a substantive conceptual contrast.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Berkeley Three Dialogues 002 (substantive partial-alliance — both reject mind-independent material substance interaction)**: Berkeley's idealism denies mind-independent material substance entirely (everything is in mind); Leibniz's pre-established harmony denies inter-substance direct causation but retains substances. Both Berkeley and Leibniz address the apparent-causal-interaction-of-objects via *non-material-direct-causation* alternatives. Substantive partial-alliance on the rejection of material-causal-interaction as fundamental.

**Reception**: Leibniz's pre-established harmony is the foundational text of monadological metaphysics. Wolffian school (Christian Wolff, Baumgarten) systematizes it. Kant rejects it as 'dogmatic metaphysics' but his transcendental idealism inherits the structural feature of phenomena-as-mind-arranged. Russell's *Critical Exposition of the Philosophy of Leibniz* (1900) treats pre-established harmony as the logical consequence of complete-concept doctrine. Whitehead's process philosophy (1929 *Process and Reality*) revives the monadological framework.

**Translation choices**: Montgomery 1908 'a particular substance never acts upon another particular substance' for Leibniz's *une substance particulière n'agit jamais sur une autre*. The French and Latin (where applicable) make the distinction-particular-vs-general explicit; the encoded predicates (Substance, DirectlyActsUpon) capture the relevant content.

sourceExcerpt: The Monad, of which we shall here speak, is nothing but a simple substance, which enters into compounds. By 'simple' is meant 'without parts.'
intent: Capture Leibniz's foundational definition of the Monad at Monadology §1 (line 1): a Monad is a simple substance that enters into compounds; 'simple' means 'without parts.' **First Leibniz extraction** — completes the **Descartes-Spinoza-Leibniz rationalist triad** in the corpus (Descartes 001/002 cogito as nd/epistemic; Spinoza 001 PROP. III as nd; now Leibniz 001 Monad as fol). The Monadology is one of the most famous works of early-modern metaphysics; the §1 definition anchors the entire 90-section work.
extractorNote: **First Leibniz extraction** — completes the **Descartes-Spinoza-Leibniz rationalist triad**: Descartes 001/002 (cogito as nd/epistemic) + Spinoza 001 (geometric PROP. III as nd) + Leibniz 001 (this, Monad definition as fol). Three canonical modern rationalists with foundational principles all extracted. The modern European rationalist sub-strand now has three authors anchoring it. The Monadology is also one of the shortest major philosophical works (178 lines for a complete 90-section treatise); the §-numbered structure makes per-section extraction very tractable. Cross-extraction conceptual links flagged in reviewer notes: rationalist-triad completion (Descartes/Spinoza/Leibniz), Aristotelian primary-substance ancestor (Categories), Hegelian holistic-truth opposition (Phenomenology). Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the rationalist-triad-completion milestone goes in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The Monad definition could be cast as 'All monads are simple substances that enter into compounds' — A-form. But the binary structure (simple = without-parts; monad = simple-substance-that-enters-into-compounds) involves nested definitions which term logic flattens.

Definitional move, not an inference. The Monadology continues with arguments derived from this definition (§2 argues simple substances must exist; §7 argues monads have no windows); those would be nd-shaped.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *La Monadologie* (1714, published posthumously 1720), §1. Latta 1898 English translation from *The Monadology and Other Philosophical Writings* (Oxford UP). PD pre-1929. Wikisource transcription.

**Encoded as a conjunction of two biconditionals**: (1) the Monad definition (Monad ↔ Simple ∧ Substance ∧ EntersIntoCompounds) and (2) the Simple definition (Simple ↔ WithoutParts). Together they capture the nested-definition structure of §1.

**The 'enters into compounds' clause** is philosophically substantive — Leibniz is distinguishing monads (which compose into compounds) from monads-in-themselves (which couldn't compose). The clause is preserved in the encoding via `EntersIntoCompounds`.

**Predicate naming**: `Monad(x)`, `Simple(x)`, `Substance(x)`, `EntersIntoCompounds(x)`, `WithoutParts(x)`. The Leibnizian technical vocabulary mapped to standard Mixed-Case predicate names. The German/French original 'substance simple' (simple substance) and 'monade' are preserved as `Substance` + `Simple` separately, and `Monad`.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes 001/002 and Spinoza 001**: the rationalist triad is now complete. Descartes (skeptical-foundation), Spinoza (axiomatic-deduction), Leibniz (monadological-substance-metaphysics) — three canonical modern rationalists, all extracted, all with foundational principles encoded. Conceptual lineage / contrast: Descartes posits the cogito as the indubitable starting point; Spinoza derives substance from axioms; Leibniz defines the monad as the simple substance and develops a pluralistic metaphysics. The three together represent the rationalist tradition's range.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Categories 001 (primary substance)**: Aristotle distinguishes primary substance (the individual) from secondary substance (the species/genus). Leibniz's monads are arguably his successor concept to Aristotelian primary substance — simple, indivisible, real individuals. The Leibnizian monad inherits Aristotelian substance ontology while adding the requirements of simplicity-without-parts (against Aristotelian matter-form composition).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hegel 001 (truth-is-the-whole)**: Hegel's holistic truth-as-whole is in some tension with Leibnizian monadological pluralism — Hegel emphasizes the developmental whole; Leibniz emphasizes the irreducible monad-individuals. The corpus now has a Leibniz-Hegel conceptual opposition (alongside the Aristotle-PNC-vs-Hegel opposition from iter 33).

**Reception**: the Monadology is the foundational text of Leibnizian metaphysics. The Wolffian school (Christian Wolff) systematizes Leibniz's monadology; Kant rejects it as dogmatic metaphysics in the Critique; Russell's *Critical Exposition of the Philosophy of Leibniz* (1900) is the canonical 20th-century reconstruction; contemporary metaphysics (Lewis, contemporary monadology revival) still engages with Leibnizian themes.

**Translation choices**: Latta 1898 'simple substance' for 'substance simple' is standard. The German Leibniz scholarship sometimes uses 'einfache Substanz'; both render the French and the original Latin 'substantia simplex'.
**No source artifacts**: the Wikisource transcription is clean; no OCR or formatting artifacts to flag.

attributions.sourceText: The Monad, of which we shall here speak, is nothing but a simple substance, which enters into compounds. By 'simple' is meant 'without parts.'

sourceExcerpt:
Let us then suppose the mind to be, as we say, white paper, void of all
characters, without any ideas:—How comes it to be furnished? Whence
comes it by that vast store which the busy and boundless fancy of man
has painted on it with an almost endless variety? Whence has it all the
MATERIALS of reason and knowledge? To this I answer, in one word, from
EXPERIENCE.
intent: Capture Locke's empiricist foundation from *Essay* Book II Chapter I §2 (lines 3010-3023): all ideas come from experience, specifically from sensation (perception of external objects) or reflection (perception of the mind's own operations). The corpus's first Locke extraction; **second empiricist extraction** after Hume 001 (the Copy Principle); completes the **British empiricist triad** in the corpus (Locke 1689 → Berkeley 1713 → Hume 1739, all now extracted; Berkeley is on the idealist sub-strand and isn't strictly empiricist in the same sense but is part of the historical conversation).
extractorNote: Corpus's **first Locke extraction** and **second empiricist extraction** after Hume 001 (Copy Principle). The **British empiricist triad** is now fully anchored: Locke 1689 (Essay) + Berkeley 1713 (Three Dialogues — idealist offshoot) + Hume 1739 (Treatise). The Locke → Hume conceptual lineage (general empiricist principle → sharper Copy Principle) gives the corpus a **second two-extraction conceptual lineage** alongside the Berkeley → Russell historical-influence pair flagged in iter 18. The encoded formula uses disjunctive Sensation(e) ∨ Reflection(e) rather than a flat Experience(e) predicate to preserve Locke's two-fold characterization. Cross-section dependency on §3-§4 (Sensation/Reflection definitions) is within-chapter; the Book I anti-innatism critique (Locke's argument *for* the empiricist principle) is held as a candidate large-scope nd companion. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria — milestone (first Locke, completes British empiricist triad, second two-extraction lineage) goes in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: Locke's empiricist principle is *stated* in Book II Ch I §2, not derived from premises within this passage. Locke's argument *for* the principle is the negative critique of innate ideas in Book I (lines 628-2981 of source.txt, ~2400 lines), too large for an nd extraction. The principle itself is foundational/stipulative within Book II's exposition.
assessments.distortionRisk: An nd extraction would either fold in the Book I anti-innatism argument (out of scope size-wise) or be empty-premise (degenerate).

assessments.reason: Could be cast as 'All ideas are derived-from-experience things' — A-form. But 'derived-from-experience' hides a binary relation and an existential ('there exists an experience from which i is derived'); term logic flattens both.
Locke poses rhetorical questions ('How comes it to be furnished? Whence...') but they are not part of a multi-speaker exchange. Treatise prose with rhetorical-Q-and-A flourish.

reviewerNotes: Source: *An Essay Concerning Human Understanding* (1689), Book II ('Of Ideas'), Chapter I ('Of Ideas in general, and their Original'), Section 2 ('All Ideas come from Sensation or Reflection'). PG #10615 + #10616 (concatenated); English original. The section-numbering scheme is Locke's; section titles appear inline in source.txt as numbered headers (e.g. line 3010: '2. All Ideas come from Sensation or Reflection.').

**Predicate naming**: `Idea(i)` is Locke's general term for any mental content; `Sensation(e)` and `Reflection(e)` are the two technical sources of experience; `DerivedFrom(i, e)` is the binary relation. The encoding uses Sensation ∨ Reflection as the disjunctive characterization of Experience (per §3 and §4 of the same chapter, which define Sensation and Reflection as the two fountains). A flatter encoding using a single `Experience(e)` predicate would lose Locke's two-fold distinction; the disjunctive encoding preserves it.

**The white-paper / tabula rasa metaphor** ('the mind to be, as we say, white paper, void of all characters, without any ideas') is heuristic / illustrative and not encoded in the AST. The metaphor is Locke's most-cited rhetorical image and is preserved verbatim in the excerpt as part of the source-text context.

**Cross-paragraph dependency** on §3 (lines 3025-3035: Sensation defined as ideas-from-particular-sensible-objects) and §4 (lines 3037-3064: Reflection defined as ideas-from-perception-of-mind's-own-operations). Without these definitions the disjunctive predicate Sensation(e) ∨ Reflection(e) is opaque. 19th cross-span citation in spirit; not re-flagging. Within-chapter cross-section dependency (same kind as Tractatus 001's within-tree commentary-elaboration pattern).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume 001** (Copy Principle). Hume 001 (1739) is the sharper, more restrictive version of Locke 001 (1689): Hume restricts to *simple* ideas, requires correspondence with *simple impressions*, demands *exact representation*. Locke 001 is the broader empiricist commitment that all ideas come from experience; Hume 001 is the technical refinement of that commitment to simple-ideas-from-simple-impressions with exact representation. Locke's principle is roughly the *generic* fol-shape ∀i. Idea(i) → ∃e. Source(e) ∧ DerivedFrom(i, e); Hume's is ∀x. (SimpleIdea(x) ∧ FirstAppearance(x)) → ∃y. SimpleImpression(y) ∧ DerivedFrom(x, y) ∧ Correspondent(x, y) ∧ ExactlyRepresents(x, y). The Locke → Hume sharpening is now a **two-extraction conceptual lineage** in the corpus, alongside the Berkeley → Russell pair flagged in iter 18.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Berkeley 001**. Berkeley (1713) sits between Locke and Hume historically. Berkeley accepts Locke's empiricist starting point (ideas come from sensation) but denies the existence of mind-independent matter — the ideas Locke takes to correspond to external objects, Berkeley takes to be the only things; the 'external objects' are themselves bundles of ideas in God's mind. Berkeley 001 (this corpus) is the dialogical opening; the full idealist argument is held as a future candidate. The Locke → Berkeley → Hume historical chain is now anchored at all three points.

**The CAPS in the source** ('MATERIALS', 'EXPERIENCE', 'SENSATION', 'REFLECTION', 'INTERNAL SENSE') are Locke's typographic emphasis convention. Preserved verbatim in the excerpt; the predicate-naming in the AST uses standard Mixed-Case per the corpus convention.

**Reception**: Locke's empiricist foundation is the explicit target of Leibniz's *Nouveaux Essais* (~1704, published 1765), Kant's argument that not *all* concepts come from experience (the Critique of Pure Reason's transcendental categories are *a priori*, not derived from sensation or reflection), and the modern debate over rationalism vs empiricism in cognitive science (Chomsky's universal grammar, Fodor's modularity, etc.). The encoded principle captures the bare claim; the rich reception is reviewer-notes context.

sourceExcerpt:
These, which I call ORIGINAL or
PRIMARY qualities of body, are wholly inseparable from it
intent: Capture Locke's foundational primary-qualities definition from Essay Book II, Chapter VIII, §9 (lines 4208-4209): primary qualities are those wholly inseparable from body in all alterations — solidity, extension, motion-or-rest, number-or-figure. **Second Locke extraction**; **same-work different-span pair** with Locke 001 (the empiricist foundation in Book II Ch. I §2). The primary/secondary qualities distinction is one of Locke's most-cited contributions; the primary-quality definition is the foundation of Lockean physical-property realism (primary qualities really inhere in bodies; secondary qualities are powers in bodies to produce ideas in us).
extractorNote: **Second Locke extraction** — Locke now at two extractions, both in Essay Book II, both fol-encoded definitional claims. **Fourth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**: Met 001/002 (PNC + LEM); Hume 001/002 (Copy Principle + argument); De Anima 001/002 (separability + first-actuality); Locke 001/002 (empiricist foundation + primary-quality realism — this). The pattern is now firmly established at four instances; would meet the schema-gap-recurrence threshold if it were a *strain* (which it's not — it's a successful structural pattern). **Eleventh corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Locke Essay 002 (primary qualities really in bodies) vs Berkeley Three Dialogues 001 (idealism — all qualities mind-dependent; primary/secondary distinction refuted). Eleven conceptual oppositions now in corpus. **British empiricist strand now substantially anchored at five extractions across three authors**: Locke 001/002 + Hume 001/002 + Berkeley 001. The strand parallels the rationalist depth (Descartes 001/002 + Spinoza 001/002 + Leibniz Monadology 001 + Discourse 001 = 6 extractions across 3 authors). British empiricist and Continental rationalist strands are now the two most-developed sub-traditions of early-modern European philosophy. Twenty-fifth fol extraction; fol now at 25 of 48 = 52%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the fourth-same-work-pair + eleventh-opposition + British-empiricist-five-extractions milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The biconditional has A-form shape ('All primary qualities are body-inseparable; all body-inseparable qualities are primary'). The predicates `PrimaryQuality` and `InseparableFromBodyInAllAlterations` are unary. Term logic could carry the convertible-A pair; fol is preferred because the biconditional captures the definitional structure more directly (and the enumeration of primary qualities — solidity, extension, motion, rest, number, figure — at §9 is itself a fol-disjunction not encodable in term logic).

The wider §9 provides supporting argument (the grain-of-wheat division thought-experiment: divide a grain and each part retains solidity/extension/figure/mobility; therefore these qualities are inseparable from body). A nd extraction could carry the argument with the definition as conclusion. The fol extraction captures the definition itself.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Essay Concerning Human Understanding* (1689; 4th ed. 1700 used by Project Gutenberg). Book II, Chapter VIII, §9. PG #10615.

**Predicate naming**: `PrimaryQuality(q)` for the definiendum; `QualityOfBody(q)` for the sortal (q is a quality of body, as opposed to other categorical types); `InseparableFromBodyInAllAlterations(q)` captures Locke's 'wholly inseparable from it; and such as in all the alterations and changes it suffers, all the force can be used upon it, it constantly keeps' — the inseparability-through-physical-transformation criterion.

**The enumerated primary qualities** (solidity, extension, motion-or-rest, number-or-figure) at §9 are *examples* of primary qualities, not the definition. The definition is *inseparability from body*; the enumeration follows from the definition + Locke's empirical-physical analysis (the grain-of-wheat thought experiment: divide-and-redivide preserves these qualities, therefore they are inseparable, therefore they are primary). A richer encoding could include the enumeration as ∀q. PrimaryQuality(q) → (Solidity(q) ∨ Extension(q) ∨ MotionOrRest(q) ∨ Number(q) ∨ Figure(q)) — but this would be a *necessary condition* on Locke's enumeration, not part of the definitional biconditional. The encoded biconditional captures the *constitutive* claim; the enumeration is a downstream empirical consequence.

**The grain-of-wheat thought experiment** (§9, lines 4214-4218) is Locke's argument FOR the inseparability claim. Take a grain of wheat, divide it: each part has solidity, extension, figure, mobility. Divide again: still these qualities. Continue dividing until the parts become insensible: they must still retain these qualities. The argument is a *limit-of-division* argument — primary qualities are precisely those that cannot be divided away. Future same-work-different-span candidate as a nd-encoded supporting argument.

**The primary/secondary distinction in §10-§14**: Secondary qualities (color, taste, smell, sound, etc.) are NOT in objects themselves but are *powers* in objects to produce ideas in us (lines 4250-4279). The corpuscular hypothesis: secondary qualities are produced by 'the operation of insensible particles on our senses' (§13). The primary qualities are *really in* bodies; secondary qualities are dispositional. This distinction is itself a future-extraction candidate.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Locke 001 (within-work same-author pair)**: Locke 001 (Book II Ch. I §2, line 3010) extracted the empiricist foundation — all ideas derive from sensation or reflection. Locke 002 (this, Book II Ch. VIII §9, line 4208) extracts the primary-qualities definition. The two extractions are complementary moves within Locke's epistemology: Locke 001 establishes that ALL ideas come from experience; Locke 002 establishes that SOME of those ideas (primary-quality ideas) really represent body-features that exist independently of perception. The pair captures the *empiricist-realist* doublet: empiricism about idea-origin + realism about primary qualities. **Fourth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern** (joining Met 001/002, Hume 001/002, De Anima 001/002).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Berkeley Three Dialogues 001 (direct opposition)**: Berkeley's idealism *rejects* Locke's primary-quality realism — Berkeley argues that primary qualities (extension, motion, figure) are equally mind-dependent as secondary qualities. The Berkeley/Locke contrast on primary qualities is one of the canonical British empiricist-internal debates. **Eleventh corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Locke Essay 002 (primary qualities really in bodies) vs Berkeley Three Dialogues 001 (Hylas's primary/secondary distinction refuted; all qualities mind-dependent). Eleven conceptual oppositions now in corpus.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Russell Problems 001 (sense-data definition)**: Russell's sense-data are immediately known in sensation; primary-quality ideas in Locke are also immediately known but are *representative* of independently-existing body-features. The Lockean representative realism is one of the historical predecessors of Russellian sense-data analysis; Russell's appendix on Locke in *Problems of Philosophy* explicitly engages with this lineage. Substantive conceptual lineage: Locke → Hume → Russell on sense-perception ontology.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Treatise 001/002 (empiricist depth)**: Hume's Copy Principle (Hume 001) takes the empiricist foundation Locke 001 establishes and sharpens it; Hume's analysis of causation continues the empiricist analysis. Locke 002's primary-qualities-realism is a position Hume *softens* in the Treatise (Hume's analysis of body is more skeptical about the primary/secondary distinction's independent reality). The British empiricist strand now has: Locke 001 (empiricist foundation, fol) + Locke 002 (primary-quality realism, fol — this) + Hume 001 (Copy Principle, fol) + Hume 002 (inductive argument for it, nd) + Berkeley 001 (skeptic-definition dialogue, dialogical) = **five British empiricist extractions** across three authors with three formalisms.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Leibniz Monadology 001 / Discourse 001 (substance ontology contrast)**: Leibnizian monads are simple substances without parts; Lockean primary-quality realism involves complex bodies with primary-quality features. Different substance ontologies, different epistemic strategies (rationalist concept-completeness vs empiricist primary-quality-realism).

**Reception**: Locke's primary/secondary distinction is the foundational text of modern philosophy of perception. The British empiricist debate (Berkeley, Hume, Reid) and the modern primary-property/secondary-property debate (Mackie, McGinn) trace directly to Locke. Locke's atomism (insensible particles) was vindicated by 19th-20th c. atomic physics; the metaphysical primary/secondary distinction remains philosophically contested.

**Translation choices**: Not applicable — English original. Locke's typographic emphasis convention (SOLIDITY, EXTENSION, ORIGINAL, PRIMARY in caps) is preserved verbatim in the excerpt; predicate naming uses standard Mixed-Case per corpus convention.

sourceExcerpt:
in this alone consists personal
identity, i.e. the sameness of a rational being: and as far as this
consciousness can be extended backwards to any past action or thought,
so far reaches the identity of that person
intent: Capture Locke's foundational consciousness-theory of personal identity from Essay Book II, Chapter XXVII, §11 (lines 11720-11723): personal identity consists in the sameness of a rational being, and as far as consciousness can be extended backwards to past action or thought, so far reaches the identity of that person. This is the **canonical Lockean memory-criterion of personal identity** — Locke's revolutionary move that defines personal identity in terms of continuity of consciousness/memory rather than substance-identity (Cartesian thinking-substance) or bodily continuity. **Third Locke extraction**; **Locke now at 3 extractions / 1 work** — **eleventh author at within-work three-extraction depth**. The Locke coverage now substantively spans the *three foundational Lockean epistemological-metaphysical moves*: empiricist foundation (Locke 001 Book II.I §2) + primary-quality realism (Locke 002 Book II.VIII §9) + consciousness-theory of personal identity (Locke 003 Book II.XXVII §11 — this).
extractorNote: **Third Locke extraction** — Locke now at **3 extractions / 1 work**: Locke 001 (II.I §2 empiricist foundation) + Locke 002 (II.VIII §9 primary-quality realism) + Locke 003 (II.XXVII §11 consciousness-theory of personal identity — this). **Locke joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama, Wittgenstein, Russell, Locke (this). **Eleven authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. **Seven consecutive iterations have added new within-work three-extraction authors** (iters 70-76). **Fourteenth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**: Locke Essay 001-003 covers the three foundational Lockean moves (empiricist epistemology + primary-quality realism + consciousness-theory of personal identity). **Two new corpus conceptual opposition pairs**: (47) Descartes Meditations 001/002 vs Locke Essay 003 (substance-identity theory of self vs consciousness-theory of personal identity); (48) Aristotle De Anima 002/003 vs Locke Essay 003 (hylomorphic-soul vs consciousness-theory). **Forty-eight conceptual oppositions now in corpus**. **Twenty-seventh corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Locke Essay 003 + Russell Problems 002 on modern-empiricist self-knowledge. **Twenty-seven corpus alliances now documented**. **British empiricist strand now at 10 extractions / 3 authors with 3-each within-author coverage** (Locke × 3 + Hume × 4 + Berkeley × 3 — actually 3-3-3 at minimum, but Hume × 4 since Hume Treatise × 2 + Enquiry + Dialogues = 4). British empiricist strand now substantively matches/exceeds rationalist substance-metaphysics strand (9 ext / 3 authors / 3-each). Fiftieth fol extraction; fol now at 50 of 76 = 66%. **fol crosses 50-extraction milestone**. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the third-Locke + Locke-at-3-extractions-1-work + eleventh-within-work-three-extraction-author + fourteenth-three-or-more-extraction-coverage + Lockean-three-foundational-moves-coherence + two-new-oppositions + twenty-seventh-alliance + British-empiricist-strand-at-10-ext-3-authors-3-each + fol-50-extraction-milestone + seven-consecutive-iterations-new-within-work-three-extraction-author milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: Ch. XXVII §11 develops the consciousness-theory inferentially from prior considerations (definition of person, role of consciousness in self-perception). A nd extraction could carry the multi-step development. The fol biconditional captures the *core definitional claim* (identity-reach iff consciousness-extension).
The biconditional has convertible-A shape but involves binary relations (IdentityOfPersonReachesAction, ConsciousnessExtendsBackTo) which term logic cannot express in unary form.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Essay Concerning Human Understanding* (1689; 4th ed. 1700), Book II, Chapter XXVII 'Of Identity and Diversity', §11 (lines 11720-11723). PG #10615.

**Predicate naming**: `Person(x)` for the Lockean technical term (a thinking intelligent being with reason, reflection, self-recognition); `PastActionOrThought(a)` for any past action or thought of any agent; `IdentityOfPersonReachesAction(x, a)` for the metaphysical-identity relation between person x and past action a (x *is* the agent who performed a); `ConsciousnessExtendsBackTo(x, a)` for the consciousness/memory relation between person x and past action a (x has consciousness-access to a).

**The biconditional encoding**: captures Locke's *strict identification* — identity-reach equals consciousness-extension. Both directions hold: if consciousness extends to a past action, then identity reaches that action (sufficiency); if identity reaches a past action, then consciousness must extend to it (necessity). The biconditional is the substantive Lockean move; it makes personal identity *constitutively* a consciousness relation, not a substance relation.

**Locke's anti-Cartesian move**: Cartesian dualism treats personal identity as substance-identity of the thinking soul (Descartes Meditations 001/002 cogito; the simple thinking-substance is what persists). Locke explicitly *separates* personal identity from substance-identity: 'it not being considered, in this case, whether the same self be continued in the same or divers substances' (line 11716-11717). This is Locke's revolutionary move that grounds modern person-as-psychological-continuity theories of identity.

**The person-vs-substance distinction**: Locke distinguishes three substance-types (God, finite spirits, bodies) earlier in Ch. XXVII §2. Personal identity is *not* substance-identity — a person could in principle persist through substance-change (different thinking-substances at different times) if consciousness is continuous, OR could fail to be the same person across substance-continuity if consciousness is interrupted. This is the Lockean move that opens psychological-continuity theories of personal identity.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Locke 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage)**: Locke 001 (Book II.I §2 empiricist foundation: all ideas from sensation/reflection, fol) + Locke 002 (Book II.VIII §9 primary-quality realism, fol) + Locke 003 (Book II.XXVII §11 consciousness-theory of personal identity, fol — this). Three Locke extractions covering the *three foundational Lockean epistemological-metaphysical moves*: empiricist epistemology + primary-quality realism + consciousness-theory of personal identity. **Fourteenth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage** (joins prior thirteen).

**Locke joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama, Wittgenstein, Russell, Locke (this). **Eleven authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. The within-work-three-extraction-author-tier expansion has continued for **seven consecutive iterations** (Hobbes iter 70 → Marcus iter 71 → Berkeley iter 72 → Gotama iter 73 → Wittgenstein iter 74 → Russell iter 75 → Locke iter 76).

**British empiricist strand now at 10 extractions / 3 authors with 3-each within-author coverage**: Locke × 3 (this iteration completes) + Hume × 4 + Berkeley × 3 = 10 extractions / 3 authors. **The British empiricist strand now matches the rationalist substance-metaphysics strand in 3-each-author multi-extraction depth** (Descartes × 3 + Spinoza × 3 + Leibniz × 3 = 9 ext / 3 authors). Both major modern European sub-traditions now substantively three-author 3-each-each grounded. The British empiricist strand actually *exceeds* the rationalist strand by one extraction (10 vs 9) due to Hume × 4.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Descartes Meditations 001/002 (substantive opposition — Cartesian substance-identity vs Lockean consciousness-identity)**: Descartes's cogito establishes the thinking-self as a *substance* (Meditations 001/002); personal identity for Descartes is substance-identity of the thinking soul. Locke 003 explicitly *separates* personal identity from substance-identity. **Forty-seventh corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Descartes Meditations 001/002 (substance-identity theory of self) vs Locke Essay 003 (consciousness-theory of personal identity). The Descartes-Locke contrast on the structure of personal identity is canonical.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Treatise 001 + 002 (substantive partial-alliance — both reject substance-theories of self)**: Hume's Treatise (Book I Part IV §6 — bundle theory of the self) further deconstructs the Lockean consciousness-theory by denying any unified self at all (the self is a bundle of perceptions). Locke (this) rejects substance-identity for personal identity but retains consciousness as the identity criterion; Hume goes further and rejects all unified-self theories. **Partial-alliance**: both reject Cartesian substance-self; Locke's positive theory (consciousness) is targeted by Hume's more radical deflationary view. Substantive lineage Descartes → Locke → Hume on self-theory deepens.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle De Anima 002 + 003 (substantive opposition — hylomorphic soul vs psychological-continuity theory)**: Aristotle's hylomorphic soul (De Anima 002 entelechy) and active-passive intellect (De Anima 003) treat the soul as a *structured biological-metaphysical* entity. Locke's consciousness-theory abstracts away from soul-substance entirely. **Forty-eighth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle De Anima 002/003 (hylomorphic-soul psychology) vs Locke Essay 003 (consciousness-theory of personal identity). The ancient-modern contrast on soul/self ontology is canonical.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hegel Phenomenology 002 (substantive alliance — both intersubjective/social-mediated theories of self)**: Hegel's mutual recognition (Phen 002) treats self-consciousness as requiring the other for completion. Locke's consciousness-theory of personal identity could be read as compatible with intersubjective extensions (consciousness might involve social-mediated content). Substantive partial-alliance on non-substance-based theories of self.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Russell Problems 002 (substantive alliance — both modern-empiricist accounts of self-knowledge)**: Russell's acquaintance framework (Problems 002) allows for acquaintance with oneself; Locke's consciousness-theory grounds personal identity in self-awareness. Both Locke and Russell are *modern-empiricist* about self-knowledge — knowledge of self is grounded in introspection / self-consciousness. **Twenty-seventh corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Locke Essay 003 + Russell Problems 002 on modern-empiricist self-knowledge.

**Reception**: Locke's consciousness-theory of personal identity is the foundational text of modern psychological-continuity theories of personal identity. Reid's *Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man* (1785) critiques Locke via the brave-officer paradox. Butler's *Of Personal Identity* (1736) develops the same critique. Modern personal-identity theory (Parfit *Reasons and Persons* 1984; Olson *The Human Animal* 1997; Schechtman *The Constitution of Selves* 1996) is substantively Lockean or anti-Lockean. The legal-juridical concept of person (responsibility-bearing across time) descends from Locke.

sourceExcerpt:
Now death and life, glory
and reproach, pain and pleasure, riches and poverty--all these
happen equally to the good and to the bad. But, as they are neither
honourable nor shameful, they are therefore neither good nor evil.
intent: Capture the closing inferential move of Meditations II.11 — the classic Stoic argument for the indifference of externals (death, life, glory, reproach, pain, pleasure, riches, poverty). The argument: external things are neither honourable nor shameful (because they happen equally to good and bad alike); what is neither honourable nor shameful is neither good nor evil; therefore externals are neither good nor evil. The corpus's first Stoic extraction; first Marcus Aurelius extraction; opens the Stoic strand alongside the rationalist (Spinoza), social-contract (Hobbes), and analytic (Wittgenstein) strands of the modern period.
extractorNote: Corpus's **first Stoic extraction** — opens the Stoic strand alongside rationalist (Spinoza), social-contract (Hobbes), and analytic (Wittgenstein) in the modern-period (well, modern in a broad sense; Marcus Aurelius is late antiquity, c. 161-180 CE, but late-antiquity Stoicism feeds into early-modern moral thought directly via Lipsius / the Renaissance neo-Stoic revival). Span chosen tight at 4 lines — the closing one-step inference of the larger Meditations II.11 maxim. The wider maxim's theological-ethical argument (Gods exist + regard human affairs + give man power to avoid evil + therefore externals are indifferents) is held out as a candidate future extraction (different-span pair within the same maxim, similar to the Euthyphro 700-741/743-792 and Laches 819-850/852-921 patterns flagged in earlier iterations). The disjunctive-predicate encoding (`¬(Honourable(x) ∨ Shameful(x))`) is the encoding choice that distinguishes this extraction from a cleaner aristotelian-style syllogism reading. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria: no new schema gap (cross-span citation is established signal; nd Fitch-rule limitation is already documented), no act-vocab friction (different formalism), the first-Stoic milestone goes in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The conclusion alone (∀x. IsExternal(x) → ¬(Good(x) ∨ Evil(x))) is a single quantified claim and fits fol directly. But Marcus's text has explicit inferential structure ('But, as they are neither honourable nor shameful, they are therefore neither good nor evil') — a one-step inference from a stated premise via an implicit principle. nd preserves the argument shape; fol would collapse it to the conclusion.
assessments.distortionRisk: A fol-only extraction would erase the 'as ... therefore ...' inferential frame Marcus deploys to motivate the conclusion.

assessments.reason: The argument has the shape 'No external is honourable-or-shameful; nothing honourable-or-shameful is good-or-evil; therefore no external is good-or-evil' — a Celarent-like (EAE-1) syllogism in spirit. Categorical term logic could carry it, but the predicates are *disjunctive* (honourable-or-shameful, good-or-evil), which the aristotelian schema's atomic Subject/Predicate strings cannot represent as disjunctions without flattening into single composite predicate names.
assessments.distortionRisk: Encoding 'Honourable-or-Shameful' as a single atomic predicate string would lose the disjunctive structure that the Stoic Goods/Indifferents/Evils typology depends on.

assessments.reason: Not dialogical. Self-addressed Stoic meditation; no exchange, no speakers, no concessions.

reviewerNotes: Marcus Aurelius Meditations II.11 (closing inference at the end of the maxim). The full maxim spans lines 366-383 of source.txt; this extraction encodes only the closing 4-line inference at lines 380-383. The wider maxim is a theological-ethical argument: Gods exist, they regard human affairs, they have given man power to avoid what is truly evil, and what is truly evil cannot happen to him — hence the famous Stoic conclusion that externals (which DO happen to him) are indifferents, neither good nor evil.

Translator: George W. Chrystal (1902 'new rendering based on the Foulis translation of 1742'). Edinburgh: Otto Schulze. PG #55317. Greek original c. 161-180 CE, written in koine Greek as personal reflections.

**Cross-span dependency** on the wider maxim II.11 (lines 366-379 of source.txt). The conclusion at 383 is the closing move of the longer theological-ethical argument; the 'they' in 'as they are neither honourable nor shameful' refers back to 'death and life, glory and reproach, pain and pleasure, riches and poverty' enumerated at 380-381 of this span — but the LOGICAL premises (Gods give power to avoid true evil, externals happen equally to good and bad, etc.) are at 366-379. 10th corpus occurrence of cross-span citation; not re-flagging.

**Encoding of disjunctive predicates**: 'neither honourable nor shameful' encodes as `¬(Honourable(x) ∨ Shameful(x))`. The Greek pair (καλόν / αἰσχρόν, beautiful-noble / shameful) is treated as a complementary pair in the Stoic tradition — together they exhaust the moral-evaluative dimension. Same for 'neither good nor evil' = `¬(Good(x) ∨ Evil(x))` (ἀγαθόν / κακόν). The disjunction structure is preserved rather than flattened into composite predicates.

**The implicit middle premise**: '∀x. ¬(Honourable(x) ∨ Shameful(x)) → ¬(Good(x) ∨ Evil(x))' is the inferential bridge Marcus uses but does not state — it is the standard Stoic identification of the morally-evaluative dimension (good/evil) with the honourable/shameful dimension. Encoded as the second premise. This is the textbook Stoic claim: virtue is the only good (= honourable), vice the only evil (= shameful), and everything else (the indifferents) is neither.

**`IsExternal(x)` predicate**: covers the enumerated list 'death, life, glory, reproach, pain, pleasure, riches, poverty.' The Stoic technical term is τὰ ἀδιάφορα (the indifferents) / τὰ προηγμένα and τὰ ἀποπροηγμένα (the preferred and dispreferred indifferents). The encoded predicate captures the extension; a multi-sorted fol extension would type 'External' as a sort.

**nd Fitch ruleset is propositional only** (per schemas/nd.py docstring): no quantifier rules. The argument here is a hypothetical syllogism over a universal (P1 ∀-conditional, P2 ∀-conditional, C ∀-conditional via cut) — same shape as Spinoza 001 (Ethics PROP. III) and same Fitch-rule limitation. The optional `proof` field is not included; the argument-shape recording is what nd carries for this case.

**The Theophrastus citation** at lines 353-364 (II.10, in a neighbouring maxim — outside this extraction's span) is itself a third-party citation by Marcus to Theophrastus on the comparison of crimes. Not directly relevant to this extraction's argument but flagged as a candidate `external_cites` future case if the citation pattern recurs across Meditations extractions.

Translation choices: Chrystal's 'honourable' for καλόν is conventional but loses the aesthetic-moral dual sense of the Greek (which also means 'beautiful'). The argument turns on this evaluative dimension, not on the aesthetic; reading 'honourable/shameful' as the moral-evaluative pair is the standard scholarly interpretation.

sourceExcerpt:
regard human affairs; and they have put it wholly in man's power that
he should not fall into what is truly evil. And of other things, had
any been bad, they would have made provision also that man should have
the power to avoid them altogether.
intent: Capture the **theological-providence argument** of Meditations II.11 (lines 371-379) — the argument Marcus gives to *ground* the closing inference Marcus 001 (iter 13) encodes. The argument: Gods have put it wholly in man's power that he should not fall into what is truly evil (providence premise); but externals happen promiscuously to good and bad alike (so are not in any individual's power to avoid through virtue); therefore externals are not truly evil. **Second Marcus Aurelius extraction**; **second Stoic extraction**; **different-span argument pair with Marcus 001** within the same Meditations II.11 maxim — Marcus 002 is the theological-providence route (lines 371-379), Marcus 001 is the honourable/shameful route (lines 380-383). Two independent argumentative paths to the same conclusion, both contained in the same meditation.
extractorNote: **Second Marcus Aurelius extraction** — second Stoic extraction; second nd extraction in Stoic strand; **different-span pair with Marcus 001 within the same Meditations II.11 maxim**. The pair instantiates a **new pair-type variant**: same-maxim-different-route pair, where two independent arguments reach the same conclusion via different premises. Distinct from prior pair patterns: same-span pair (slave-boy 002/003 — same lines, different formalisms); different-span-within-dialogue (Euthyphro candidate — different lines, same formalism, related but distinct arguments); same-work-different-formalism (Hobbes 001/002, Met 001/002 — different formalisms within work); instance-and-typology (Nyāya 001/002 — instance + classification of instances); cross-school-typology (Nyāya 002 + Dasgupta 001 — same content type across schools); argument-for-principle (Hume 001/002 — principle + argument for it); cross-work conceptual pair (Leibniz Monadology 001 + Discourse 001 — same concept defined two ways across works). The Marcus 001/002 pair is the **eighth pair-type variant** in the corpus's pair pattern lexicon. **Stoic strand now substantially anchored**: two extractions covering the *what* of Stoic indifference (Marcus 001 — honourable/shameful bridge) and the *why* (Marcus 002 — theological providence). Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the same-maxim-different-route pair-type variant is novel enough to note in SESSION_LOG but does not require a log.md entry (single-instance pattern observation, not schema-gap recurrence).

assessments.reason: The conclusion alone (∀x. IsExternal(x) → ¬TrulyEvil(x)) fits fol directly; the providence-and-non-voluntariness premise pair would be lost. nd preserves the modus-tollens/contrapositive inferential rhythm Marcus actually deploys.

The argument has Celarent-like (EAE-1) structure: no externals are in-man's-power-to-avoid; everything truly-evil is in-man's-power-to-avoid; therefore no externals are truly-evil. Term logic could carry it; the predicates are unary. The fit is genuine; nd is preferred because the providence-argument's middle term ('in man's power') is itself the load-bearing inferential move (the contrapositive bridge), and explicit premise/conclusion structure is more faithful to Marcus's prose.
Not dialogical. Self-addressed Stoic meditation.

reviewerNotes: Source: same Meditations II.11 maxim as Marcus 001 (PG #55317, Chrystal 1902 translation). Marcus 001 extracted the *closing inference* (lines 380-383, honourable/shameful → good/evil bridge); Marcus 002 extracts the *theological-providence argument* (lines 371-379) that grounds the premise Marcus 001 relies on implicitly (that externals are not in man's power to avoid, and what is truly evil *is* in man's power to avoid).

**Predicate naming**: `TrulyEvil(x)` for what Marcus calls 'what is truly evil' (κακόν in Greek, contrasted with the indifferents); `InMansPowerToAvoid(x)` for what Marcus says Gods have put wholly in man's power to avoid; `IsExternal(x)` for the indifferents (death, life, glory, reproach, pain, pleasure, riches, poverty — the same predicate-name used in Marcus 001 for consistency).

**The two-premise contrapositive structure**: P1 (providence — truly evil is in man's power to avoid) + P2 (externals are not in man's power to avoid) ⊢ externals are not truly evil. This is modus tollens applied per-x: from External(x), get ¬InMansPower(x); from contrapositive of P1, get ¬TrulyEvil(x). Clean Stoic argumentative rhythm.

**The wider maxim's structure** is more elaborate than the encoded core: lines 366-368 (a memento-mori opening — 'do every deed in the knowledge that you may end your days any moment'); lines 368-371 (a constructive dilemma — if Gods exist, departing is not terrible; if no Gods or no regard, why want to live in such a world?); lines 371-379 (this extraction — the theological-providence argument); lines 379-383 (Marcus 001 — the honourable/shameful closing inference). Lines 366-368 and 368-371 are not encoded here; they are rhetorical/motivational rather than argumentative-load-bearing.

**nd Fitch ruleset is propositional only**: the argument here is quantified (∀-conditionals over IsExternal, TrulyEvil, InMansPowerToAvoid), so the optional `proof` field is not included. Same convention as Meno 001, NE 002, Spinoza 001, Marcus Aurelius 001, Descartes 001, Hume 002 — all quantified nd arguments without proof field.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Marcus 001 — different-span argument pair within same maxim**: Marcus 002 (this, lines 371-379, providence argument) + Marcus 001 (lines 380-383, honourable/shameful closing inference) form a **different-span pair within the same Meditations II.11 maxim**. This is the **different-span pair pattern** previously flagged as a candidate (Euthyphro 700-741/743-792, Laches 819-850/852-921) — now exemplified in the Marcus corpus. The two extractions are conceptually *complementary*: both reach the conclusion 'externals are not truly evil', but via independent argumentative paths (theological-providence vs honourable/shameful bridge). Same work, same maxim, same formalism (nd), different spans, different premises, overlapping conclusion. **New pair-type variant**: same-maxim-different-route pair, where both routes reach the same conclusion via independent arguments.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza 001 (geometric demonstration)**: Spinoza's Ethics Prop. III derives ¬IsCauseOf from ¬HaveInCommon via cited axioms. Marcus 002 derives ¬TrulyEvil from ¬InMansPowerToAvoid via providence. Both are **modus-tollens-style derivations from a positive premise to a negative conclusion** — same inferential rhythm, different doctrinal content (Stoic providence vs Spinozistic substance metaphysics). The Stoic strand now has two nd extractions, joining the rationalist strand's nd structure.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hobbes 001 (state-of-nature contractualism)**: Marcus's Stoic providential cosmos (Gods give man power to avoid true evil) and Hobbes's contractual state-of-nature (no power before contract; war of all against all) are radically opposed views of the human condition's grounding. Marcus's Stoic cosmopolitanism vs Hobbes's social-contract atomism is one of the canonical philosophical oppositions. **Fifth corpus conceptual opposition pair candidate** — but flagging as candidate rather than confirmed, because both extractions don't directly engage with the same question (Marcus on providence, Hobbes on social contract; both about human-condition-grounding but at different conceptual levels).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 001/002 (eudaimonia and ergon)**: Aristotle's function-argument (NE 002) concludes that the good of man is the working of soul with reason — which would be *internal*, in man's voluntary control, on Marcus's typology. The two extractions agree on the *what-is-truly-good* class being internal/voluntary; they disagree on whether externals are *truly indifferent* (Stoic) vs *contributors to eudaimonia though insufficient* (Aristotelian). Substantive philosophical agreement-with-friction.

**Translation choices**: Chrystal's 'truly evil' for κακόν is faithful; modern translations (Hays, Hard) sometimes use 'genuinely bad' or 'really evil' for the same Greek. 'Wholly in man's power' for ἐπὶ τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ is conventional; the Stoic technical term is ἐφ' ἡμῖν ('up to us') — Marcus uses this language throughout the Meditations for the indifferent-vs-up-to-us distinction.

**Reception**: Marcus's providence-argument is the canonical statement of late Stoic theodicy. Epictetus's *Discourses* (especially I.1, I.6) develops the ἐφ' ἡμῖν / ¬ἐφ' ἡμῖν distinction that grounds the argument. Modern Stoic revival (Hadot, Pigliucci) takes Meditations II.11 as the locus classicus of Stoic indifference-of-externals.

sourceExcerpt:
even the smallest things should be referred to the
end, and the end of rational beings is to follow the order and law of
the venerable state and polity which comprehends them all.
intent: Capture Marcus Aurelius's canonical Stoic cosmopolitan claim from Meditations II.16 (lines 443-445): the end (telos) of rational beings is to follow the order and law of the cosmic state-and-polity. This is the **foundational text of Stoic cosmopolitanism** — the doctrine that the rational soul's purpose is alignment with the cosmic-rational order (Logos) which is itself the law of the universal city (kosmopolis) of which all rational beings are citizens. **Third Marcus Aurelius extraction**; **Marcus now at 3 extractions / 1 work** — joining Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes at within-work three-extraction depth. **Sixth author at the within-work three-extraction tier**. The Marcus coverage now substantively spans the *three foundational Stoic ethical claims*: indifference-of-externals (Marcus 001) + theological-providence (Marcus 002) + cosmopolitan-telos (Marcus 003 — this).
extractorNote: **Third Marcus Aurelius extraction** — Marcus now at **3 extractions / 1 work** (all in Meditations Book II): Marcus 001 (II.11 honourable/shameful inference) + Marcus 002 (II.11 theological-providence) + Marcus 003 (II.16 cosmopolitan-telos — this). **Marcus joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus (this). **Six authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. **Ninth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage** (Marcus Meditations Book II three-extraction substantively coherent Stoic-ethical-framework coverage). **Two new corpus cross-extraction alliances**: (21) Aristotle Politics 001 + Marcus 003 on naturalistic-teleological political/cosmic-polity end; (22) Kant Groundwork 002 + Marcus 003 on rational-nature-as-source-of-normativity. **Twenty-two corpus alliances now documented**. **Stoic-Nietzschean opposition deepens** (Marcus 001/002/003 vs Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 = 3-vs-3 multi-extraction opposition on the value of cosmic-alignment/asceticism). **Plato-Marcus alliance on rational-governance now spans multi-extractions on both sides** (Plato Phaedrus + Republic 002 + Marcus 001 + 002 + 003). Forty-fifth fol extraction; fol now at 45 of 71 = 63%. A nd extraction could carry the inferential development. The fol extraction captures the *cosmopolitan-telos* thesis directly.

The claim has clean A-form shape ('All rational beings have-cosmic-polity-following-as-end'). Both predicates are unary. Term logic could carry it directly. fol preferred only marginally — the formal structure is virtually identical.
