reviewerNotes: Source: *Meditations* (c. 161-180 CE), II.16 (lines 443-445 in source.txt). George W. Chrystal 1902 translation. PG #55317.

**Predicate naming**: `RationalBeing(x)` for the Stoic *to logikon* (rational being — including humans, gods, and any rational substance in the Stoic universe); `EndIsFollowingOrderAndLawOfCosmicPolity(x)` captures Marcus's *telos* claim — the *order and law of the venerable state and polity* (the cosmic order / Logos / nature's law) is what the rational soul should follow.

**The cosmopolitan-telos thesis**: Marcus identifies the *end* (telos) of rational beings with *cosmic-political* alignment. The cosmic order (Logos) *is* the law of a universal state — the Stoic *kosmopolis* of which all rational beings are citizens by virtue of their rational nature. This is the foundational Stoic cosmopolitan move: the *individual* good is not separate from the *cosmic-rational* good; the rational soul's purpose is alignment with the cosmic-rational structure.

**The Greek technical content**: *telos* (end, purpose, goal); *logos* (rational principle, cosmic reason, divine word); *kosmopolis* (cosmic city/polity); *politēs tou kosmou* (citizen of the cosmos — the Stoic cosmopolitan identification). The encoded predicate captures this constellation as a single technical content predicate.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Marcus 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage)**: Marcus 001 (II.11 closing inference, lines 380-383 — externals are neither good nor evil, via honourable/shameful bridge) + Marcus 002 (II.11 theological-providence argument, lines 371-379 — gods give man power to avoid true evil) + Marcus 003 (II.16, lines 443-445 — cosmic-polity telos of rational beings — this). Three Marcus extractions covering the **three foundational Stoic ethical claims**: indifference-of-externals (Marcus 001 *what* — the value-status of externals) + theological-providence (Marcus 002 *why* — the divine-providential ground of the indifference) + cosmopolitan-telos (Marcus 003 *whither* — the positive end-direction of rational life). The three together cover the *core Stoic ethical framework*. **Ninth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage** (joining Aristotle De Int 001-004; Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003; Descartes Meditations 001-003; Aristotle De Anima 001-003; Hegel Phenomenology 001-003; Aristotle Metaphysics 001-003; Spinoza Ethics 001-003; Hobbes Leviathan 001-003; Marcus Aurelius Meditations 001-003 — this).

**Marcus joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus (this). **Six authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. The corpus's within-work-three-extraction-depth structural feature continues to expand.

**Marcus Aurelius three-extraction substantive coherence**: Marcus 001 + 002 are both at Meditations II.11 (different-spans-within-same-maxim pair from iter 43); Marcus 003 is at Meditations II.16 (different-maxim same-book — Book II). The three Marcus extractions all from Book II span two adjacent maxims; substantively cohere as the *core Stoic ethical framework* documentation. The pattern (3 ext / 1 book within 1 work) is similar to Aristotle De Int 001-004 (4 ext in De Int Ch. 9) and Spinoza Ethics 001-003 (3 ext in Part I).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Politics 001 (substantive alliance — both naturalistic political telos)**: Aristotle Politics 001 (humans are naturally political animals; political society is a natural production) and Marcus Meditations 003 (rational beings have cosmic-polity-following as end) are both *naturalistic-teleological* accounts of the political/cosmic-political end. Aristotle: the *natural* polis (Greek city-state) is constitutive of human flourishing. Marcus: the *cosmic* kosmopolis is constitutive of rational-being flourishing. The Greek-tradition naturalistic-teleological-political alliance is substantive. **Twenty-first corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle Politics 001 + Marcus Aurelius Meditations 003 on naturalistic-teleological political/cosmic-polity end.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Phaedrus 001 + Republic 002 (substantive alliance — rational-soul-rules-other-faculties)**: Plato's chariot (Phaedrus 001) and tripartite-class-functional polis (Republic 002) and Marcus's cosmic-polity-telos (this) share the *rational-element-as-foundational* structure. Plato's charioteer (reason) governs horses (spirit + appetite); Marcus's rational beings align with the cosmic-rational order; Plato's polis has rulers (reason) governing auxiliaries + producers (spirit + appetite). Substantive cross-extraction alliance on rational-governance-as-foundational. The thirteenth corpus alliance (Plato Phaedrus 001 + Marcus Aurelius 001/002, iter 61) is now reinforced with Marcus 003 — the **Plato-Marcus alliance on rational-governance now spans multi-extractions on both sides** (Plato Phaedrus + Republic 002 + Marcus 001 + 002 + 003 — substantive multi-extraction Greek-tradition alliance).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 002 (substantive alliance — both ergon/function-based ethics)**: Aristotle's function-argument (NE 002 — good-of-man = working-of-soul-with-reason) and Marcus's cosmopolitan-telos (rational-beings-end = following-cosmic-order) are both *ergon*-functional ethics. Aristotle: individual ergon (rational-soul activity); Marcus: rational-beings ergon (cosmic-order alignment). Substantive cross-extraction alliance on ergon-functional ethics; the eighth corpus alliance (Plato Republic 002 + Aristotle NE 002 on ergon-functional ethics, iter 56) is now further reinforced with Marcus 003.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Nietzsche Genealogy 003 (substantive opposition deepened — Stoic cosmopolitan-telos vs Nietzsche will-to-nothingness anti-telos)**: Nietzsche Genealogy 003 (will-to-nothingness — humans prefer willing-nothingness over not-willing-at-all) frames the Stoic cosmic-polity-telos as a *high-philosophical form of will-to-nothingness*. The Stoic cosmopolitan-alignment Nietzsche reads as ascetic-renunciation disguised as ethical achievement. **Fifteenth corpus conceptual opposition pair deepened** (Marcus Aurelius 001/002 vs Nietzsche Genealogy 003, established iter 54) — now substantively documented with Marcus 003's positive cosmopolitan-telos claim opposing Nietzsche's anti-telos critique. The Stoic-Nietzschean opposition on the value-of-cosmic-alignment is now multi-extraction-grounded.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Kant Groundwork 002 (substantive alliance — both rational-nature-as-source-of-normativity)**: Kant's Formula of Humanity grounds normativity in *rational nature as end-in-itself*; Marcus's cosmopolitan-telos grounds normativity in *rational beings' end as following cosmic-rational order*. Both philosophers ground normativity in rational nature. **Twenty-second corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Kant Groundwork 002 + Marcus Aurelius 003 on rational-nature-as-source-of-normativity. The Kant-Stoic alliance on rational-nature-grounded ethics is historically substantive — Kant's deontological tradition has explicit Stoic roots.

**Reception**: Marcus's Stoic cosmopolitanism is the foundational text of cosmopolitan-philosophical tradition. Modern cosmopolitanism (Kant *Perpetual Peace* 1795; Nussbaum *Cultivating Humanity* 1997) inherits the Stoic framework. World-citizenship discourse in international law and ethics traces conceptually to Stoic cosmopolitanism. Foucault's *Hermeneutics of the Subject* (1981-82 lectures) treats Stoic ethics as the canonical *care of the self* tradition.

**Translation choices**: Chrystal 1902 'rational beings' for *to logikon*; 'order and law of the venerable state and polity' for the Greek cosmic-political framework. Modern translations (Hays, Hard) use 'rational and social' beings or 'reasonable creatures' for *to logikon*; 'order of the universe' or 'law of the cosmos' for the Logos.

sourceExcerpt:
the only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any
member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to
others.
intent: Capture Mill's canonical Harm Principle from On Liberty Chapter I (lines 787-789): the only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others. This is the **foundational liberal political principle** — the criterion for legitimate state coercion and the basis of the modern liberal-democratic framework. **Second Mill extraction**; **second Mill work** (corpus has Mill Utilitarianism); first Mill On Liberty extraction. **Mill strand now at 2 extractions across 2 works**: Utilitarianism 001 (Greatest Happiness Principle — ethical foundation) + On Liberty 001 (Harm Principle — political foundation). The pair captures Mill's two-foundational philosophical contributions: utilitarian ethics + liberal politics.
extractorNote: **First Mill On Liberty extraction** — Mill now at **2 extractions across 2 works**: Utilitarianism 001 (Greatest Happiness Principle — ethics) + On Liberty 001 (Harm Principle — politics — this). Mill becomes the **third multi-work author with within-author across-work coherence** (joining Aristotle, Hume; Plato has 3+ works extracted but coverage scattered, not unified). **Three new corpus conceptual opposition pairs** introduced (eighteenth, nineteenth, twentieth, twenty-first): (18) Aristotle Politics 001/002 vs Mill On Liberty 001 (natural-political vs liberal-restricted); (19) Hobbes Leviathan 001/002 vs Mill On Liberty 001 (sovereign-absolute vs sovereign-restricted); (20) Plato Republic 001/002 vs Mill On Liberty 001 (paternalist-class-functional vs anti-paternalist-liberal); (21) Mill On Liberty 001 vs Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (egalitarian-liberalism vs aristocratic-radicalism). **Twenty-one corpus conceptual oppositions now documented** — substantive jump from 17 to 21 in one extraction. Mill On Liberty 001 is **the corpus's most-opposed single extraction so far** (4 oppositions in one iteration); reflects Mill's *systematic position* in the political-philosophy debates — liberalism is opposed by ancient (Plato, Aristotle), modern-contractualist (Hobbes), and continental-critical (Nietzsche) traditions. **Tenth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Rousseau Social Contract 001 + Mill On Liberty 001 as liberal-democratic foundational principles. **Ten alliances now in corpus**. **Political-philosophy cluster now at 12 extractions across 5 authors** — first 5-author corpus cluster. Thirty-fourth fol extraction; fol now at 34 of 58 = 59%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the four-new-oppositions + tenth-alliance + Mill-most-opposed-single-extraction + political-philosophy-cluster-at-12-extractions-5-authors + Mill-strand-at-2-works milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The principle has biconditional structure within a conditional context ('for coercive actions against will: rightful iff prevents harm to others'). Term logic could carry the inner biconditional as a convertible-A pair but loses the conditional context (Mill restricts the principle to civilised-community-coercion-against-will). fol preserves both layers.

Mill's Chapter I builds inferentially to the principle, with the principle as conclusion. The wider chapter argues against paternalism, against majority-tyranny, against moral coercion — all converging on the harm-principle conclusion. A nd extraction could carry the argumentative chain. The fol extraction captures the principle itself; nd would be the supporting argument.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *On Liberty* (1859), Chapter I 'Introductory'. Project Gutenberg #34901. The canonical Harm Principle statement at lines 787-789.

**Predicate naming**: `CoerciveActionAgainstWillInCivilisedCommunity(a)` for the scope-restriction (Mill explicitly limits the principle to mature adults in civilised society — lines 803-814); `RightfullyExercised(a)` for the deontic-justificational status; `AimsToPreventHarmToOthers(a)` for the harm-prevention criterion (the *other-regarding harm* condition).

**The biconditional encoding**: captures Mill's *strict* formulation — coercion against will is *rightful* iff it prevents harm to others. Both directions hold: harm-prevention coercion is rightful (sufficiency); only harm-prevention coercion is rightful (necessity). Self-regarding paternalism (coercing for one's own good) is explicitly excluded by the necessity direction; harm-prevention is the *only* justification.

**Mill's scope-restrictions** (lines 803-814): the Harm Principle applies only to human beings in the maturity of their faculties; children, persons in non-self-determining condition, and 'backward states of society' are excluded. The encoded predicate name (`...InCivilisedCommunity`) captures this restriction; the explicit scope-clauses are reviewer-noted but not encoded.

**Mill's distinction between coercion and persuasion**: 'These are good reasons for remonstrating with him, or reasoning with him, or persuading him, or entreating him, but not for compelling him, or visiting him with any evil in case he do otherwise' (lines 793-795). The principle restricts *coercion* (force, legal penalties, moral coercion via public opinion); persuasion remains permissible. The encoded predicate (`CoerciveAction...`) preserves this distinction; the persuasion-permitted clarification is reviewer-noted.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill Utilitarianism 001 (within-author across-work coherence)**: Mill Utilitarianism 001 (Greatest Happiness Principle: ∀a. Right(a) ↔ TendsToPromoteHappiness(a)) is Mill's *ethical foundation*; Mill On Liberty 001 (this) is Mill's *political foundation*. The two are typically read as compatible: the Harm Principle is the *political* implication of utilitarianism (state coercion that prevents harm tends to promote happiness; state coercion for paternalistic ends does not). The Mill strand now has **2 extractions across 2 works** with substantive within-author coherence (ethics + politics integrated per Mill's own systematic philosophy).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Rousseau Social Contract 001 (substantive partial-alliance — both liberal-democratic foundations)**: Rousseau's convention-only legitimacy framework and Mill's harm-principle restriction on state coercion are both *liberal-democratic foundational principles*. Substantive partial-alliance on the legitimacy-of-state-coercion question; partial-tension on the *positive* role of state (Rousseau: state actualizes general will; Mill: state is restricted to harm-prevention). **Tenth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Rousseau Social Contract 001 + Mill On Liberty 001 as liberal-democratic foundational principles for legitimate state authority.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Politics 001/002 (substantive opposition — natural-political vs liberal-restricted-state)**: Aristotle's naturalism about political society (Politics 001) and participation-citizenship (Politics 002) treat political community as constitutive of human flourishing; Mill's Harm Principle restricts the political to harm-prevention, treating individual liberty as the default. The Aristotle-vs-Mill contrast on the *role of political community* in human life is substantive. **Eighteenth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Aristotle Politics 001/002 (political community as constitutive of human flourishing) vs Mill On Liberty 001 (political community restricted to harm-prevention; individual liberty as default).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hobbes Leviathan 001/002 (substantive opposition — sovereign-absolute vs sovereign-restricted)**: Hobbes's sovereign has absolute power via covenant; Mill's sovereign is restricted to harm-prevention. The Hobbes-vs-Mill contrast on the *scope of legitimate state power* is foundational to the modern political-philosophy debate. **Nineteenth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Hobbes Leviathan 001/002 (sovereign-absolute) vs Mill On Liberty 001 (sovereign-restricted-to-harm-prevention).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Republic 001/002 (substantive opposition — paternalist-class-functional vs liberal-individual)**: Plato's Republic implements a *paternalist* class-functional polis (each class assigned proper role; the philosopher-kings rule for the good of all); Mill's Harm Principle is *anti-paternalist* (coercion-for-one's-own-good is excluded). **Twentieth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Plato Republic 001/002 (paternalist class-functional politics) vs Mill On Liberty 001 (anti-paternalist liberal politics).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (substantive opposition — egalitarian liberalism vs aristocratic radicalism)**: Nietzsche's Genealogy critique of slave morality and the will-to-nothingness applies *directly* to liberal-democratic ideals (Nietzsche reads liberal egalitarianism as a high-philosophical form of slave morality). Mill's harm-principle-with-equal-liberty-for-all is, in Nietzschean terms, the consummation of slave-morality victory. **Twenty-first corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Mill On Liberty 001 (egalitarian liberalism) vs Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (aristocratic radicalism critiquing egalitarianism as slave-morality).

**Political-philosophy cluster now at 12 extractions across 5 authors**: Plato 3 + Aristotle 2 + Hobbes 2 + Rousseau 1 + Mill 1. The cluster gains a fifth author with Mill On Liberty 001 — first 5-author cluster in the corpus. The cluster's structural feature: most positions oppose each other (Plato vs Aristotle; Hobbes vs Mill; Aristotle vs Mill; etc.) with only partial alliances (Rousseau-Mill liberal-democratic affinity).

**Reception**: Mill's Harm Principle is the foundational text of modern liberalism. Rawls's *A Theory of Justice* (1971) systematizes the liberal harm-principle framework via the original position. Berlin's 'Two Concepts of Liberty' (1958) distinguishes negative liberty (Mill's domain) from positive liberty. Nozick's *Anarchy, State, and Utopia* (1974) develops a libertarian-Millian framework. Critiques: Devlin's *The Enforcement of Morals* (1965) defends moralistic state intervention against the Harm Principle; communitarian critics (Sandel, Taylor, MacIntyre) argue the harm-principle's individualism is too thin.
**Translation choices**: Not applicable — English original. Mill's italics not preserved in the PG plain-text source; encoded predicate naming uses standard Mixed-Case per corpus convention.

sourceExcerpt:
the free development of individuality is one of the
leading essentials of well-being
intent: Capture Mill's foundational individuality-as-essential-element-of-well-being claim from On Liberty Chapter III 'Of Individuality, as one of the Elements of Well-being' (lines 2503-2504): the free development of individuality is one of the leading essentials of well-being. This is the **canonical Millian individuality-as-essential thesis** — Mill's positive complement to the Harm Principle's negative restriction on state coercion (Mill 001). On Liberty 001 (Harm Principle) restricts the *what state may legitimately coerce*; On Liberty 002 (individuality-as-essential — this) provides the *why liberty matters* — individuality is intrinsically valuable, not merely instrumentally useful. **Second On Liberty extraction**; **Mill now at 3 extractions / 2 works**: Mill On Liberty × 2 (Harm Principle + individuality) + Mill Utilitarianism × 1 (Greatest Happiness Principle).
extractorNote: **Second Mill On Liberty extraction** — Mill now at **3 extractions / 2 works**: Mill Utilitarianism × 1 (Greatest Happiness Principle) + Mill On Liberty × 2 (Harm Principle + individuality-as-essential — this). Mill joins **Hume + Kant + Brandom at multi-work-three-extraction tier** (multi-work depth though within-work distribution differs). **Eighteenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**: Mill On Liberty 001 (Ch. I negative-restriction Harm Principle) + Mill On Liberty 002 (Ch. III positive-value individuality-as-essential — this) = *negative-restriction + positive-value pair* — substantively complementary. **Mill within-work pair captures the Harm-Principle-protects-Individuality structural relation**. **Thirty-fifth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle NE 002 + Mill On Liberty 002 on flourishing-requires-distinctively-human-faculty-exercise (Aristotle: reason-activity; Mill: free-individuality-development). **Thirty-five corpus alliances now documented**. **Mill-Nietzsche opposition deepens**: Mill On Liberty 002 (egalitarian individuality-celebration) joins Mill Utilitarianism 001 + Mill On Liberty 001 on the Mill side vs Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (aristocratic individuality-celebration) on the Nietzsche side. The Mill-vs-Nietzsche meta-ethical-individuality opposition is now substantively multi-extension-grounded. **No new conceptual opposition pair this iteration**: Mill 002 substantively *deepens* existing oppositions and alliances rather than adding new ones. Sixtieth fol extraction; fol now at 60 of 86 = 70%. **fol crosses 70% milestone**. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the second-Mill-On-Liberty + Mill-at-3-extractions-2-works + eighteenth-same-work-pair + Mill-within-work-pair-Harm-Principle-protects-Individuality + thirty-fifth-alliance + Mill-Nietzsche-opposition-multi-extension + fol-70%-milestone milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The claim has clean A-form shape ('All well-being is individuality-essential'). Both predicates are unary. Term logic could carry it cleanly. fol preferred for the relational structure of the essential-element predicate.

Mill Chapter III develops the individuality-as-essential argument across many paragraphs (von Humboldt quotation, customary-character critique, experiments-of-living defense). A nd extraction could carry the multi-step argument. The fol extraction captures the *thesis* directly.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *On Liberty* (1859), Chapter III 'Of Individuality, as One of the Elements of Well-being'. Project Gutenberg #34901. Lines 2503-2504.

**Predicate naming**: `WellBeing(w)` for the Millian (and broader utilitarian) technical term — *welfare*, *happiness*, *flourishing*; `HasFreeDevelopmentOfIndividualityAsEssentialElement(w)` for the constitutive-essential relation (individuality is *not merely instrumental* to well-being but is *part of* it).

**The conditional encoding**: captures Mill's *necessary-element* claim — well-being requires the free development of individuality as a constituent element, not merely as an instrumental means. Mill's argument is that *individuality is part of what well-being is*, not just a tool for achieving well-being.

**The relation to Mill Utilitarianism 001**: Mill's Utilitarianism (extracted as Utilitarianism 001 — Greatest Happiness Principle: Right ↔ TendsToPromoteHappiness) defines moral rightness in terms of *happiness-promotion*. On Liberty 002 (this) substantively *deepens* the utilitarian framework by identifying *individuality* as a constitutive element of well-being/happiness. The Mill strand's three extractions together cover: (1) ethics (Utilitarianism 001 — GHP); (2) politics (On Liberty 001 — Harm Principle); (3) the substantive content of well-being (On Liberty 002 — individuality as essential — this).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill Utilitarianism 001 (within-author across-work alignment deepened)**: Mill Utilitarianism 001 + On Liberty 001 + On Liberty 002 = **three-extraction Mill strand** covering utilitarian ethics + Harm Principle + individuality-as-essential. The three extractions are *jointly coherent*: utilitarianism grounds the ethical framework; Harm Principle restricts state coercion within the framework; individuality-as-essential identifies the substantive content of the happiness/well-being utilitarianism aims to maximize. **Mill joins multi-extraction author tier at 3 ext / 2 works** — same depth as Hume Treatise × 2 + Enquiry, and Kant Groundwork × 2 + Prolegomena × 2 / 4 ext / 2 works.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to On Liberty 001 (within-work same-author pair)**: Mill On Liberty 001 (Ch. I Harm Principle — negative restriction on state coercion) + Mill On Liberty 002 (Ch. III individuality-as-essential — positive value being protected by the Harm Principle). The two extractions are *substantively complementary*: the Harm Principle *protects* the conditions for the free development of individuality; individuality is *the substantive value* that justifies the Harm Principle. **Eighteenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern** (joins prior seventeen).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (substantive partial-opposition — Mill's egalitarian individuality vs Nietzschean aristocratic individual-development)**: Nietzsche Genealogy 001 (slave/aristocratic morality typology) critiques *herd morality* (Mill's egalitarian framework is, in Nietzsche's analysis, slave-morality-egalitarianism). Mill's individuality-as-essential thesis (this) is *egalitarian* — every individual's free development is essential. Nietzsche's individuality-celebration is *aristocratic* — only the *exceptional* individual matters. Partial-alliance on *individuality's value* + opposition on *which-individuals-matter*. The Mill-vs-Nietzsche opposition (twenty-first opposition from iter 46) is now substantively *deepened* with On Liberty 002 providing a third Mill extraction on the individuality-celebration side.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 002 (substantive partial-alliance — both treat human flourishing as requiring activity of distinctively-human faculties)**: Aristotle NE 002 (function-argument: good of man = working of soul with reason) treats *distinctively-rational activity* as the essential element of human flourishing. Mill On Liberty 002 (this) treats *free development of individuality* as the essential element of well-being. Both philosophers treat well-being as requiring the *active exercise* of distinctively-human capacities (Aristotle: reason; Mill: individuality). Substantive partial-alliance. **Thirty-fifth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle NE 002 + Mill On Liberty 002 on flourishing-requires-distinctively-human-faculty-exercise.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Rousseau Social Contract 001 (substantive partial-alliance deepened — liberal-democratic individuality protection)**: Rousseau Social Contract 001 (convention-only legitimate political authority) and Mill's individuality-as-essential (this) both ground *liberal-democratic* frameworks. The tenth alliance (Rousseau + Mill On Liberty 001 on contractualist-liberal foundations from iter 58) is now substantively *deepened* with Mill On Liberty 002 — both Rousseau and Mill identify *individual freedom and development* as substantive values requiring institutional protection.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hegel Phenomenology 002 (substantive partial-opposition — Hegelian intersubjective-recognition vs Millian individual-development)**: Hegel Phen 002 (mutual recognition — self-consciousness requires the other) emphasizes the *intersubjective* constitution of self. Mill On Liberty 002 (this) emphasizes the *individual* free development. The Hegel-Mill contrast on individual-vs-intersubjective self-constitution is substantive. Partial-opposition (Hegel doesn't deny individual development; Mill doesn't deny social context — but their *priority* differs).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Marcus Aurelius 003 (substantive partial-alliance — both rational-self-development as essential)**: Marcus Aurelius 003 (Stoic cosmopolitan telos — rational beings have cosmic-polity-alignment as end) treats *rational self-development* as essential to the telos of rational beings. Mill On Liberty 002 (this) treats *free individual development* as essential to well-being. Both philosophers treat *self-development* as essential, though Marcus's is cosmic-rational-alignment-oriented and Mill's is individual-self-determination-oriented. Substantive partial-alliance on self-development-as-essential.

**Mill becomes third author with three-extraction multi-work coverage at maximum depth (3 ext / 2 works)**: Hume (3 + 1 = 4 ext / 3 works), Kant (2 + 2 = 4 ext / 2 works), Mill (1 + 2 = 3 ext / 2 works — this iteration brings Mill to this tier). Mill's depth-distribution: Mill Utilitarianism × 1 + Mill On Liberty × 2 = 3 / 2 = bimodal at 2+1.

**Reception**: Mill's individuality-as-essential thesis is the foundational text of modern liberal-individualist political-ethical theory. von Humboldt's *The Sphere and Duties of Government* (cited at line 2528 — 'individuality of power and development') is Mill's explicit source. Modern revival via Berlin's *Two Concepts of Liberty* (1958), Rawls's primary-goods framework, Nussbaum's *Capabilities Approach*. Critics from communitarianism (MacIntyre, Sandel, Taylor) argue the individualist priority is one-sided; perfectionist liberalism (Raz) defends a deeper individualist framework. Contemporary liberal-political-philosophy continues to engage with the Millian framework.

sourceExcerpt:
All silencing of discussion is an assumption
of infallibility
intent: Capture Mill's iconic free-speech claim from On Liberty Chapter II 'Of the Liberty of Thought and Discussion' (line 1038): all silencing of discussion is an assumption of infallibility. This is the **canonical Millian free-speech principle** — the *epistemic* argument against censorship and silencing. Mill's argument: silencing an opinion requires asserting the opinion is false; asserting false-without-acknowledging-fallibility presupposes infallibility; therefore *any* silencing presupposes infallibility (which no human or institution has). **Third On Liberty extraction**; **Mill On Liberty now at 3 ext / 1 work** — **fourteenth author at within-work three-extraction tier**. The Mill On Liberty coverage now substantively spans the *three foundational On Liberty claims*: (1) negative-restriction Harm Principle (Mill 001 Ch. I); (2) positive-value individuality-as-essential (Mill 002 Ch. III); (3) epistemic-argument silencing-assumes-infallibility (Mill 003 Ch. II — this).

assessments.reason: Chapter II builds the silencing-assumes-infallibility argument across multiple paragraphs (lines 1010-1190+): silencing requires asserting falsehood of silenced opinion → asserting requires certainty → certainty without acknowledged-fallibility = infallibility-assumption. A nd extraction could carry the multi-step argument. The fol extraction captures the conclusion-thesis.
The claim has clean A-form shape ('All silencing-of-discussion acts are infallibility-assuming acts'). Both predicates are unary. Term logic could carry it directly. fol preferred only marginally.

reviewerNotes: Source: *On Liberty* (1859), Chapter II 'Of the Liberty of Thought and Discussion' (line 1038). Project Gutenberg #34901.

**Predicate naming**: `SilencingOfDiscussion(a)` for any act of silencing discussion (state censorship, social-coercive suppression, etc.); `AssumesInfallibility(a)` for the meta-epistemic presupposition (the act treats *some judge* — the silencing-agent or its authority-source — as infallible-about-the-silenced-claim).

**The conditional encoding**: captures Mill's universal-conditional claim. Every silencing-act *necessarily* assumes infallibility because: silencing presupposes the silenced-opinion is false-and-not-worth-considering; this presupposition without acknowledged-fallibility = infallibility-assumption. Mill explicitly says 'All silencing of discussion is an assumption of infallibility' — universally, not conditionally on specific circumstances.

**The wider Chapter II argument structure** (lines 1010-1024 preceding the encoded thesis): Mill identifies the 'peculiar evil of silencing the expression of an opinion' — it *robs the human race* of either (a) the opportunity to exchange error for truth (if silenced opinion is true) or (b) the clearer perception of truth via collision with error (if silenced opinion is false). The two-hypothesis argument (Mill calls them 'two distinct branches of the argument') develops the silencing-evil claim. Mill 003 extracts the *meta-epistemic* infallibility-assumption claim (line 1038) as the *load-bearing* meta-principle that underwrites the whole free-speech argument.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill On Liberty 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage)**: Mill On Liberty 001 (Ch. I Harm Principle — negative restriction on state coercion) + Mill On Liberty 002 (Ch. III individuality-as-essential — positive value) + Mill On Liberty 003 (Ch. II silencing-assumes-infallibility — epistemic argument — this). Three On Liberty extractions covering **the three foundational On Liberty claims**:
- Ch. I (negative-political restriction): Harm Principle.
- Ch. II (epistemic free-speech): silencing-assumes-infallibility.
- Ch. III (positive substantive value): individuality-as-essential.

**Eighteenth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage** (joins prior seventeen). The Mill On Liberty 001-003 coverage substantively spans the work's *three load-bearing argumentative pillars*: political-restriction + epistemic-restriction + substantive-positive-value.

**Mill On Liberty joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama, Wittgenstein, Russell, Locke, Plato (Republic), Brandom, Mill (On Liberty — this). **Fourteen authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. The within-work-three-extraction-author-tier expansion has now extended to 14 authors with one new addition in this iteration.

**Mill three-extraction within-work substantive coherence**: Mill On Liberty 001 + 002 + 003 substantively cover the work's *complete argumentative-structural framework*. The three Mill On Liberty extractions together capture: (1) *what* the state may legitimately do (Harm Principle); (2) *why* the state may not do otherwise (epistemic-fallibility argument); (3) *for what* — what substantive value the restriction protects (individuality-as-essential). Substantive structural-architectural coverage of one work's complete philosophical framework.

**Mill total**: now at **4 extractions across 2 works**: Mill Utilitarianism × 1 (Greatest Happiness Principle — ethical framework) + Mill On Liberty × 3 (Harm Principle + epistemic-fallibility + individuality — political framework). Mill's depth-distribution: 1 + 3 = 4 / 2 = matches Kant (2+2=4/2). Mill joins **Kant + Hume + Brandom at 4-or-more-extraction multi-work-multi-extraction tier**.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Brandom on-the-structure 003 (substantive alliance — both normative-deontic frameworks for rational discursive practice)**: Brandom 003 (incompatibility as commitment-precluding-entitlement — discursive practice constituted by deontic-normative commitment-entitlement attribution) and Mill On Liberty 003 (silencing-assumes-infallibility — discursive practice requires fallibilist epistemic stance) both treat *rational discursive practice* as essentially *deontic-normative*. Brandom's framework grounds discourse in commitment-entitlement; Mill's framework requires discourse to acknowledge fallibility. Both frameworks treat *intersubjective discursive practice* as constituted by *normative-deontic-epistemic* commitments. **Thirty-sixth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Brandom on-the-structure 003 + Mill On Liberty 003 on rational-discursive-practice-as-normative-deontic-epistemic. The Brandom-Mill alliance bridges contemporary inferentialism with classical liberal epistemology.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Enquiry 001 (substantive partial-alliance — both fallibilist-empiricist epistemology)**: Hume's Fork (Enquiry 001) restricts knowledge to Relations of Ideas and Matters of Fact, both with acknowledged-fallibility. Mill On Liberty 003 (this) requires acknowledged-fallibility as the meta-epistemic condition for legitimate discursive restriction. Both philosophers' frameworks rest on *fallibilism* — no claim to absolute infallibility. Substantive partial-alliance on fallibilist-epistemology.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Theaetetus 001 + Republic 002/003 (substantive opposition — Platonic philosopher-king authority vs Millian fallibilism)**: Plato Republic 003 (philosopher-king thesis — rulers should have philosophical-nature) implicitly assumes that philosophers can *know* the truth and *rule based on this knowledge*. Mill On Liberty 003 (this) argues *no one* can claim infallibility — *all* silencing assumes infallibility. The Plato-vs-Mill opposition (twentieth opposition from iter 58) is now substantively *deepened* with Mill 003 providing a third Mill extraction on the anti-paternalist-fallibilist side. The Plato Republic × 3 vs Mill On Liberty × 3 opposition is now **3-vs-3 multi-extension-grounded**.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Russell Problems 003 (substantive alliance — both rational-cognition requires fallibilist-epistemic-humility)**: Russell's principle of induction (Problems 003) acknowledges that induction cannot be proven from experience — accepts it as substantive a priori while acknowledging the limitation. Mill's silencing-assumes-infallibility (this) requires similar fallibilist-epistemic-humility about *any* claim to silencing-justifying-certainty. Substantive partial-alliance on fallibilist-rational-cognition. **Thirty-seventh corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Russell Problems 003 + Mill On Liberty 003 on fallibilist-rational-cognition-requiring-epistemic-humility.

**Reception**: Mill's silencing-assumes-infallibility is the foundational text of modern free-speech / open-society epistemology. Popper's *The Open Society and Its Enemies* (1945) explicitly develops the Millian fallibilist epistemology into the *critical-rationalist* framework. Justice Holmes's free-speech dissents (*Abrams v. United States* 1919) explicitly invoke the Millian marketplace-of-ideas framework. Contemporary free-speech theory (Schauer, Sunstein) continues to engage with the Millian argument.

sourceExcerpt:
The creed which accepts as the foundation of morals, Utility, or the
Greatest Happiness Principle, holds that actions are right in proportion
as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the
reverse of happiness.
intent: Capture the Greatest Happiness Principle from Utilitarianism Chapter II (lines 263-267) — the foundational consequentialist commitment that actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce its reverse. The corpus's first Mill extraction; first consequentialist-ethics extraction. Pairs with Kant 001 (deontological FUL, just extracted in iteration 15) to give the corpus its first explicit ethics-vs-ethics comparison across modern traditions.
extractorNote: Corpus's **first Mill extraction** and **first consequentialist-ethics extraction**. Pairs directly with Kant 001 (Formula of Universal Law, just extracted in iteration 15) to give the corpus its first explicit **ethics-vs-ethics cross-traditional comparison**: deontological (Kant — rightness depends on maxim form) vs consequentialist (Mill — rightness depends on consequences). Both extractions use fol; both are bound to single quantified principles; both are foundational for their respective traditions. The same-formalism cross-traditional pair is a candidate worth flagging as a recurring pattern — the corpus is now building several such pairs (Hume↔Kant on empiricism/transcendentalism, Spinoza↔Descartes on rationalist-axiomatic/rationalist-skeptical, Kant↔Mill on deontology/consequentialism), each anchored at both ends by single extracted principles. The 'in proportion as' graded reading is flattened to a strict biconditional per the corpus convention for fol-flattening of dispositional/modal nuances (analogous to De Int 001's πεφυκός flattening and Hobbes 001's temporal flattening). Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria — the milestone goes in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The Greatest Happiness Principle is *stated* in this passage, not derived from premises. Mill's argument *for* the principle is in Chapter IV (the 'proof of utility' — that happiness is desirable because it is desired, etc.), well outside this span. A future nd extraction of Chapter IV's proof would carry the derivation; this extraction encodes the principle itself.
assessments.distortionRisk: An nd extraction confined to this passage would have empty premises and the GHP as the conclusion — degenerate. The principle is foundational/stipulative within Chapter II's exposition.

assessments.reason: Could be cast as two A-form-like biconditionals between predicates. Term logic carries A/E/I/O propositions about predicate distribution; the GHP's substantive content is the action-to-consequence proportion, which term logic flattens to bare predicate equivalence.
Not dialogical. Mill's exposition is treatise prose, even though it explicitly responds to anticipated objections in subsequent paragraphs.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Utilitarianism* (1863), Chapter II 'What Utilitarianism Is'. PG #11224. Mill wrote in English; no translator. Mill's qualified utilitarianism distinguishes higher from lower pleasures (Chapter II, against Bentham's quantitative calculus), provides the famous 'proof of utility' (Chapter IV), and relates justice to utility (Chapter V).

**Predicate naming**: `Right(a)` and `Wrong(a)` are normative-evaluative predicates of actions; `TendsToPromoteHappiness(a)` and `TendsToProduceUnhappiness(a)` are dispositional-consequential predicates. The four predicates encode Mill's two-sided characterization (right ⇔ promotes happiness; wrong ⇔ produces unhappiness).

**The 'in proportion as' qualifier** is philosophically load-bearing but is **flattened to a strict biconditional** in the fol encoding. Mill's phrasing 'right *in proportion as* they tend to promote happiness' suggests a graded / matter-of-degree reading: a more happiness-promoting action is *more* right; a less happiness-promoting action is *less* right. The fol encoding ∀a. Right(a) ↔ TendsToPromoteHappiness(a) is a binary biconditional that loses the gradation. A graded-logic or fuzzy-set encoding would carry the proportionality; the corpus has no such mirror. Documenting the flattening here per standard practice (analogous to the dispositional-flattening note for πεφυκός in De Int 001).

**The happiness definition** at lines 266-267 ('By happiness is intended pleasure, and the absence of pain; by unhappiness, pain, and the privation of pleasure') is a separate definitional move and is kept in reviewer notes rather than folded into the primary formula. The dual definitions:
  - ∀x. Happiness(x) ↔ (Pleasure(x) ∧ ¬Pain(x))
  - ∀x. Unhappiness(x) ↔ (Pain(x) ∧ ¬Pleasure(x))
  These could be encoded as a separate fol extraction (Mill 002 candidate) or as additional conjuncts in the present formula; kept separate for formula-tightness per the existing convention.

**Cross-paragraph dependency** on Chapter IV's 'proof of utility' (the famous argument that happiness is desirable because it is desired, etc., outside this span). The GHP is the *principle* Mill defends; Chapter IV is the defence. 16th cross-span citation in spirit; not re-flagging.

**Direct contrast with Kant 001 (Categorical Imperative)**. Kant's deontological FUL: ∀a∀m. (PerformedBy(a, I) ∧ MaximOf(a, m)) → CanWillAsUniversalLaw(I, m) — rightness depends on the *form* of the maxim (universalizability), not its consequences. Mill's consequentialist GHP: ∀a. Right(a) ↔ TendsToPromoteHappiness(a) — rightness depends on the *consequences* (happiness-promotion), not the maxim's form. The two principles are explicit alternatives in normative ethics; this same-period (modern) and same-formalism (fol) pair gives the corpus its first cross-traditional comparison in normative ethics.

**Bentham as predecessor**: Mill attributes the term 'Greatest Happiness Principle' to Bentham (line 151 of source.txt — 'as Bentham latterly called it, the greatest happiness principle'). Bentham's *An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation* (1789) gives the quantitative-calculus version; Mill's *Utilitarianism* (1863) qualifies with the higher/lower-pleasures distinction. Bentham is not yet in the corpus; the canonical Bentham passage would be the second occupant of the consequentialist sub-strand if added.

**Reception**: the GHP is the foundational claim of consequentialism, the dominant normative-ethics tradition contesting Kantian deontology. G. E. Moore's *Principia Ethica* (1903) attacks the 'naturalistic fallacy' that Mill is sometimes accused of; Sidgwick's *The Methods of Ethics* (1874) systematizes utilitarian theory; modern utilitarianism (Singer, Parfit) builds directly on the Mill/Bentham foundation. The encoded biconditional captures the bare principle; the rich reception is reviewer-notes context.

**Translation choices**: none — English original. Mill's prose is characteristically 19th-century-Victorian-academic; the encoded predicate names use modern Mixed-Case (Right, Wrong, TendsToPromoteHappiness, TendsToProduceUnhappiness) per the corpus convention.

sourceExcerpt:
the sole evidence it is possible to produce that
anything is desirable, is that people do actually desire it
intent: Capture Mill's canonical 'proof of utility' from Utilitarianism Chapter IV ('Of what sort of Proof the Principle of Utility is Susceptible', lines 1372-1373): the sole evidence it is possible to produce that anything is desirable, is that people do actually desire it. This is the **canonical Millian sole-evidence-of-desirability thesis** — Mill's notorious 'proof' of utilitarianism by analogy with sense-perception (visible-iff-people-see; audible-iff-people-hear; therefore desirable-iff-people-actually-desire). This is the *most controversial* argument in Mill's utilitarianism, *the* source of G.E. Moore's *naturalistic fallacy* charge (*Principia Ethica* 1903, §13-14). Whether or not the argument succeeds, the *thesis* is canonical and substantive. **Second Mill Utilitarianism extraction**; **Mill now at 5 ext / 2 works**: Mill Utilitarianism × 2 + Mill On Liberty × 3.
extractorNote: **Second Mill Utilitarianism extraction** — Mill Utilitarianism now at **2 extractions / 1 work**; Mill total at **5 extractions across 2 works** (Utilitarianism × 2 + On Liberty × 3). **Mill at 5 ext / 2 works**: matches Brandom 5 ext (a-tune 3 + on-the-structure 2 pre-iter-87), but Brandom now at 7 ext / 2 works; Mill remains second-most-developed multi-work coverage after Brandom. **Mill substantively covers Mill's complete ethical-political-epistemological framework** with 5 extractions: ethics-foundation (Utilitarianism 001 — GHP) + ethics-grounding (Utilitarianism 002 — proof-of-utility — this) + politics-negative-restriction (On Liberty 001 — Harm Principle) + politics-epistemic-argument (On Liberty 003 — silencing-assumes-infallibility) + politics-positive-value (On Liberty 002 — individuality-as-essential). **Nineteenth corpus same-work different-span pair pattern**: Mill Utilitarianism 001 (Ch. II — GHP definitional) + Mill Utilitarianism 002 (Ch. IV — proof-of-utility epistemological) = substantively complementary chapters within Utilitarianism. **One new corpus conceptual opposition pair**: (58) Mill Utilitarianism 002 (proof-of-utility — desirability follows from actual-desire) vs implicit Hume Treatise III.1.1 (is-ought-gap — no derivation-of-ought-from-is); the Mill-vs-Hume is-ought opposition is now substantively *pending* (Hume Treatise III.1.1 not yet extracted but in corpus). **One new corpus alliance**: (47) Aristotle NE 001 + Mill Utilitarianism 002 on actual-human-pursuit-as-evidence-of-the-good (Aristotle's teleological + Mill's empirical-naturalistic both treat pursuit-as-evidence-of-good). **Forty-seven corpus alliances now documented**. **Fifty-eight conceptual oppositions now in corpus**. **Future-pending opposition**: G.E. Moore naturalistic-fallacy critique of Mill 002 — not yet extracted as Moore not yet in corpus, but Mill 002 is *the* canonical naturalistic-fallacy subject. Sixty-sixth fol extraction; fol now at 66 of 92 = 72%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the second-Mill-Utilitarianism + Mill-at-5-ext-2-works + Mill-second-most-developed-multi-work-coverage + Mill-substantively-covers-complete-framework + nineteenth-same-work-pair + Mill-vs-Hume-is-ought-opposition + Aristotle-NE-001-Mill-Utilitarianism-002-alliance + fol-at-72% milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: Biconditional ('All evidence-of-desirability is people-actually-desiring; vice versa'). Term-logic could carry the bi-direction as two A-forms; fol preferred for the explicit biconditional structure.

Mill's Chapter IV develops the proof-of-utility via *analogy* (visible → people see it; audible → people hear it; desirable → people desire it). A nd extraction could carry the analogical-inferential structure. The fol extraction captures the *conclusion-thesis* directly.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Utilitarianism* (1861), Chapter IV. Project Gutenberg #11224. Lines 1372-1373.

**Predicate naming**: `EvidenceOfDesirability(x)` for the meta-evidential property of *being-able-to-produce-evidence-that-x-is-desirable* (Mill's argument is *about* the evidential basis of desirability-claims); `PeopleActuallyDesire(x)` for the empirical-psychological fact of *actual desiring-by-people* (the contingent psychological-empirical fact).

**The biconditional encoding**: captures Mill's *sole-evidence* claim. The biconditional reflects Mill's *exclusivity*: people-actually-desiring-x is *the only* evidence-of-desirability-of-x. Any candidate evidence of x's desirability *must reduce* to (= consists of) people actually desiring x. Conversely, if people actually desire x, that *is* evidence of x's desirability. The biconditional is *exclusive-evidential*. Note: Moore's naturalistic-fallacy critique (*Principia Ethica* §13-14) argues that this biconditional commits an *evidence-vs-constitution* category-error — even if people-actually-desiring is *evidence-of-being-desirable*, it is not *what desirability is*. The encoding preserves the substantive Millian thesis while making the controversy visible.

**The wider Chapter IV argument** (lines 1369-1384): Mill's argument is by analogy with sense-perception: visible = people-actually-see; audible = people-actually-hear; therefore desirable = people-actually-desire. Each person desires *their own happiness* (psychological fact); aggregate-happiness is the *sum* of each-person-happiness; therefore aggregate-happiness is *desirable for the aggregate*. Mill explicitly states (lines 1378-1382): 'No reason can be given why the general happiness is desirable, except that each person, so far as he believes it to be attainable, desires his own happiness... we have... all the proof which the case admits of, but all which it is possible to require, that happiness is a good.'

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill Utilitarianism 001 (within-work two-extraction coverage)**: Mill Utilitarianism extractions:
- Mill Utilitarianism 001 (lines 263-267, fol): Greatest Happiness Principle definitional formulation (Ch. II): actions are right ↔ they tend to promote happiness.
- Mill Utilitarianism 002 (lines 1372-1373, fol — this): proof-of-utility: evidence-of-desirability ↔ people actually desire it (Ch. IV).

**Mill Utilitarianism 001 + 002 substantively cover the work's two foundational claims**: (1) the *definitional formulation* (Greatest Happiness Principle — Ch. II) + (2) the *epistemological grounding* (proof-of-utility via desire — Ch. IV). The two extractions are *substantively complementary*: Utilitarianism 001 *defines* utility; Utilitarianism 002 *grounds* the definition epistemologically. **Nineteenth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**.

**Mill now at 5 extractions across 2 works**: Mill Utilitarianism × 2 (Greatest Happiness Principle + proof-of-utility) + Mill On Liberty × 3 (Harm Principle + individuality-as-essential + silencing-assumes-infallibility). Mill's depth-distribution: 2 + 3 = 5 / 2 works. **Mill joins the multi-work-multi-three-or-more-extraction tier**: previously this tier had 2 authors (Brandom 4+3, Aristotle 4+4 — both works at within-work three-or-more-extraction depth). Mill at 2+3 has *one* work at within-work three-extraction depth (On Liberty); Utilitarianism at 2 ext is at within-work-two-extraction depth, *not* three-or-more. **So Mill does NOT yet join the multi-work-multi-three-or-more-extraction tier** — Mill remains at multi-work-multi-extraction tier (4+ ext, 2+ works) with 5 ext / 2 works, but Utilitarianism still needs one more extraction to reach within-work-three-extraction depth.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill On Liberty 001/002/003 (within-author across-work coverage at 5 extractions across 2 works)**: Mill strand is now at **5 extractions across 2 works** — joining Hume (4 / 3 works), Kant (4 / 2 works) at multi-work-multi-extraction depth; Mill's 5 ext is more than Kant/Hume's 4 ext but less than Brandom's 7 ext. **Mill's substantive coverage spans Mill's complete ethical-political-epistemological framework**:
- *Ethics-foundation* (Utilitarianism 001 — GHP)
- *Ethics-grounding* (Utilitarianism 002 — proof-of-utility — this)
- *Politics-negative-restriction* (On Liberty 001 — Harm Principle)
- *Politics-epistemic-argument* (On Liberty 003 — silencing-assumes-infallibility)
- *Politics-positive-value* (On Liberty 002 — individuality-as-essential)

The 5-extraction Mill strand substantively covers Mill's complete philosophical framework.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to G.E. Moore framework — first canonical 'naturalistic fallacy' subject in the corpus**: Mill Utilitarianism 002 (this) is *the* canonical text that G.E. Moore's *Principia Ethica* (1903) §13-14 attacks as the 'naturalistic fallacy'. The corpus does *not yet* have Moore extractions (Moore is on the roadmap but not yet collected). When Moore is added, the Moore-vs-Mill naturalistic-fallacy opposition will be substantively grounded. *Pending future Moore extraction*.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Enquiry 001 + Treatise 001 (substantive partial-alliance — empiricist-naturalistic-ethical-epistemology)**: Hume's *is-ought* gap (*Treatise* III.1.1 — derivation-of-ought-from-is-is-illegitimate) is *historically prior* to Mill's proof-of-utility and *substantively-tense* with it. Mill's proof-of-utility *derives* desirability-as-evidence-from-actual-desire, which is *precisely* a derivation-of-ought-from-is. However, Mill is also *empiricist-naturalistic-ethical-epistemologist* in the Humean tradition — both philosophers ground ethics in psychological-empirical facts. Substantive partial-alliance + partial-opposition. **Fifty-eighth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Mill Utilitarianism 002 (proof-of-utility — desirability *follows from* actual-desire) vs implicit Hume *Treatise* III.1.1 (is-ought-gap — no derivation-of-ought-from-is). *Hume Treatise III.1.1 is in the corpus but the is-ought-gap is not yet extracted; would be substantive subject for future Hume Treatise extraction*.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 001 (substantive partial-alliance — every-art-aims-at-some-good + Mill's desire-as-evidence-of-good)**: Aristotle NE 001 (every art and every inquiry aims at some good) treats *aim-at-the-good* as constitutive of human activity. Mill Utilitarianism 002 (this) treats *actual-desire* as evidence-of-the-desirable. Both philosophers treat *desire/aim* as evidentially-tied-to-the-good. Substantive partial-alliance: Aristotle's *teleological* ethics + Mill's *empirical-naturalistic* ethics both treat *human-pursuit* as evidence of the *good*. **Forty-seventh corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle NE 001 + Mill Utilitarianism 002 on actual-human-pursuit-as-evidence-of-the-good.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 002 (substantive partial-alliance — function-argument + Mill's proof-of-utility both grounded in human-nature-facts)**: Aristotle NE 002 (function-argument: good of man = working of soul with reason, grounded in human-rational-nature) treats *human-distinctive-rational-activity* as the *naturalistic basis* of ethical evaluation. Mill Utilitarianism 002 (this) treats *human-actually-desiring* as the naturalistic basis. Both philosophers are *naturalistic-ethical-grounding* in human-nature-facts (Aristotle: rational-activity; Mill: actual-desire). Substantive partial-alliance.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Bentham 001 implicit (corpus does not yet have Bentham extractions)**: Mill Utilitarianism 002 substantively extends Bentham's *quantitative-hedonistic-calculus* with the *qualitative-distinction-among-pleasures* (Ch. II, lines 313-327 — extracted in Mill Utilitarianism 001 context). When Bentham is added, the Bentham-vs-Mill within-utilitarian distinction will be substantively grounded.

**Reception**: Mill Utilitarianism Ch. IV proof-of-utility is the most-attacked argument in the utilitarian canon. G.E. Moore's *Principia Ethica* (1903) §13-14 attacks the argument as the *naturalistic fallacy*: from 'people desire X' (psychological fact) to 'X is desirable / good' (normative claim) is an illegitimate inference. Subsequent defenders of Mill (Sidgwick, Hare, Crisp) argue that Mill's argument is *not* committing the fallacy — Mill is *not* defining 'good' as 'what is desired' but offering *evidence* (and evidence-vs-definition is the relevant distinction). Contemporary metaethics (Cornell realism, expressivism, error theory) continues to engage with the Millian framework. **Reception of Mill's argument is mostly negative** but the argument is canonical and foundational to subsequent metaethical-realism debates.

sourceExcerpt:
It is better to be a human
being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates
dissatisfied than a fool satisfied
intent: Capture Mill's iconic *better-to-be-Socrates-dissatisfied-than-fool-satisfied* claim from Utilitarianism Chapter II (lines 377-379): it is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied. This is the **canonical Millian qualitative-hedonism thesis** — Mill's *radical break* with Benthamite quantitative-hedonism. Bentham (*Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation* 1789): pleasures differ only in *quantity* (intensity, duration, certainty, propinquity, fecundity, purity, extent). Mill (Utilitarianism Ch. II): pleasures *also* differ in *quality* — some are intrinsically superior to others, even controlling for quantity. The Socrates-dissatisfied/fool-satisfied comparison is *the* iconic statement of qualitative-hedonism. **Third Mill Utilitarianism extraction**; **Mill Utilitarianism now at 3 ext / 1 work — joins within-work three-extraction tier**. **Mill is now uniquely the THIRD author at within-work-three-or-more-extraction across TWO distinct works** (joining Brandom and Aristotle).
extractorNote: **Third Mill Utilitarianism extraction** — Mill Utilitarianism now at **3 extractions / 1 work** — joins within-work three-extraction tier. **17th author at within-work-three-extraction-tier**. **Mill now uniquely the THIRD author at within-work-three-or-more-extraction across TWO distinct works** (joining Brandom and Aristotle): Mill Utilitarianism × 3 + Mill On Liberty × 3 = 6 ext / 2 works with uniquely-balanced 3+3 distribution. **Multi-work-multi-three-or-more-extraction tier now has 3 authors**: Brandom (4+3=7), Aristotle (4+4=8 for the two within-work-four-extraction works, total 13/6), Mill (3+3=6 — this iteration). **Twenty-third corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**: Mill Utilitarianism 001-003 covers complete utilitarian framework (definitional + epistemological-grounding + axiological-qualitative). **Mill 6-extraction substantive coverage of Mill's complete philosophical framework**: ethics-foundation + ethics-grounding + ethics-axiology + politics-negative-restriction + politics-epistemic-argument + politics-positive-value. **One new corpus conceptual opposition pair**: (59) Mill Utilitarianism 003 (Socrates-dissatisfied as substantively superior) vs Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (Socratic-rational-dissatisfaction as life-denying decadence). The Mill-vs-Nietzsche opposition now substantively deepened with explicit Socratic-rationality-as-better commitment. **Two new corpus alliances**: (48) Aristotle NE 002 + Mill Utilitarianism 003 on rational-higher-faculty-engagement-as-intrinsically-superior; (49) Plato Republic 003 + Mill Utilitarianism 003 on philosophical-life-as-substantively-best (notably creates *partial-alliance-within-opposition* with Plato — Plato-vs-Mill is opposed politically but partially-allied axiologically). **Forty-nine corpus alliances now documented**. **Fifty-nine conceptual oppositions now in corpus**. **Within-work intra-author substantive tension noted**: Mill Utilitarianism 002 (proof-of-utility — all desire evidence of desirable) vs Mill Utilitarianism 003 (qualitative-hedonism — higher desires substantively superior) — canonical Mill-scholarship tension, resolvable in Mill's framework via competent-judges-actual-preference. Sixty-seventh fol extraction; fol now at 67 of 93 = 72%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the third-Mill-Utilitarianism + Mill-at-6-ext-2-works + 17th-within-work-three-extraction-author + Mill-third-author-at-multi-work-multi-three-or-more-extraction-tier + twenty-third-three-or-more-extraction-coverage + Mill-Utilitarianism-001-003-complete-utilitarian-framework + Mill-Nietzsche-Socrates-opposition + Aristotle-NE-002-Mill-Utilitarianism-003-alliance + Plato-Republic-003-Mill-Utilitarianism-003-partial-alliance-within-opposition + within-work-intra-author-tension milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: Comparative-relational claim (BetterToBe is a 2-place predicate). Aristotelian term-logic does not handle 2-place relations cleanly. fol preferred.

Mill Ch. II develops the qualitative-hedonism argument across multiple paragraphs (lines 313-393): some pleasures are intrinsically superior; competent-judges-of-both-sides prefer higher; therefore *quality* matters not just *quantity*. A nd extraction could carry the argument-structure. The fol extraction captures the *canonical iconic statement* directly.
Not dialogical. Treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Utilitarianism* (1861), Chapter II. Project Gutenberg #11224. Lines 377-379.

**Predicate naming**: `HumanBeingDissatisfied(x)` / `PigSatisfied(x)` for the human-pig comparison (lower-faculties-pig vs higher-faculties-human-but-dissatisfied); `SocratesDissatisfied(x)` / `FoolSatisfied(x)` for the Socrates-fool comparison (higher-rational-faculties-Socrates vs lower-faculties-fool); `BetterToBe(a, b)` for the comparative-evaluation relation (being-in-state-a is better-to-be-in than being-in-state-b).

**The two-fold disjunctive-conjunctive encoding**: captures Mill's *two parallel examples* of the same qualitative-hedonism principle. Both examples instantiate the general principle: *higher-faculty-dissatisfied is better than lower-faculty-satisfied*. The disjunction in the antecedent reflects Mill's *parallel-rhetorical* presentation. The general principle (not explicitly captured by this fol but reflected in the two examples): *qualitative-differences-among-pleasures override quantitative-differences*.

**The general qualitative-hedonism principle** (Ch. II lines 313-327, not in the encoded line-range but the substantive context): 'It would be absurd that while, in estimating all other things, quality is considered as well as quantity, the estimation of pleasures should be supposed to depend on quantity alone.' Mill's argument: competent-judges-who-experience-both-kinds (higher and lower pleasures) *consistently prefer* the higher; this preference is *not explainable* by quantity alone; therefore quality matters intrinsically.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill Utilitarianism 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage — completes Mill's Utilitarianism architecture)**: Mill Utilitarianism extractions:
- Utilitarianism 001 (lines 263-267, fol): Greatest Happiness Principle definitional (Ch. II early statement) — *what* utility is
- Utilitarianism 002 (lines 1372-1373, fol): proof-of-utility — *how* utility is grounded
- Utilitarianism 003 (lines 377-379, fol — this): qualitative-hedonism Socrates-dissatisfied — *what kind of utility* matters (qualitative dimension)

The three Utilitarianism extractions substantively cover **Mill's complete utilitarian framework**: definitional (GHP) + epistemological-grounding (proof-of-utility) + axiological-qualitative (Socrates-dissatisfied). **Twenty-third corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**.

**Mill Utilitarianism joins within-work three-extraction author tier — 17th author at within-work-three-extraction-tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama, Wittgenstein, Russell, Locke, Plato (Republic), Brandom (on-the-structure), Mill (On Liberty), Brandom (A-Tune), Aristotle (Post. An.), Mill (Utilitarianism — this iteration). **Seventeen authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. Mill is now uniquely at the tier across *two distinct works* (Utilitarianism × 3 + On Liberty × 3).

**Mill now uniquely the THIRD author at within-work-three-or-more-extraction across TWO distinct works** (joining Brandom and Aristotle):
- Brandom: on-the-structure × 4 + a-tune × 3 = 7 / 2 works (within-work-three-or-more-extraction across two distinct works at depth 4+3)
- Aristotle: De Int × 4 + Post. An. × 4 = 8 / 2 works for these two; total Aristotle 13 / 6 (within-work-three-or-more-extraction across two distinct works at depth 4+4)
- Mill: Utilitarianism × 3 + On Liberty × 3 = 6 / 2 works (within-work-three-or-more-extraction across two distinct works at depth 3+3 — this iteration)

**Mill total now at 6 extractions across 2 works** with uniquely-balanced 3+3 distribution (matching Brandom on-the-structure-of-reasons 3+3 a-tune from iter 88 before on-the-structure became 4; Mill is now at the *Brandom-iter-88 distribution-pattern*). **Three authors at multi-work-multi-three-or-more-extraction tier**.

**Mill 6-extraction substantive substantively-complete coverage of Mill's philosophical framework**:
- *Ethics-foundation* (Utilitarianism 001 — GHP definitional)
- *Ethics-grounding* (Utilitarianism 002 — proof-of-utility epistemological)
- *Ethics-axiology* (Utilitarianism 003 — qualitative-hedonism Socrates-dissatisfied — this)
- *Politics-negative-restriction* (On Liberty 001 — Harm Principle)
- *Politics-epistemic-argument* (On Liberty 003 — silencing-assumes-infallibility)
- *Politics-positive-value* (On Liberty 002 — individuality-as-essential)

The Mill 6-extraction coverage substantively spans Mill's complete *ethical-axiological-political-epistemological* framework. **Mill's Utilitarianism × 3 substantively covers ethics's complete structure**: definitional (what) + grounding (why-true) + axiological (what-counts-as-substantively-good).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle NE 002 (substantive direct alliance — both qualitative-distinction-among-activities-and-pleasures)**: Aristotle NE 002 (function-argument: good of man = working of soul with reason, and pleasure-tied-to-distinctively-human-activity) treats *rational-distinctively-human activity* as the highest *kind* of human good. Mill Utilitarianism 003 (this — qualitative-hedonism Socrates-dissatisfied) treats *higher-faculty-engagement* (rational, intellectual) as intrinsically superior to *lower-faculty-engagement* (bodily). Both philosophers identify *higher-rational-faculty-engagement* as intrinsically superior. **Forty-eighth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle NE 002 + Mill Utilitarianism 003 on rational-higher-faculty-engagement-as-intrinsically-superior-form-of-good. The Aristotle-NE-002-Mill-Utilitarianism-002-alliance (47, from iter 92) is now substantively *deepened* — Mill 003 substantively extends the Aristotle-Mill convergence on rational-faculty-engagement.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Marcus Aurelius 003 (substantive partial-alliance — rational-self-development as essential)**: Marcus Aurelius 003 (Stoic cosmopolitan telos — rational beings have cosmic-polity-alignment as end) treats *rational self-development* as essential to the telos of rational beings. Mill Utilitarianism 003 (this — qualitative-hedonism with Socrates-dissatisfaction) treats *rational-higher-faculty-engagement* as substantively better even when accompanied by dissatisfaction. Both philosophers privilege *rational-faculty-engagement* over *mere-satisfaction*. Substantive partial-alliance.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Republic 003 (substantive partial-alliance — philosopher-king and Socrates-dissatisfied both valorize philosophical-rational-life)**: Plato Republic 003 (philosopher-king's philosophical-nature) treats *philosophical-life* as the highest form of human existence; Mill Utilitarianism 003 (this) endorses *Socrates dissatisfied* as better than *fool satisfied* — implicitly endorsing the *philosophical-life* even at the cost of comfort. The Plato-Mill convergence on *philosophical-life-as-substantively-best* is substantive (despite Mill's general liberal-egalitarian framework opposing Plato's political-aristocratic framework — see iter 87 3-vs-3 Plato Republic × 3 vs Mill On Liberty × 3 opposition). **Forty-ninth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Plato Republic 003 + Mill Utilitarianism 003 on philosophical-life-as-substantively-best-form-of-existence.

**Note**: this creates a *partial-alliance-within-opposition* between Plato and Mill — they oppose on the *political* framework (Plato authoritarian-rational, Mill liberal-egalitarian) but partially-ally on the *axiological* framework (both valorize philosophical-rational-life). The Plato-Mill relationship is substantively *multi-dimensional* in the corpus.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (substantive partial-opposition — Mill's higher-faculties-as-better vs Nietzschean transvaluation-of-higher)**: Nietzsche Genealogy 001 (slave/aristocratic morality typology) critiques *life-denying values* — Nietzsche treats *Socrates-as-paradigm-of-life-denying-rationality*. Mill Utilitarianism 003 (this — *Socrates dissatisfied* as better than *fool satisfied*) endorses *Socratic-rational-dissatisfaction* as substantively superior. The Mill-vs-Nietzsche opposition on the *valuation of Socratic rationality* is substantive: Mill endorses Socrates-dissatisfied as superior; Nietzsche treats Socratic dissatisfaction as *symptom* of life-denying decadence. **Fifty-ninth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Mill Utilitarianism 003 (Socrates-dissatisfied as substantively superior) vs Nietzsche Genealogy 001/002/003 (Socratic-rational-dissatisfaction as life-denying decadence). The Mill-vs-Nietzsche opposition (twenty-first opposition from iter 46) now substantively *deepens* with Mill 003 providing the explicit *Socratic-rationality-as-better* commitment.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Mill Utilitarianism 002 (within-work intra-author tension — qualitative-hedonism complicates proof-of-utility)**: Mill Utilitarianism 002 (proof-of-utility — evidence-of-desirability ↔ people-actually-desire) suggests *all desire is on a par* (any actually-desired thing is desirable). Mill Utilitarianism 003 (this — qualitative-hedonism with higher/lower distinction) requires that *some desires* (for higher pleasures) are *substantively more authoritative* than others. The within-work-intra-author *substantive tension* between Mill 002 and Mill 003 is canonically noted in Mill scholarship: how can Mill *both* hold that *all actual desire is equally evidence of desirability* (002) *and* that *higher-faculty desire is qualitatively superior* (003)? Mill's resolution (Ch. II): *competent-judges-who-experience-both* consistently prefer higher; this *competent-judge-actual-preference* satisfies the proof-of-utility framework. The within-work tension is *substantive but resolvable* in Mill's framework.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Wittgenstein PI 001 + Brandom on-the-structure 004 (meta-alliance — qualitative-distinctions presuppose practices)**: Wittgenstein PI 001 (meaning-as-use language-games) + Brandom on-the-structure 004 (GoGAR) both treat *practices* as constitutive of meaning/rationality. Mill Utilitarianism 003 (this) requires *practices-of-experiencing-and-judging-both-higher-and-lower-pleasures* to ground the qualitative-distinction (the *competent-judge* must have *participated in both kinds of practice*). Substantive meta-alliance bridging Mill's qualitative-hedonism and Wittgensteinian-Brandomian practice-foundations.

**Reception**: Mill's qualitative-hedonism is the canonical *non-Benthamite* utilitarian framework. The Bentham-vs-Mill within-utilitarian distinction is foundational to subsequent utilitarian thought (Sidgwick, Hare, Singer). Crisp 1997 *Mill on Utilitarianism* and Skorupski 1989 *John Stuart Mill* both treat the qualitative-hedonism as Mill's *most distinctive* contribution. The Socrates-dissatisfied claim is canonical in introductory ethics anthologies (Cahn, Cottingham). The within-Mill tension between 002 and 003 is widely discussed (Berger 1984 *Happiness, Justice, and Freedom*; Donner 1991 *The Liberal Self*).
