sourceExcerpt: PROP. XIV.  Besides God no substance can be granted or conceived.
intent: Capture Spinoza's pantheistic-monism proposition (Ethics I, PROP. XIV, lines 464-476): besides God no substance can be granted or conceived — therefore there is only *one* substance, namely God. The famous claim 'Deus sive Natura' (God or Nature) follows from this proposition together with the broader development of Part I. The proof is a reductio chaining three prior results: Def. vi (God is absolutely infinite, has all attributes), Prop. xi (God necessarily exists), Prop. v (no two substances share an attribute). **Second Spinoza extraction**; **second geometric-method extraction in the corpus**; **different-span same-formalism within-work pair** with Spinoza 001 (both Part I propositions, both nd-encoded chained geometric demonstrations). The pair documents Spinoza's deductive method across the early (Prop. III) and middle (Prop. XIV) of Part I.
extractorNote: **Second Spinoza extraction** — second Ethics extraction; second geometric-method extraction; eighth nd extraction in the corpus. **Within-work different-span same-formalism pair**: Spinoza 001 (Prop. III, negative-deductive-chain) + Spinoza 002 (Prop. XIV, reductio-elimination). The pair documents Spinoza's *More Geometrico* method across both modes (direct-chain demonstration and reductio elimination). Distinct from the same-maxim-different-route pair pattern (Marcus 001/002) — Spinoza 001/002 are *different propositions*, not different paths to the same conclusion. **Fifth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Spinoza one-substance monism (Ethics XIV) vs Leibniz monadological pluralism (Monadology 001 + Discourse 001). Five conceptual oppositions now in corpus: Aristotle-PNC vs Hegel; Leibniz vs Hegel; Aristotle-Politics vs Hobbes; Plato-Statesman vs Aristotle-Politics; Spinoza vs Leibniz. **First nd extraction with equality in the conclusion** — confirms the nd schema's ability to carry equality-bearing claims (validator OK). Rationalist tradition now at **five extractions**: Descartes 001 (nd) + 002 (epistemic); Spinoza 001 (nd) + 002 (this, nd); Leibniz Monadology 001 (fol) + Discourse 001 (fol) — three core rationalist authors, each at two extractions, with mixed-formalism strand depth. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the fifth conceptual opposition pair + first-equality-in-nd-conclusion + Spinoza-method-pair go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The conclusion alone (∀x. Substance(x) → x=God) is a single quantified equality and fits fol directly. The three-premise reductio structure (God has all attributes; substances have attributes; no two substances share an attribute) would collapse out. nd preserves the chained-citation geometric-demonstration structure that is the signature of Spinoza's *More Geometrico* method.

The conclusion involves equality (x=God) which term logic cannot express. The reductio structure also relies on existential quantification over attributes and the binary AttributeOf relation. Term-logic is structurally insufficient.
Not dialogical. Geometric-deductive treatise; no exchange.

reviewerNotes: Source: same Ethics Part I as Spinoza 001 (PG #3800, Elwes 1883 translation). Spinoza 001 extracted Prop. III (lines 121-127); Spinoza 002 extracts Prop. XIV (lines 464-476). The two propositions are eleven propositions apart in the same Part I deductive chain; Prop. XIV transitively depends on Prop. III (via Prop. v which builds on Prop. iv which builds on Prop. iii).

**Predicate naming**: `Substance(x)` (subject of inquiry); `Attribute(a)` (sortal for attributes); `AttributeOf(a, x)` (binary relation: attribute a belongs to substance x); `God` (constant for the unique substance). The Spinoza-specific vocabulary maps to standard Mixed-Case predicate names; the technical Latin terms (substantia, attributum, modus) are preserved as the English Substance/Attribute/Mode.

**The reductio structure**: assume some Substance(z) with z≠God; by P2 (substances have attributes), z has some attribute a; by P1 (God has all attributes), AttributeOf(a, God); so a is an attribute of both z and God; by P3 (no two substances share an attribute), z=God. Contradicts the assumption z≠God. Therefore no substance besides God can be granted.

**Premise 1 (God has all attributes)** condenses two cited steps: Def. vi (God is absolutely infinite, of whom no attribute is denied) and Prop. xi (God exists). The encoded P1 captures the operative content: every attribute is an attribute of God. The existence claim (Prop. xi) is implicit in the use of God as a constant — the proof assumes God's existence as given.

**Premise 2 (substances have attributes)** is implicit in Spinoza's definition of substance (Def. iii — substance is what is conceived through itself) combined with Def. iv (attribute is what intellect perceives of substance as constituting its essence). Spinoza's text doesn't explicitly cite this in Prop. XIV's proof but uses it implicitly when reasoning 'it would have to be explained by some attribute.'

**Premise 3 (no two substances share an attribute)** is Prop. v, cited explicitly in the proof. The encoded P3 captures the contrapositive: if two substances share an attribute, they are identical. Spinoza's Prop. v is itself a derived result from Prop. iv (distinguishability requires attribute-difference or modification-difference) plus the priority of substance over modes (Prop. i).

**nd Fitch ruleset is propositional only** (per schemas/nd.py docstring): the argument is quantified (∀-conditionals, ∃-existentials, equality), so the optional `proof` field is not included. Same convention as Meno 001, NE 002, Spinoza 001, Marcus Aurelius 001/002, Descartes 001, Hume 002 — all quantified or equality-bearing nd arguments without proof field.

**Use of equality in the conclusion**: ∀x. Substance(x) → x=God uses the AST's `eq` operator, expressing strict identity. This is **the second use of equality** in the corpus (the first was Meno 002's `AreaOf(SquareOn(DiagonalOf(s))) = 2·AreaOf(s)`); the first use of equality in an nd-encoded argument's conclusion. Equality use within nd's premises/conclusion is new for the corpus and confirms the nd schema can carry equality-bearing conclusions (the validator confirms this).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza 001 (within-work pair)**: Spinoza 001 (Prop. III, lines 121-127) extracted a *negative* claim derived via chained citation (no causation between things with nothing in common). Spinoza 002 (Prop. XIV, lines 464-476) extracts a *uniqueness* claim derived via chained reductio (only one substance, namely God). Both exemplify Spinoza's *More Geometrico* — chained-citation deductive demonstration. Same work, same formalism, different spans, different propositions, different inferential structures (direct chain vs reductio). The pair documents Spinoza's method's range: positive cumulative deduction and reductio elimination.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Leibniz Monadology 001 + Discourse 001 (substance metaphysics opposition)**: Spinoza's monism (one substance, namely God) is directly opposed by Leibniz's pluralism (infinitely many monads, each a simple substance with a complete concept). **Fifth corpus conceptual opposition pair** — Spinoza Ethics XIV (one-substance monism) vs Leibniz Monadology + Discourse (many-monad pluralism). Joins existing oppositions: Aristotle-PNC vs Hegel (Met 001 vs Hegel 001); Leibniz vs Hegel (Monadology 001 vs Hegel 001); Aristotle-Politics vs Hobbes (Politics 001 vs Hobbes 001); Plato-Statesman vs Aristotle-Politics (Statesman 001 vs Politics 001).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Categories 001 (primary substance)**: Aristotle distinguishes many primary substances (individuals like Socrates, this horse); Spinoza collapses primary substance to ONE (God/Nature). The Aristotelian-Spinozistic substance ontology contrast is one of the canonical philosophical contrasts. Spinoza's argument essentially rules out Aristotelian primary substances *as* substances; Aristotle's many primary substances are, in Spinoza's vocabulary, *modes* of the one substance.

**The pantheism / 'Deus sive Natura' identification**: Prop. XIV by itself establishes only that God is the only substance; the identification of God with Nature comes from the broader development (Part I Prop. XV: 'Whatsoever is, is in God'; Part I Prop. XXIX scholium: 'Natura naturans' and 'Natura naturata'). The 'God or Nature' formula appears explicitly in Part IV Preface. Prop. XIV is the deductive linchpin — without it, the pantheism doesn't follow.

**Reception**: Spinoza's Prop. XIV is the foundational text of modern monism / pantheism / Spinozism. The Pantheismusstreit (1785-1789, between Jacobi and Mendelssohn) made Spinozism a German-philosophical touchstone. Hegel's substance-monism (Phenomenology 001 — 'truth is the whole') is partially Spinozistic in inheritance. 20th-century revival via Deleuze's *Expressionism in Philosophy: Spinoza* (1968). Contemporary Spinozism (Macherey, Hardt-Negri) traces directly to this proposition.

**Translation choices**: Elwes 1883 'no substance can be granted or conceived' for Latin 'nulla substantia praeter Deum dari neque concipi potest' — 'granted' = dari (be given / posited); 'conceived' = concipi. Modern translations (Curley, Shirley) preserve the dual modality: substance besides God cannot be *posited as existing* nor *conceived as possible*.

attributions.sourceText: PROP. XIV.  Besides God no substance can be granted or conceived.

sourceExcerpt:
Nothing in the universe is contingent, but all
things are conditioned to exist and operate in a particular
manner by the necessity of the divine nature.
intent: Capture Spinoza's foundational necessitarianism claim from Ethics Part I, PROP. XXIX (lines 1034-1036): nothing in the universe is contingent; all things are conditioned to exist and operate by the necessity of the divine nature. This is the **canonical statement of Spinozistic necessitarianism** — the doctrine that everything that exists is *metaphysically necessary*, with no genuine contingency in the universe. The necessitarianism claim follows from Spinoza's prior propositions about substance (Prop. XIV — only one substance, namely God) and modes (Prop. XXVIII — everything is conditioned by something else, which is conditioned, ad infinitum, all back to God). **Third Spinoza extraction**; **Spinoza now at 3 extractions across 1 work** — joining Descartes (3 ext / 1 work) and Hegel (3 ext / 1 work) as a within-work three-extraction author. The Spinoza coverage now substantively spans Part I's three foundational doctrines: causation-requires-commonality (Prop. III) + substance-monism (Prop. XIV) + necessitarianism (Prop. XXIX — this).
extractorNote: **Third Spinoza extraction** — Spinoza now at **3 extractions across 1 work** (all in Ethics Part I): Spinoza 001 (Prop. III no-cross-substance-causation), Spinoza 002 (Prop. XIV substance-monism), Spinoza 003 (Prop. XXIX necessitarianism — this). Spinoza joins **Descartes (3 ext / 1 work), Hegel (3 ext / 1 work), Nietzsche (3 ext / 1 work)** as within-work three-extraction authors. **Seventh corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**: joins Aristotle De Int 001-004; Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003; Descartes Meditations 001-003; Aristotle De Anima 001-003; Hegel Phenomenology 001-003; Aristotle Metaphysics 001-003; Spinoza Ethics 001-003 (this). The Spinoza Ethics coverage is **three-from-Part-I** (similar to Aristotle Metaphysics 001-003 three-from-Book-IV); captures the *core deductive chain* of Spinoza's substance-and-modes metaphysics. **Two new corpus conceptual opposition pairs**: (39) Spinoza Ethics 003 vs Hume Treatise 002 / Enquiry 001 (necessitarianism vs matter-of-fact contingency); (40) Spinoza Ethics 003 vs Aristotle De Int 002/003/004 (necessitarianism vs sea-battle paradigm of contingency). **Forty conceptual oppositions now in corpus**. **Nineteenth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle Posterior Analytics 001 + Spinoza Ethics 003 on necessity-as-fundamental. **Nineteen alliances now in corpus**. **Rationalist substance-metaphysics strand now at 9 extractions across 3 authors**: Descartes Meditations × 3 + Spinoza Ethics × 3 + Leibniz Monadology/Discourse × 3. The corpus's deepest single-tradition multi-author cluster — rationalist metaphysics now substantively three-author-three-extractions-each documented. Forty-third fol extraction; fol now at 43 of 69 = 62%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the third-Spinoza + seventh-three-or-more-extraction-coverage + Spinoza-three-extraction-substantive-coherence + two-new-oppositions + nineteenth-alliance + rationalist-strand-at-9-extractions-3-authors-3-each milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: PROP. XXIX has an explicit geometric-deductive proof chaining Props. xv, xi, xvi, xxiv (Coroll.), xxvi, xxvii. A nd extraction could carry the multi-citation chain that derives the conclusion. The fol extraction captures the *conclusion-claim* (universal necessitarianism); nd would carry the Spinozistic *More Geometrico* derivational chain. Spinoza 001 and 002 were nd extractions of propositions III and XIV; for diversity and to match the corpus's existing Spinoza nd-pattern Spinoza 003 could plausibly be nd. fol chosen here because the conjoined-universals form is the natural encoding of *necessitarianism as a metaphysical thesis*.

The conjoined universals have categorical-A shape ('No things are contingent; all things are divine-necessity-conditioned'). Both predicates are unary. Term logic could carry it. fol preferred for the explicit conjoined-universal structure.
Not dialogical. Geometric-deductive treatise.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Ethics* (1677, published posthumously), Part I, PROP. XXIX. Elwes 1883 translation. PG #3800.

**Predicate naming**: `Contingent(x)` for the modal-status property (x could-have-been-otherwise / x's existence is not necessary); `ConditionedByNecessityOfDivineNature(x)` captures Spinoza's *necessitas divinae naturae* — the necessity flowing from God's (= the one substance's) nature.

**The conjoined-universals encoding**: captures Spinoza's *two* claims — (1) the *negative* claim that nothing is contingent; (2) the *positive* claim that everything is divinely-necessity-conditioned. Both directions hold; the conjunction is necessary to capture both. The biconditional ¬Contingent ↔ ConditionedByNecessity would also work but obscures the two-claim structure that Spinoza explicitly asserts.

**The geometric-deductive proof structure** (lines 1038-1055): Spinoza's proof chains Props. xv (Whatsoever is, is in God), xi (God exists necessarily), xvi (modes follow necessarily from divine nature), xxiv Coroll. (God is cause of modes' existence), xxvi (a thing acts in a way conditioned by God), xxvii (a thing cannot un-condition itself). The chained-citation derivation is canonical Spinozistic *More Geometrico*. Spinoza 001 (Prop. III) and Spinoza 002 (Prop. XIV) both used chained-citation derivations in their nd encodings; this extraction uses fol for the *conclusion* but the *derivation* could be a future nd extraction.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage)**: Spinoza 001 (Prop. III, line 121 — no causation without commonality), Spinoza 002 (Prop. XIV, line 464 — only one substance, namely God), Spinoza 003 (Prop. XXIX, line 1034 — necessitarianism — this). **Seventh corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage** (joining Aristotle De Int 001-004; Nietzsche Genealogy 001-003; Descartes Meditations 001-003; Aristotle De Anima 001-003; Hegel Phenomenology 001-003; Aristotle Metaphysics 001-003; Spinoza Ethics 001-003 — this). Spinoza Ethics coverage is **three-from-Part-I** — analogous to Aristotle Metaphysics 001-003 (three-from-Book-IV) and structurally distinct from across-multiple-parts/books coverage. The Spinoza Part I three-extraction coverage captures the *core* of Spinoza's substance-and-modes metaphysics.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Spinoza 001 + 002 (Spinoza substance-metaphysics strand now substantively coherent)**: Spinoza 001 (causation requires commonality) → Spinoza 002 (only one substance: God) → Spinoza 003 (necessitarianism: nothing contingent, all by divine necessity). The three Spinoza extractions trace the *deductive chain* of Part I's metaphysics: from no-cross-substance-causation, to one-substance-only, to all-necessary. Spinoza joins **Leibniz (3 ext / 2 works) at three-extraction substantive-coherence multi-aspect coverage** for rationalist substance metaphysics.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Leibniz Discourse 002 (substantive opposition deepened — pre-established harmony vs absolute necessity)**: Leibniz Discourse 002 (pre-established harmony, no direct inter-substance causation) and Spinoza Ethics 003 (necessitarianism — nothing contingent) are *similar in spirit* (both restrict freedom and contingency) but *opposed in metaphysical commitment*: Spinoza denies contingency entirely; Leibniz preserves contingency-via-infinite-analysis (Discourse §XIII flagged in iter 41 reviewer notes). The Spinoza-Leibniz opposition on the contingency-vs-necessity question is now substantively documented at this third Spinoza extraction.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Treatise 002 (substantive opposition — Spinozistic necessitarianism vs Humean contingency-of-matters-of-fact)**: Hume's Treatise 002 develops the inductive argument that grounds *matters of fact* in observed constant conjunction — preserving the contingency of matters of fact (their negation is conceivable). Spinoza's necessitarianism asserts that *nothing* is contingent; even apparently contingent matters are metaphysically necessary. **Thirty-ninth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Spinoza Ethics 003 (necessitarianism) vs Hume Treatise 002 (matter-of-fact contingency) / Hume Enquiry 001 (Hume's Fork dividing necessary-Relations-of-Ideas from contingent-Matters-of-Fact). The Spinoza-vs-Hume contrast on the contingency of empirical reality is canonical.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Posterior Analytics 001 (substantive alliance — both necessity-as-mark-of-truth)**: Aristotle's *epistēmē* requires cannot-be-otherwise (necessity); Spinoza's necessitarianism asserts everything-is-necessary. Both treat necessity as fundamental to reality (Aristotle: necessity is the mark of *scientific knowledge*; Spinoza: necessity is the mark of *being itself*). **Nineteenth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle Posterior Analytics 001 + Spinoza Ethics 003 on necessity-as-fundamental.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Kant Prolegomena 002 (substantive partial-alliance — both deny pure-contingency)**: Kant's synthetic a priori cognition has necessary character (a-priori knowledge is necessary); Spinoza's necessitarianism is more radical (necessity extends to everything, not just a-priori cognition). Both philosophers admit *necessity* as a substantive metaphysical-and-epistemic category; Spinoza is more thorough in his necessitarianism. Substantive partial-alliance on necessity-as-substantive-category.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle De Interpretatione 002 / 003 / 004 (substantive opposition — sea-battle as paradigm of contingency vs Spinoza necessitarianism)**: Aristotle's sea-battle problem (De Int 9, extracted as 002/003/004 in three formalisms) treats future contingents as genuinely contingent (between necessity and impossibility). Spinoza's necessitarianism denies contingency entirely — there are no future contingents. **Fortieth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Spinoza Ethics 003 (necessitarianism, nothing contingent) vs Aristotle De Int 002/003/004 (sea-battle as paradigm of contingency in future-tensed propositions). The Aristotle-vs-Spinoza contrast on contingency-vs-necessity is doubly grounded by the De Int trio of sea-battle extractions on one side and Spinoza 003 on the other.

**Reception**: Spinoza's necessitarianism is the foundational text of *fatalist* / *determinist* philosophical traditions. Einstein famously professed Spinozist necessitarianism. Modern compatibilist analyses of free will (Frankfurt, Dennett) engage with the Spinozistic challenge that genuine freedom requires contingency that may not actually obtain. Quantum-mechanical indeterminism (post-1925) is taken by some to refute Spinozistic necessitarianism on physical grounds, though the philosophical debate continues.

Nothing in the universe is contingent, but all
things are conditioned to exist and operate in a particular
manner by the necessity of the divine nature.

sourceExcerpt:
11

The world is everything that is the case.
intent: Capture the opening proposition of the Tractatus — 'The world is everything that is the case' — as a single quantified biconditional between being-in-the-world and being-the-case. The most-cited single line in 20th-century philosophy; the foundational ontological claim from which all the elaborations of propositions 1.x derive. The corpus's first 20th-century extraction and first analytic-tradition extraction.
extractorNote: Corpus's first **20th-century** extraction (Wittgenstein 1921, published 1922) and first **analytic-tradition** extraction. Adds the analytic sub-strand alongside the rationalist (Spinoza Ethics) and social-contract (Hobbes Leviathan) sub-strands already exercised. The Tractatus's aphoristic numbered-proposition structure is highly extraction-friendly per individual proposition — each is bounded, often a single quantified claim. The cross-cutting interpretive challenge is that each proposition is *commentary-structured*: 1.1 elaborates 1, 1.11 elaborates 1.1, etc. A single-proposition extraction (this one) implicitly defers context to the proposition tree, which is the right move for a first Tractatus extraction but means readers should understand prop 1 in the context of 1.1-1.21 (and beyond). The `frege` secondary at 0.5 is notable — Wittgenstein and Frege are in the same Begriffsschrift-influenced tradition, and the frege mirror could be the better long-term home for Tractatus extractions if the judgment/content distinction becomes load-bearing in later propositions. Not yet — proposition 1 is bare-assertional and fol carries it cleanly. The `11` source artifact at line 71 is preserved verbatim in the excerpt per the existing convention (Phase B Aristotle artifacts, Spinoza Q.E.D., etc.); the artifact is acknowledged in reviewer notes and in the manifest.

assessments.reason: The Tractatus is explicitly in the Frege/Russell logical-atomist tradition, and proposition 1 is paradigmatically the kind of foundational claim Begriffsschrift was designed to formalize. The frege mirror could carry it as either a `judgment` (assertion) or a `content` (mere content) wrapping the same biconditional. Not chosen as primary because (a) the biconditional structure is what carries the philosophical content here, and fol handles biconditionals natively; (b) the judgment/content distinction would be a Fregean overlay on Wittgenstein's bare proposition-statement, which the source does not particularly highlight.
assessments.distortionRisk: Using frege would impose a Begriffsschrift-typographical packaging that is anachronistic for the 1921 Tractatus — Frege's Begriffsschrift (1879) precedes it but Wittgenstein does not adopt the judgment/content stroke notation.

assessments.reason: Not an inference. nd would have to either treat proposition 1 as an empty-premise conclusion (degenerate) or fold in propositions 1.1, 1.11, etc. as premises, but those are *elaborations* of 1, not premises *for* 1 in any standard inferential reading.
assessments.distortionRisk: An nd extraction would mis-frame the commentary structure of propositions 1.x as a premises-to-conclusion inference, which it is not.

assessments.reason: The biconditional ∀x. IsInWorld(x) ↔ IsTheCase(x) is convertibility-shaped (all A are B AND all B are A), which categorical term logic can carry as a convertible A-pair. But the load-bearing structure is the *identity* between 'the world' and 'the totality of cases', which is a first-order relation between two abstract entities — not a categorical claim about how a predicate distributes over a kind. The aristotelian framing flattens the totality-identity into predicate-extension.
Not dialogical. A single declarative proposition with no exchange, no speakers, no concessions.

reviewerNotes: **Source artifact**: the '11' on line 71 of source.txt is the proposition number '1' concatenated with the footnote reference '[1]', a known artifact of the Standard Ebooks XHTML-to-text conversion. Documented in the manifest (`sources/wittgenstein/tractatus/manifest.yaml`) and in `log.md` 2026-05-26. The actual proposition number is **1**. The verbatim excerpt preserves '11' because the validator's verbatim-excerpt check operates on source.txt; the semantic interpretation routes around the artifact.

Translator: C. K. Ogden (1922), with Wittgenstein's collaboration. The Ogden translation is the public-domain English version of the Tractatus; the later Pears/McGuinness translation (1961) is rights-locked. Standard Ebooks edition, source `standardebooks/ludwig-wittgenstein_tractatus-logico-philosophicus_c-k-ogden` on GitHub.

German original: 'Die Welt ist alles, was der Fall ist.' (Tractatus, 1921). Ogden's 'everything that is the case' is the canonical English rendering for 'alles, was der Fall ist'. The Pears/McGuinness translation (rights-locked) gives 'The world is all that is the case' — slightly different rhythm, same logical structure.

**Tractatus numbered proposition structure**: 1, 1.1, 1.11, 1.12, 1.13, 1.2, 1.21, 2, 2.01, 2.011, ... — the decimal numbering is Wittgenstein's commentary tree, with each sub-proposition elaborating its parent. Proposition 1 is the canonical opening; propositions 1.1-1.21 elaborate the world-as-totality-of-facts ontology that 1 introduces. This extraction picks only proposition 1; future extractions could pick 1.1 separately, or bundle 1 + 1.1 + 1.11-1.13 as a multi-proposition cluster encoded as conjunction. The commentary-structure dependency is intrinsic to the Tractatus and is a different shape from the cross-span citation pattern flagged in `log.md` 2026-05-28 iteration 3 — there, the dependency is on definitions/axioms outside the source span; here, the dependency is on *elaborations* of the same proposition, which sit just *after* it in the source.

**Predicate choice**: `IsInWorld(x)` encodes 'x is part of the world' / 'x belongs to the world'. `IsTheCase(x)` encodes 'x is the case' / 'x is a fact' / 'x obtains'. The biconditional ∀x. IsInWorld(x) ↔ IsTheCase(x) is the natural fol idiom for 'world = totality of cases'. fol does not have set membership; the predicate `IsInWorld` does the work of 'x is part of the world'.

**Variable interpretation**: the universal ∀x ranges over candidate facts / cases / states-of-affairs. The schema does not type-annotate variables; a multi-sorted fol extension would type x as a fact/case. Wittgenstein's *Sachverhalt* (state of affairs / atomic fact) is the relevant sortal; the schema underdetermines this.

**The proposition's force is definitional**, not empirical — Wittgenstein is *stipulating* what he means by 'world' in the Tractatus, not making a discovery-claim about it. The biconditional encoding preserves the definitional character; the German 'ist' here is the *defining* copula, not the descriptive one.

Cross-proposition-tree dependency. Propositions 1.1-1.21 elaborate this one and are inside the same numbered tree. The seventh occurrence of cross-span citation in the corpus, but distinct in character — within-tree elaboration rather than outside-span definition reference. Not re-flagging; the human signal for cross-span citation is already in log.md from iteration 3.

7

Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.
intent: Capture the Tractatus's closing Proposition 7 (line 3075): 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.' This is the iconic closing aphorism of the Tractatus — the regulative norm that follows from the entire 89-page architectonic about the limits of meaningful language. **Second Wittgenstein extraction**; **sixth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern** with Wittgenstein 001 (Proposition 1 'The world is everything that is the case'). The two extractions bracket the Tractatus's structural arch: Prop 1 opens the work with the world-as-totality-of-facts ontology; Prop 7 closes it with the silence-obligation about what falls outside meaningful speakability.
extractorNote: **Second Wittgenstein extraction** — Wittgenstein now at two extractions, both fol-encoded, bracketing the Tractatus's structural arch (Prop 1 ontological opening + Prop 7 normative closing). **Sixth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern**: Met 001/002 (PNC + LEM); Hume 001/002 (Copy Principle + argument); De Anima 001/002 (separability + first-actuality); Locke 001/002 (empiricist foundation + primary-quality realism); Hegel 001/002 (truth-is-whole + mutual recognition); Wittgenstein 001/002 (world-as-totality + silence-norm — this). Pattern now at six instances — most-recurrent corpus structural feature. **Thirteenth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Hegel Phenomenology (holistic metaphysics + dialectical recognition theory) vs Wittgenstein Tractatus 002 (silence-norm forbidding such metaphysics-construction). The Wittgenstein-Hegel opposition is canonical 20th-century-analytic-vs-19th-century-continental contrast. **Analytic-tradition strand now at three extractions across two authors**: Wittgenstein 001/002 + Russell 001. **Iteration 50 corpus milestone**: this is the 50th iteration of the autonomous loop. Cumulative status: 49 successful extractions + 1 STUCK (iter 39 Kant CPR encoding). Twenty-seventh fol extraction; fol now at 27 of 50 = 54%. **Cross-extraction alliance with Aristotle Met 001 (PNC)**: both are limit-principles (universal-conditional claims about what is excluded from the meaningful domain). Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the sixth-same-work-pair + thirteenth-opposition + iteration-50-milestone + analytic-strand-three-extractions + cross-extraction-alliance-with-PNC milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The wider Tractatus develops the speakable/showable distinction across §§4.1-6.522 leading to Proposition 7 as conclusion. A nd extraction of the Tractatus would carry that staged development. The single Proposition 7 is the principle itself, fol-extractable as the universal-conditional regulative norm; the nd extraction would be the supporting Tractarian architectonic.

The principle has A-form shape ('All unspeakable subjects are subjects-about-which-silence-is-mandatory'). Both predicates are unary; term logic could carry it. fol is preferred because the conditional connective ¬Speakable → Silence preserves the *implication* structure that the principle is *about* (limits of speakability entail obligations of silence), where term logic just makes both into bare categorical predicates.
Not dialogical. Aphoristic treatise prose; no exchange.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus* (1922), Proposition 7. C. K. Ogden translation (1922, with Wittgenstein's collaboration), Standard Ebooks XHTML-to-text conversion. Lines 3073-3075 in cleaned source.txt.

**Predicate naming**: `MeaningfullySpeakable(x)` for what the Tractatus's framework permits as content of meaningful propositions (states of affairs / Sachverhalte that can be pictured by propositions). `SilenceMandatory(x)` for the deontic-modal obligation of remaining silent. The German original *Wovon man nicht sprechen kann, darüber muss man schweigen* makes the negative conditional and the deontic obligation explicit; the encoded ¬Speakable → SilenceMandatory captures both.

**The speakable/showable distinction** is the Tractatus's signature architectonic move. Propositions §4.1-§4.12 establish that propositions picture states of affairs; §6.41-§6.522 establish that ethics, aesthetics, the mystical, and the logical-form of propositions themselves are *unspeakable* but *showable* (they can be displayed but not stated). Proposition 7 is the *regulative norm* derived from this architectonic: about the unspeakable, silence is mandatory. The encoded principle does not capture the broader bipartition; it captures the closing deontic conclusion.

**The performative paradox**: Proposition 6.54 (the proposition immediately preceding §7) states that the Tractatus's own propositions are 'elucidatory' — 'he who understands me finally recognizes them as senseless... he must throw away the ladder, after he has climbed up on it.' The Tractatus's own propositions about the speakable/showable distinction are themselves attempting to speak about what (by their own principles) cannot be spoken. Proposition 7 enforces silence about precisely the content the Tractatus has been attempting to convey. This is the famous *Tractarian self-overcoming* / *ladder-paradox*. The encoded principle captures the norm; the paradox is a meta-level commentary on the principle's application to itself.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Wittgenstein 001 (within-work same-author pair)**: Wittgenstein 001 (Prop 1, line 71) extracted 'The world is everything that is the case' — the *ontological* opening claim. Wittgenstein 002 (this, Prop 7, line 3075) extracts 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent' — the *normative-regulative* closing claim. The two extractions bracket the Tractatus's structural arch: Prop 1 establishes the ontology of facts-as-world; Prop 7 establishes the limit of meaningful discourse about what is and is not within that ontology. The pair captures the Tractatus's *two foundational moves* — ontology-of-facts + norm-of-silence. **Sixth corpus instance of same-work different-span pair pattern** (joining Met 001/002, Hume 001/002, De Anima 001/002, Locke 001/002, Hegel 001/002).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle Metaphysics 001 (PNC) — alignment via universal claim**: PNC is a universal-conditional claim about what cannot be the case (it is impossible that a thing both is and is not in the same respect). Proposition 7 is a universal-conditional claim about what one cannot speak (those subjects require silence). Both are *limit-principles* — universal conditionals about what is excluded from the meaningful domain. Different domains (Aristotle on metaphysical possibility; Wittgenstein on linguistic-semantic possibility) but structurally analogous *limit-principle* moves.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Russell Problems 001 (analytic-tradition alliance)**: Russell's sense-data definition (Problems 001) and Wittgenstein's silence-norm (this) are both analytic-tradition foundational moves: Russell establishes the immediate-knowledge basis of empirical claims; Wittgenstein establishes the limits of meaningful discourse. Together they form the analytic-tradition strand at three extractions across two authors (Wittgenstein 001 ontology + 002 silence-norm; Russell 001 sense-data).

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hegel Phenomenology 001/002 (substantive opposition)**: Hegel's holistic-truth principle (Phen 001) and intersubjective-mutual-recognition (Phen 002) are *examples* of exactly the kind of holistic-systematic metaphysical claims the Tractatus rejects as senseless attempts to speak the unspeakable. Hegel's entire systematic-philosophical project is, from the Tractarian view, precisely what Proposition 7 forbids. **Thirteenth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Hegel Phenomenology (holistic systematic metaphysics + dialectical recognition theory) vs Wittgenstein Tractatus 002 (silence-norm forbidding such metaphysics-construction). The Wittgenstein-Hegel opposition is one of the canonical 20th-century-analytic-vs-19th-century-continental philosophical contrasts.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Marcus Aurelius 001/002 (Stoic alliance/parallel)**: Marcus's Stoic indifference about externals shares a *limit-principle* structure with Wittgenstein's silence-norm — both prescribe a *withdrawal* from certain domains (Stoic withdrawal from passion-attachment to externals; Wittgensteinian withdrawal from speech about the unspeakable). Different domains, similar structural move.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Charmides 001 (aporia / silence-of-Socrates)**: Plato's Charmides ends in *aporia* — Socrates and Critias unable to determine what wisdom is. The Charmides-style aporia could be read as a kind of philosophical-silence about an unresolved question. Substantive structural link: Wittgensteinian silence ≠ Socratic aporia (the former is principled-deontic; the latter is investigative-confessional) but both are confrontations with limits of philosophical articulation.

**Reception**: Proposition 7 is one of the most-cited single sentences in 20th-century philosophy. The Vienna Circle (Carnap, Schlick, Neurath) embraced the silence-norm as anti-metaphysical positivism. The later Wittgenstein (Philosophical Investigations) reframes the speakable/unspeakable bipartition as ordinary-language-games and forms-of-life — the Tractatus's clean silence-norm becomes a much more pluralist therapeutic-philosophy. Frank Ramsey's response: 'What we cannot say, we cannot say, and we cannot whistle it either' — questioning whether Wittgenstein's distinction can be maintained.

**Translation choices**: Ogden 1922 'whereof / thereof' for *wovon / darüber* is the classic translation, slightly archaic. Pears-McGuinness 1961 uses 'What we cannot speak about we must pass over in silence' — more direct. Both preserve the universal-conditional + deontic-modal structure.

sourceExcerpt:
2.12

The picture is a model of reality.
intent: Capture Wittgenstein's foundational picture-theory proposition from Tractatus 2.12 (line 285): the picture is a model of reality. This is the **canonical picture-theory of meaning** — Wittgenstein's central claim about the relation between language/propositions and reality. The Tractatus's picture-theory holds that meaningful propositions *picture* atomic facts; the proposition's elements *correspond* to objects in reality; the proposition is true iff the pictured fact obtains. Proposition 2.12 is the most-distilled formulation of this framework. **Third Wittgenstein extraction**; **Wittgenstein now at 3 extractions / 1 work** — **ninth author at within-work three-extraction depth** (joining Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama). The Wittgenstein coverage now substantively spans the *three foundational Tractarian moves*: world-as-facts (Tractatus 001, Prop 1) + picture-theory (Tractatus 003, Prop 2.12 — this) + silence-norm (Tractatus 002, Prop 7).
extractorNote: **Third Wittgenstein extraction** — Wittgenstein now at **3 extractions / 1 work**: Tractatus 001 (Prop 1 world-as-facts) + Tractatus 002 (Prop 7 silence-norm) + Tractatus 003 (Prop 2.12 picture-theory — this). **Wittgenstein joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama, Wittgenstein (this). **Nine authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. **Five consecutive iterations have added new within-work three-extraction authors** (iters 70-74). **Twelfth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**: Wittgenstein Tractatus 001-003 substantively coherent — covers the Tractatus's three foundational moves (ontology + semantics + regulative norm). **Two new corpus conceptual opposition pairs**: (44) Hume Enquiry 001 vs Wittgenstein Tractatus 003 (empiricist epistemic partition vs picture-theory); (45) Berkeley Three Dialogues 003 vs Wittgenstein Tractatus 003 (Berkeleyan idealism vs Tractarian mind-independent-fact picture). **Forty-five conceptual oppositions now in corpus**. **Twenty-fourth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle De Int 001 + Wittgenstein Tractatus 003 on linguistic-content-via-correspondence-to-reality. **Twenty-four corpus alliances now documented**. **Analytic-tradition strand now at 5 ext / 2 authors** with division-of-labor coverage (Russell epistemology × 2 + Wittgenstein ontology+semantics+meta × 3). Forty-eighth fol extraction; fol now at 48 of 74 = 65%. Routine extraction by AUTONOMOUS_PROMPT.md flagging criteria; the third-Wittgenstein + Wittgenstein-at-3-extractions-1-work + ninth-within-work-three-extraction-author + twelfth-three-or-more-extraction-coverage + Wittgenstein-Tractatus-structural-arch-coverage + two-new-oppositions + twenty-fourth-alliance + analytic-tradition-strand-at-5-ext-2-authors + five-consecutive-iterations-new-within-work-three-extraction-author milestones go in SESSION_LOG.

assessments.reason: The biconditional has clean convertible-A shape ('All pictures are models of reality; all models of reality are pictures'). Both predicates are unary. Term logic could carry it directly. fol preferred only marginally — biconditional structure preserved more cleanly.

Proposition 2.12 is stated as a *definitional/conceptual claim* rather than as inferred from premises. The surrounding propositions (2.1 'We make to ourselves pictures of facts'; 2.11 'The picture presents the facts in logical space'; 2.13 'To the objects correspond in the picture the elements of the picture') build a *conceptual framework* rather than a sequential argument. nd would impose inferential structure not present in the source.
Not dialogical. Tractatus aphoristic-treatise prose.

reviewerNotes: Source: *Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus* (1922), Proposition 2.12. C. K. Ogden translation (1922, with Wittgenstein's collaboration). Standard Ebooks XHTML-to-text conversion, lines 283-285 in source.txt.

**Predicate naming**: `Picture(p)` for the Tractarian technical term *Bild* (picture — propositional/linguistic representation of reality); `ModelOfReality(p)` for the canonical claim about pictures (each picture *is* a model of reality, in the sense that the picture's structure corresponds to the structure of what it represents).

**The biconditional encoding**: captures the *definitional* identification of picture with model-of-reality. Both directions hold textually: the Tractarian framework treats every meaningful linguistic representation as a picture, and every picture functions as a model of reality. The biconditional is stricter than the surface text ('The picture is a model of reality'), but the Tractarian framework supports the biconditional reading — Wittgenstein's picture-theory identifies pictures with reality-models.

**The picture-theory framework's broader structure**: Propositions 2.1-2.225 develop the picture-theory: pictures are facts (2.141), pictures consist of elements corresponding to objects (2.13), pictures have a *form* shared with reality (2.151-2.17 — the *form of representation*), pictures can be *true* or *false* depending on whether the pictured fact obtains (2.21-2.22). Proposition 2.12 is the foundational *identification* of picture with reality-model; the subsequent propositions develop the consequences.

**The 2.12 prop-number formatting**: source.txt line 283 shows '2.12' clean (no footnote concatenation issue, unlike Tractatus 001 which had '11' for Prop 1 due to footnote-reference concatenation). The Standard Ebooks XHTML-to-text conversion artifact only affected certain propositions; 2.12 is artifact-free.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Wittgenstein 001 + 002 (within-work three-extraction coverage)**: Wittgenstein 001 (Prop 1, line 71 — *world is everything that is the case*, fol world-as-totality-of-facts) + Wittgenstein 002 (Prop 7, line 3075 — *whereof one cannot speak*, fol silence-norm) + Wittgenstein 003 (Prop 2.12, line 285 — *picture is a model of reality*, fol picture-theory — this). Three Wittgenstein extractions covering the *three foundational Tractarian moves*: ontological opening (Prop 1: world-as-facts) + semantic-representational middle (Prop 2.12: picture-theory) + regulative closing (Prop 7: silence-norm). The three together exhaust the Tractatus's foundational architectural structure: what-is-the-case + how-language-represents-it + what-falls-outside-meaningful-language. **Twelfth corpus three-or-more-extraction same-work coverage**: joins prior eleven coverages.

**Wittgenstein joins within-work three-extraction author tier**: Descartes, Spinoza, Hegel, Nietzsche, Hobbes, Marcus Aurelius, Berkeley, Gotama, Wittgenstein (this). **Nine authors at within-work three-extraction depth**. The within-work-three-extraction-depth structural feature has expanded at one new author per iteration for **five consecutive iterations** (Hobbes iter 70 → Marcus iter 71 → Berkeley iter 72 → Gotama iter 73 → Wittgenstein iter 74).

**Wittgenstein three-extraction substantive coherence**: the three Wittgenstein extractions span the Tractatus's structural arch — Prop 1 (ontological opening) → Prop 2.12 (semantic-representational middle) → Prop 7 (regulative closing). The three propositions together capture the Tractatus's foundational philosophical architecture: world (1) + picture/language (2.12) + silence (7). **Substantive structural-architectural coverage**.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Russell Problems 001 + 002 (analytic-tradition strand deepening to 5 ext / 2 authors)**: Russell × 2 (sense-data + acquaintance) + Wittgenstein × 3 (world + silence + picture-theory) = **5 extractions across 2 authors** in the analytic-tradition strand. Russell handles epistemology (sense-data + acquaintance); Wittgenstein handles ontology + semantics + meta-philosophy (world + picture + silence). The analytic-tradition strand now has substantive division-of-labor coverage across two authors at 2 + 3 extractions.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Hume Enquiry 001 (substantive opposition — Hume's empiricist epistemology vs Tractarian semantic-representational framework)**: Hume's Fork divides knowledge into Relations of Ideas + Matters of Fact (perception-based); Tractatus 003's picture-theory is a *semantic-representational* framework where propositions picture facts. Both restrict legitimate epistemology/semantics to specific structures. **Forty-fourth corpus conceptual opposition pair**: Hume Enquiry 001 (empiricist epistemic partition) vs Wittgenstein Tractatus 003 (semantic-representational picture-theory) — distinct frameworks for grounding meaningful claims about reality.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Plato Theaetetus 001 (substantive opposition — Theaetetan refutation of knowledge=perception vs Tractarian picture-theory of language-reality correspondence)**: Plato Theaetetus rejects knowledge=perception via Socratic refutation; Tractatus 003 establishes picture-as-model-of-reality as the semantic-representational basis. Both philosophers reject naive epistemic frameworks, but with different alternatives: Plato → universal-categories-required-beyond-perception; Wittgenstein → picture-theory-of-meaning. Substantive cross-extraction conceptual link.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Aristotle De Interpretatione 001 (substantive alliance — both ground meaningful language in correspondence-to-objects)**: Aristotle De Int 001 (universal-vs-individual; definitions like *PredicableOfManySubjects*) and Tractatus 003 (picture-theory) both ground linguistic-semantic content in *correspondence to reality* (Aristotle: terms predicate of subjects; Wittgenstein: picture elements correspond to objects). **Twenty-fourth corpus cross-extraction alliance**: Aristotle De Int 001 + Wittgenstein Tractatus 003 on linguistic-content-via-correspondence-to-reality. The ancient-modern correspondence-theory-of-meaning alliance is substantive.

**Cross-extraction conceptual link to Berkeley Three Dialogues 003 (substantive opposition — Tractarian picture-theory of mind-independent fact vs Berkeleyan idealism)**: Tractatus 003 presupposes that pictures represent *facts about reality* (mind-independent reality being pictured). Berkeley Three Dialogues 003 identifies sensible bodies with perceived things (mind-dependent). The Wittgenstein-vs-Berkeley contrast on the mind-independent-fact-vs-mind-dependent-perception question is substantive. **Forty-fifth corpus conceptual opposition pair**.

**Reception**: Tractatus 2.12 picture-theory is the foundational text of mid-20th-century semantic-representational philosophy of language. The Vienna Circle (Carnap, Schlick) develops it. Later Wittgenstein (Philosophical Investigations) explicitly *abandons* the picture-theory in favor of language-games. Davidson's truth-conditional semantics (1967+) descends from a related framework. Brandom's *Making It Explicit* (1994) critiques picture-theory in favor of inferentialism.
